Snowflake is chosen only when storage is severely constrained or dimension hierarchies are deep and frequently updated. #### **Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks: Numerical Computation** **Given:** - Number of dimensions: $N_d = 4$ - Hierarchy levels: $h = (3, 3, 4, 2)$ for the 4 dimensions respectively. **Step 1 вҖ” Star Schema Calculations:** Total number of tables: $$ T_{\text{star}} = N_d + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ tables} $$ Number of joins for a full multi-dimensional query: $$ J_{\text{star}} = N_d = 4 \text{ joins} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Snowflake Schema Calculations:** Total number of tables: $$ T_{\text{snow}} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{4} h_i = 1 + (3 + 3 + 4 + 2) = 1 + 12 = 13 \text{ tables} $$ Number of joins for the same query (using derived formula $J_{\text{snow}} = \sum h_i$): $$ J_{\text{snow}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} h_i = 3 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 12 \text{ joins} $$ **Step 3 вҖ” Overhead Analysis:** Increase in table count: $$ \Delta T = T_{\text{snow}} - T_{\text{star}} = 13 - 5 = 8 \text{ additional tables} $$ Increase in join count (a 3Г— increase): $$ \Delta J = J_{\text{snow}} - J_{\text{star}} = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ additional joins} $$ Join ratio: $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{J_{\text{snow}}}{J_{\text{star}}} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.0 $$ **Step 4 вҖ” Interpretation:** - The snowflake schema requires **3 times more joins** for the same analytical query. - On a row-store RDBMS, each join can add 50вҖ“500 ms overhead; for 8 extra joins, this translates to 0.4вҖ“4 seconds of additional latency. - On a columnar store (Snowflake-the-warehouse, Redshift, BigQuery), this overhead is partially mitigated by predicate pushdown, but still measurable. - **Storage savings** in snowflaking for this scenario would typically be 20вҖ“30% of star-schema size. **Conclusion:** For this specific dimensional complexity, the **Star Schema is strongly preferred** because the 3Г— join cost outweighs the modest storage savings. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. **Skipping the Grain Declaration** (loses 2 marks): Always explicitly state the grain in step 2 of the four-step process. > 2. **Confusing Star vs Snowflake Joins** (loses 1вҖ“2 marks): A common mistake is to write $J_{\text{snow}} = N_d$ вҖ” that is the **star** formula. The snowflake formula is $J_{\text{snow}} = \sum h_i$. > 3. **Mixing Additive and Non-Additive Facts** (loses 1 mark): Never include a ratio or unit price as a sum-able fact. > 4. **Forgetting Surrogate Keys** (loses 1 mark): KTU expects you to mention that dimension PKs are **surrogate integer keys**, not natural business keys. > 5. **Not Drawing the Schema** (loses 2вҖ“3 marks): Always accompany design questions with a **diagram** (even a hand-drawn ASCII or text-based one). Pure textual descriptions without a diagram fetch partial credit only. > 6. **Incorrectly Naming Schema Types** (loses 1 mark): Do not call Snowflake a "normalized fact table" вҖ” it is the **dimensions** that are normalized, not the fact. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist вҖ” Read This the Night Before the Exam** - вң… **Dimensional Modeling (DM)** is a **read-optimized, denormalized** logical design technique for data warehouses (Kimball methodology). - вң… Every DM has exactly **two types of tables**: **Fact tables** (numeric measurements) and **Dimension tables** (descriptive context). - вң… **Fact Table** = Foreign Keys (composite PK) + Numeric Measures (additive/semi-additive/non-additive) + optional Degenerate Dimensions. - вң… **Dimension Table** = Surrogate Key (PK) + Natural Key + Descriptive Attributes + Hierarchical Attributes. - вң… **Grain = the atomic level of one fact row** вҖ” declared in Step 2 of the Kimball process, and it is **non-negotiable** thereafter. - вң… **Four-Step Process**: (1) Business Process вҶ’ (2) Grain вҶ’ (3) Dimensions вҶ’ (4) Facts. **Always in this order.** - вң… **Star Schema**: $T_{\text{star}} = N_d + 1$ tables, $J_{\text{star}} = N_d$ joins. **Denormalized dimensions.** - вң… **Snowflake Schema**: $T_{\text{snow}} = 1 + \sum h_i$ tables, $J_{\text{snow}} = \sum h_i$ joins. **Normalized dimensions.** - вң… **Star vs Snowflake Trade-off**: Star = faster queries + more storage; Snowflake = slower queries + less storage. - вң… **Conformed Dimensions** are the glue of an enterprise DW вҖ” built once, reused across many fact marts. - вң… **Role-Playing Dimensions** (e.g., Date used as Order Date / Ship Date / Delivery Date) appear multiple times in a fact table via alias FKs. - вң… **Junk Dimensions** consolidate low-cardinality flags; **Degenerate Dimensions** are operational IDs stored in the fact table itself. - вң… **Fact Table Types**: Transaction (one row per event), Periodic Snapshot (one row per period), Accumulating Snapshot (one row per lifecycle). - вң… **Default Recommendation**: **Star Schema** for ~80% of data marts (Kimball's empirical guideline). - вң… **Galaxy / Fact Constellation Schema** = multiple fact tables sharing conformed dimensions; used in enterprise warehouses. - вң… **Surrogate Keys** decouple DW from OLTP systems and enable SCD Type 2 (historical tracking). - вң… **Hierarchy** in dimensions supports drill-down (topвҶ’bottom) and roll-up (bottomвҶ’top) operations in OLAP. - вң… **Cardinality** of dimension key = number of unique values; high-cardinality dimensions (Customer, Product) are common. - вң… **Fact Row Count** upper bound: $\vert F \vert = \prod C_k$ (Cartesian product, but actual rows are usually sparser).

topics.title: Steps in Dimensional Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steps in Dimensional Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dimensional Modeling (DM)** is a logical design technique for data warehouses, popularized by **Ralph Kimball**, that structures data into two distinct types of tables вҖ” **Fact tables** (which hold measurable, quantitative business events) and **Dimension tables** (which hold descriptive, textual context about those events). The *Steps in Dimensional Modeling* refer to the **canonical four-step methodology** that a data warehouse architect follows to convert raw business requirements into an optimized star (or snowflake) schema.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT632, Module 2):**
> *Dimensional modeling is a design technique used to present data in a standardized, intuitive framework that allows for high-performance access. The four steps are: (1) Select the Business Process, (2) Declare the Grain, (3) Identify the Dimensions, and (4) Identify the Facts.*

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Steps (High-Level)

1. **Select the Business Process** вҖ“ Decide *what* real-world event to model (e.g., retail sales, hospital admissions, ATM withdrawals).
2. **Declare the Grain** вҖ“ Precisely define *what* a single row in the fact table represents (e.g., one line item per receipt).
3. **Identify the Dimensions** вҖ“ List the *who, what, when, where, why, and how* descriptors of the grain.
4. **Identify the Facts** вҖ“ List the *numeric measurements* that fit exactly that grain (e.g., quantity sold, revenue, profit).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Movie Reel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Movie Reel рҹҺ¬**
> - **Step 1 (Business Process)** вҶ’ The genre and subject of the movie. Are you filming a *Sales Story*, an *Inventory Story*, or a *Shipping Story*? You cannot film all three in one reel.
> - **Step 2 (Grain)** вҶ’ Each individual *frame* of the movie. You must commit to "one frame = one customer buying one product on one day at one store" вҖ” the most atomic description.
> - **Step 3 (Dimensions)** вҶ’ The *labels* and *metadata* around the frame: actor names (Customer), movie name (Product), shooting date (Date), and location (Store).
> - **Step 4 (Facts)** вҶ’ The *visible content* inside the frame itself вҖ” the numbers like ticket price, box office collection, and seat count.
>
> Just as you cannot enlarge a blurry frame later, **you cannot summarize a fact table to a finer grain after the warehouse is built**. The grain decision is irreversible.

## 1.4 Why Follow These Steps in Order?

Each step creates a *contract* that locks the design:

| Step | Locks | Cannot Be Undone Without |
|------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1. Business Process | Scope of the model | Re-engineering source pipelines |
| 2. Grain | Atomic granularity of every row | Rebuilding fact tables & ETL |
| 3. Dimensions | The set of "by" attributes for slicing | Restructuring BI reports |
| 4. Facts | The numeric measures | Historical data re-aggregation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students who skip directly from "Business Process" to "Facts" without declaring the grain almost always lose **3вҖ“5 marks** in Part B questions. The grain is the *spine* of every dimensional model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Step 1 вҖ” Select the Business Process

A **business process** is a natural operational activity performed by the organization that is **captured in an OLTP source system** and worth analyzing. Examples include:

- Retail: Point-of-Sale (POS) transactions
- Banking: ATM cash withdrawals, loan disbursements
- Telecom: Call Detail Records (CDR)
- Healthcare: Patient admission, lab test orders
- Education: Course enrollments, exam results

### Operational Logic
- The process must be **measurable** (it produces numeric data).
- It must be **supported by an operational source system** (real ETL feeds).
- It must be **useful to business users** (the organization actually needs to analyze it).

> [!TIP]
> If the business user cannot phrase a question beginning with *"How many / How much / What is the trend ofвҖҰ"*, the process is **not a valid dimensional modeling candidate**.

---

## 2.2 Step 2 вҖ” Declare the Grain

**Grain** is the most atomic level of detail captured by a single row in the fact table.

### Examples of Grain Declarations
| Bad (Too Vague) | Good (Atomic) |
|-----------------|---------------|
| Daily sales | One row per product per receipt line per day per store |
| Monthly transactions | One row per ATM withdrawal event |
| Customer activity | One row per individual phone call placed |

### The "Ask the Reporter" Test
- Imagine a business reporter asks for the data. **The grain is the answer to "What does one row in the report represent?"**
- If two reporters disagree, **the grain is finer than what one of them asked for**.

### Consequences of Mistaken Grain
- **Coarse grain** вҶ’ Cannot drill down to atomic details. Many "How" questions become unanswerable.
- **Mixed grain** вҶ’ Fact table becomes meaningless; subtotals will be wrong. The single most common modeling error.

---

## 2.3 Step 3 вҖ” Identify the Dimensions

Dimensions provide the **contextual "by" axes** of analysis. They answer:
- **Who?** вҶ’ Customer, Employee, Patient
- **What?** вҶ’ Product, Service, Course
- **When?** вҶ’ Date, Time, Shift
- **Where?** вҶ’ Store, Branch, City
- **Why?** вҶ’ Promotion, Campaign, Reason
- **How?** вҶ’ Channel, Payment Mode, Device

### Conformed Dimensions
A dimension shared across multiple fact tables (e.g., a `Dim_Date` used by Sales, Inventory, and Shipping) is called a **conformed dimension**. This is a *key pillar* of the Kimball methodology because it enables **drill-across** queries between data marts.

---

## 2.4 Step 4 вҖ” Identify the Facts

Facts are the **numeric measurements** that arise from the business process event at the declared grain.

### Types of Facts
| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Additive** | Can be summed across *all* dimensions | Revenue, Quantity Sold |
| **Semi-Additive** | Summed across some, but not all dimensions | Account Balance (sum across customers, NOT across dates) |
| **Non-Additive** | Cannot be summed (must be averaged or ratioed) | Profit Margin %, Unit Price |

### Factless Fact Tables
A fact table that contains **only foreign keys and no numeric measures** is used to record an *event happened* (e.g., a student attended a class on a given date). It is a perfectly valid dimensional construct.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Step | Key Output | Common Pitfall |
|---|------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Select Business Process | A clear, verb-noun statement of the event | Modeling "all of finance" as one process |
| 2 | Declare Grain | A sentence: "One row = вҖҰ" | Imprecise wording like "monthly sales" |
| 3 | Identify Dimensions | List of 4вҖ“15 dimension tables | Forgetting Slowly Changing Dimensions |
| 4 | Identify Facts | List of additive, semi, and non-additive metrics | Mixing transaction-level and snapshot facts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Bloom's Tag for this topic:** *Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)*. You are expected to apply the four steps to a case study and analyze the resulting star schema.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Decision Matrix

Because dimensional modeling is a *methodology* rather than a formula, this section formalizes the **decision logic** of each step using an analytical matrix.

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix for Each Step

### Step 1 вҖ” Business Process Selection Logic

$$
\text{Valid Process} = (\text{Operational Source Exists}) \;\land\; (\text{Produces Metrics}) \;\land\; (\text{Business Need Confirmed})
$$

| Input Variable | Decision | Output |
|----------------|----------|--------|
| Source OLTP system exists? | No вҶ’ Reject | Stop |
| Generates numeric measures? | No вҶ’ Reject | Stop |
| Business user needs analysis? | No вҶ’ Reject | Stop |
| All three = Yes | Accept | Proceed to Step 2 |

### Step 2 вҖ” Grain Declaration Logic

The grain must satisfy the **uniqueness constraint**:
$$
\forall \, \text{row}_i, \text{row}_j \in \text{FactTable}: \; i \neq j \implies \text{Keys}(i) \neq \text{Keys}(j)
$$

In simpler terms: **no two rows can share the same set of dimension foreign keys**. If they do, either the grain is wrong or the table needs a degenerate dimension (e.g., receipt number).

### Step 3 вҖ” Dimension Identification Logic

For each candidate dimension, apply the **"By-Axis" test**:
$$
\text{Is Dimension}_k \text{ valid?} \iff \exists \text{ a question of the form: "Total Metric by } k \text{?"}
$$

If the answer is **Yes** вҶ’ the attribute is a dimension.
If **No** вҶ’ it may be a fact (numeric) or should be dropped.

### Step 4 вҖ” Fact Identification Logic

Each fact must satisfy the **grain-alignment test**:
$$
\text{Fact}_f \text{ is valid} \iff \text{Fact}_f \text{ is measured exactly once per FactTable row at the declared grain}
$$

| Grain | Valid Fact | Invalid Fact (Why) |
|-------|------------|---------------------|
| One row per sale line | Quantity Sold, Line Revenue | Total Store Revenue (violates grain) |
| One row per ATM withdrawal | Withdrawal Amount, Fee | Account Daily Balance (different grain) |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example вҖ” Hospital Pharmacy

**Scenario:** A hospital wants to analyze its pharmacy operations.

### Step 1 вҖ” Business Process
> *"The pharmacy dispensing of medicine to in-patients and out-patients."*

### Step 2 вҖ” Grain
> *"One row per medicine line item per prescription per patient visit per day."*

### Step 3 вҖ” Dimensions
- **Dim_Patient** (patient demographics)
- **Dim_Doctor** (prescribing physician)
- **Dim_Medicine** (drug name, category, manufacturer)
- **Dim_Date** (date of dispensing)
- **Dim_Pharmacy_Location** (where dispensed)
- **Dim_Prescription_Type** (IPD/OPD)

### Step 4 вҖ” Facts
- **Quantity_Dispensed** (additive)
- **Unit_Price** (non-additive вҖ” should be averaged)
- **Total_Cost** (additive)
- **Insurance_Amount** (additive)
- **Patient_Paid_Amount** (additive)

> [!NOTE]
> The fact **Total_Cost** is calculated as $Quantity\_Dispensed \times Unit\_Price$ at the ETL layer and stored in the fact table for query performance вҖ” this is the **"pre-computed additive fact"** pattern.

### Validation Check
$$
\text{Quantity\_Dispensed} = \sum_{\text{all rows}} \text{Quantity\_Dispensed} \;\;\text{(Additive across every dimension вң“)}
$$

$$
\text{Unit\_Price} \;\text{cannot be summed} \;\Rightarrow \text{Non-additive, use AVG() in queries}
$$

---

## 3.3 Anti-Patterns That Violate the 4-Step Discipline

| Anti-Pattern | Which Step is Violated | Symptom |
|--------------|------------------------|---------|
| "I will add dimensions later" | Step 2 / 3 | Null FKs in fact table |
| "Store the customer's lifetime spend as a fact" | Step 2 | Mixes transaction grain with snapshot grain |
| "Date and time go in the same column" | Step 3 | Loss of drill-down to hour/minute |
| "Revenue and Margin % stored together as a fact" | Step 4 | Mixes additive with non-additive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequential Flow of the Four Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Business Requirement"] --> B["Step 1: Select Business Process"]
    B --> C{"Process measurable, source exists, business needs it?"}
    C -->|"No"| Z["Reject / Re-scope"]
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Step 2: Declare the Grain"]
    D --> E["One row = atomic event description"]
    E --> F["Step 3: Identify Dimensions"]
    F --> G["Who What When Where Why How axes"]
    G --> H["Step 4: Identify Facts"]
    H --> I["Additive, Semi, Non-Additive metrics"]
    I --> J["Output: Star Schema Design"]
    J --> K["Deploy to BI Tools"]
```

## 4.2 Resulting Star Schema (Hospital Pharmacy Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DP["Dim Patient"]:::dim
    DD["Dim Doctor"]:::dim
    DM["Dim Medicine"]:::dim
    DDT["Dim Date"]:::dim
    DPL["Dim Pharmacy Location"]:::dim
    DPT["Dim Prescription Type"]:::dim

    FT["Fact Pharmacy Dispense"]:::fact

    DP --- FT
    DD --- FT
    DM --- FT
    DDT --- FT
    DPL --- FT
    DPT --- FT

    classDef fact fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B5651D,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef dim fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Granularity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Need a numeric measure?"] -->|"No"| X1["Not a fact вҖ” check if it is a dimension attribute"]
    S -->|"Yes"| T["Is it measured at every grain row?"]
    T -->|"No"| X2["Wrong grain вҖ” create a separate fact table"]
    T -->|"Yes"| U["Sums correctly across all dimensions?"]
    U -->|"Yes"| A1["Additive Fact вң“"]
    U -->|"Only some"| A2["Semi-Additive вҖ” restrict SUM in BI"]
    U -->|"Never"| A3["Non-Additive вҖ” use AVG, MIN, MAX, ratio"]
```

## 4.4 Conformed Dimension Pattern (Drill-Across)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DD["Dim Date"]:::dim

    FS["Fact Sales"]:::fact
    FI["Fact Inventory"]:::fact
    FH["Fact HR Attendance"]:::fact

    DD --- FS
    DD --- FI
    DD --- FH
```

> [!NOTE]
> Because `Dim_Date` is **conformed** (shared across all three fact marts), a user can write a single query joining sales, inventory, and HR attendance on the same date dimension вҖ” this is the *drill-across* technique that distinguishes Kimball's architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024 Model Question]**
*List and briefly explain the four steps of dimensional modeling proposed by Ralph Kimball.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Select the Business Process (1 mark)** вҖ” Identify the real-world operational event (e.g., retail sales, hospital admission) to be modeled. It must be measurable and supported by source data.
2. **Declare the Grain (1 mark)** вҖ” Precisely state what one row in the fact table represents (e.g., one product line per receipt per day).
3. **Identify the Dimensions (0.5 mark)** вҖ” List the descriptive "who, what, when, where, why, how" context attributes.
4. **Identify the Facts (0.5 mark)** вҖ” List the numeric measurements (additive, semi-additive, non-additive) that fit the declared grain.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is meant by the "grain" of a fact table? Why must it be declared before dimensions are designed?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Grain definition (1 mark):** Grain is the atomic level of detail represented by a single row in the fact table. It answers the question: *"What does one row represent?"*
- **Why declare first? (2 marks):** Because the grain acts as the **contract** that locks the schema. All dimensions must describe the grain, and all facts must be measured *at* the grain. If the grain is changed after design, the entire fact table must be rebuilt. Declaring it first prevents mixed-grain errors and ensures every dimension and fact is valid.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students typically lose marks in three areas: (1) writing a vague grain statement like *"daily sales"* instead of the atomic form, (2) forgetting to classify facts as additive/semi/non-additive, and (3) drawing the star schema *without* labeling primary and foreign keys. Always include keys in your diagram.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024 Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **four steps of dimensional modeling** in detail. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Consider a **retail supermarket chain** that wants to analyze its sales. Apply the four steps to design a dimensional model. Clearly state the business process, grain, dimensions, and facts. Draw the resulting star schema. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

The four steps of dimensional modeling, as defined by Ralph Kimball, are as follows:

**1. Select the Business Process (1.5 marks)**
A business process is a low-level operational activity performed by the organization that is captured by an OLTP source system. Examples include order processing, shipments, and material movements. The data warehouse models these processes вҖ” not entire departments. The chosen process must be measurable, supported by available source data, and aligned with a real business need.

**2. Declare the Grain (2 marks)**
Grain is the most atomic level of detail stored in a fact table. It is a precise declaration in the form *"one row = one <event> per <entity> per <time>."* Declaring grain first prevents mixing different levels of detail. Examples:
- *One row per product per receipt line per day per store*
- *One row per ATM withdrawal per customer*

**3. Identify the Dimensions (2 marks)**
Dimensions provide the *descriptive context* вҖ” the "who, what, when, where, why, how" вҖ” surrounding each fact row. Each dimension becomes a separate dimension table linked to the fact via a foreign key. Conformed dimensions (shared across marts) enable consistent drill-across reporting.

**4. Identify the Facts (1.5 marks)**
Facts are the numeric measurements of the business process at the declared grain. They are classified as:
- **Additive** (sum across all dimensions, e.g., revenue)
- **Semi-additive** (sum across some, e.g., account balance)
- **Non-additive** (cannot be summed, e.g., unit price вҶ’ use AVG)

**Diagram (included for full 7 marks):**
The four-step flow is illustrated in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

**Step 1 вҖ” Business Process (1.5 marks):**
> *"The Point-of-Sale (POS) sales transactions of a retail supermarket chain."*

**Step 2 вҖ” Grain (1.5 marks):**
> *"One row per product line item per customer receipt per day per store."*

**Step 3 вҖ” Dimensions (2 marks):**
- Dim_Date (full date, day, month, quarter, year, day_of_week)
- Dim_Store (store_id, store_name, city, state, region, manager)
- Dim_Product (product_id, name, category, subcategory, brand, unit_price)
- Dim_Customer (customer_id, name, age_group, gender, loyalty_tier)
- Dim_Promotion (promo_id, type, start_date, end_date, discount_pct)
- Dim_Payment_Mode (cash, card, UPI, wallet)

**Step 4 вҖ” Facts (2 marks):**
- **Quantity_Sold** (additive)
- **Gross_Revenue** (additive)
- **Discount_Amount** (additive)
- **Net_Revenue** (additive, computed as Gross вҲ’ Discount)
- **Cost_Price** (semi-additive вҖ” meaningful as total, not per-time)
- **Profit_Margin_%** (non-additive вҖ” must be computed as ratio)

**Resulting Star Schema Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DD["Dim Date"]:::dim
    DS["Dim Store"]:::dim
    DP["Dim Product"]:::dim
    DC["Dim Customer"]:::dim
    DPR["Dim Promotion"]:::dim
    DPM["Dim Payment Mode"]:::dim
    FS["Fact Sales"]:::fact
    DD --- FS
    DS --- FS
    DP --- FS
    DC --- FS
    DPR --- FS
    DPM --- FS
    classDef fact fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B5651D,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef dim fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative ESE Choice]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **additive, semi-additive, and non-additive facts**. Give one example of each from a banking data warehouse. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **university** wants to design a data warehouse to analyze student academic performance. Apply the four steps of dimensional modeling and produce a star schema for the **course enrollment and examination** process. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

| Type | Definition (2 marks) | Banking Example (0.33 each) |
|------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Additive** | Can be summed across **all** dimensions without producing a meaningless result. | `Withdrawal_Amount` (sum across customer, branch, date, account type is valid) |
| **Semi-Additive** | Can be summed across **some** dimensions but not all (typically not across time). | `Account_Balance` вҖ” can sum across customers and branches, but summing daily balances across dates is meaningless; use latest snapshot. |
| **Non-Additive** | Cannot be summed across any dimension. Must be averaged, ratioed, or treated individually. | `Interest_Rate_%` вҖ” summing 5% + 5% = 10% has no meaning. Use AVG() or weighted computation. |

**Justification (remaining 3 marks):**
- Additive facts are the most flexible and the preferred design choice.
- Semi-additive facts are stored as snapshots in periodic snapshot fact tables.
- Non-additive facts should be pre-computed as ratios during ETL if the BI tool cannot handle them at query time.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

**Step 1 вҖ” Business Process (1.5 marks):**
> *"The course enrollment, internal assessment, and end-semester examination process of a university."*

**Step 2 вҖ” Grain (1.5 marks):**
> *"One row per student per course per semester per examination component (e.g., assignment 1, mid-term, end-sem)."*

**Step 3 вҖ” Dimensions (2 marks):**
- Dim_Student (student_id, name, program, department, batch, gender, category)
- Dim_Course (course_id, name, credits, program, semester_number, type)
- Dim_Date (enrollment_date, exam_date, year, month)
- Dim_Faculty (faculty_id, name, department, designation)
- Dim_Examination (exam_id, exam_type, max_marks, weightage_pct)
- Dim_Semester (semester_id, term, academic_year, start_date, end_date)

**Step 4 вҖ” Facts (2 marks):**
- `Marks_Scored` (non-additive at the row level but stored as measure)
- `Max_Marks` (semi-additive вҖ” denormalized in fact)
- `Attendance_Percentage` (non-additive вҖ” use AVG)
- `Grade_Points` (additive вҖ” useful for CGPA computation)
- `Is_Pass` (boolean flag вҖ” 0/1, additive вҶ’ useful for pass percentage)
- `Credit_Value` (semi-additive вҖ” used for weighted CGPA)

**Resulting Star Schema Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DST["Dim Student"]:::dim
    DCO["Dim Course"]:::dim
    DDT["Dim Date"]:::dim
    DFA["Dim Faculty"]:::dim
    DEX["Dim Examination"]:::dim
    DSE["Dim Semester"]:::dim
    FE["Fact Exam Result"]:::fact
    DST --- FE
    DCO --- FE
    DDT --- FE
    DFA --- FE
    DEX --- FE
    DSE --- FE
    classDef fact fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B5651D,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef dim fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- вӯҗ The **four steps in order** are: **Business Process вҶ’ Grain вҶ’ Dimensions вҶ’ Facts** (the *order is non-negotiable* in the exam).
- вӯҗ A **business process** is *what happens*, not *what department you work in*.
- вӯҗ **Grain** is the most atomic, most precise description of "one row" вҖ” always phrased as *"One row per ... per ... per ..."*
- вӯҗ **Dimensions** answer: Who? What? When? Where? Why? How?
- вӯҗ **Conformed dimensions** = shared across marts вҶ’ enable drill-across.
- вӯҗ **Additive facts** sum across all dimensions; **Semi-additive** sum across some; **Non-additive** never sum.
- вӯҗ **Factless fact tables** are valid вҖ” they record that an event happened (e.g., student attendance).
- вӯҗ Mixed grain = the most common **dimensional modeling anti-pattern**; never combine transaction-level and snapshot-level data in one fact table.
- вӯҗ Always classify facts and label the star schema with **PK/FK** in diagrams to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fact Tables, Dimension Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fact Tables, Dimension Tables # рҹ“ҳ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fact Tables вҖ” The Quantitative Heart of a Data Warehouse A **Fact Table** is the central, primary table in a star or snowflake schema of a data warehouse. It stores **quantitative business measurements (facts/measures)** and **foreign keys** that reference dimension tables. Fact tables typically contain the largest number of rows in a data warehouse but the fewest columns, and they are **highly normalized (typically 3NF)** in terms of dimensional modeling (i.e., they store numerical transaction-level data and reference all descriptive attributes via foreign keys). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Kimball):** A fact table stores the **performance measurements** resulting from an organization's business process events. It is composed of two types of columns: **foreign keys** (to dimension tables) and **numeric measures** (the actual business facts). ### рҹ”‘ Key Characteristics of a Fact Table - **Numeric & Additive (mostly)**: Stores measurable, usually additive, business metrics. - **Foreign Keys**: Each row contains a set of foreign keys referencing dimension tables. - **Sparse**: Many combinations of dimension keys may have no corresponding fact rows. - **Large in rows**: The most voluminous table in the warehouse (millions to billions of rows). - **Grain**: Defined by the **primary key** (composite of dimension foreign keys). ### рҹ“Ұ Common Examples - `Sales_Fact (date_key, product_key, store_key, customer_key, sales_amount, quantity_sold, profit)` - `Inventory_Snapshot_Fact` - `Bank_Transaction_Fact` - `Call_Center_Fact` - `Web_Clickstream_Fact` --- ## 1.2 Dimension Tables вҖ” The Descriptive Context A **Dimension Table** stores the **descriptive (textual/categorical) attributes** that provide context to the numeric facts stored in the fact table. Dimensions answer the *"who, what, where, when, why, how"* questions of the business. They are typically **highly denormalized** (flat, wide tables) and contain far fewer rows than fact tables. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A dimension table contains the **attributes** (or *characteristics*) of a business entity that are used to filter, group, and label fact data. Examples include *date, product, customer, store, employee, geography*. ### рҹ”‘ Key Characteristics of a Dimension Table - **Descriptive Text Fields**: Contains names, descriptions, categories. - **Surrogate Keys**: Uses integer surrogate keys (not natural keys) for performance and flexibility. - **Denormalized**: Wide, flat, redundant (e.g., entire hierarchy in one table). - **Smaller in row count**: Typically thousands to millions of rows (not billions). - **Slowly Changing**: Subject to change over time (managed via SCD types). --- ## 1.3 рҹҢҚ Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The Restaurant Receipt Imagine a **restaurant bill receipt**: | Component on Receipt | Data Warehouse Equivalent | |---|---| | **Items ordered, quantity, price, total** (the *numbers*) | **FACT TABLE** вҖ” measures like `quantity_sold`, `total_amount` | | **Date of bill, bill number, waiter name, table number** | **DIMENSION TABLEs** вҖ” `Dim_Date`, `Dim_Waiter`, `Dim_Table` | | **Cuisine type, dish category, spice level** (descriptors) | **DIMENSION attributes** | > [!TIP] > **Easy Memory Trick:** The **Fact Table = The Numbers** (verbs of business: sold, earned, shipped, clicked). **Dimension Tables = The Context** (nouns of business: who, what, where, when, how). --- ## 1.4 Why We Separate Facts from Dimensions? | Reason | Benefit | |---|---| | **Storage Efficiency** | Descriptive text is stored once in dim, not repeated in every fact row. | | **Query Performance** | Star joins (small dim + large fact) are highly optimized. | | **Flexibility** | Add new attributes to dimensions without disturbing fact tables. | | **Historical Tracking** | Dimensions can be versioned (SCDs) to track changes over time. | | **Usability** | Business users can intuitively navigate by attributes (drill-down, slice, dice). | # рҹ“ҳ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Fact Table A fact table is structurally composed of: ### A. Foreign Keys (Dimension Keys) вҖ” Composite Primary Key - One foreign key per related dimension. - The set of all FKs forms the **composite primary key** (or its supertype). - Defines the **grain** of the fact table. ### B. Numeric Measures (Facts) Measures are categorized into three families: | Measure Type | Definition | Example | Aggregation Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Additive** | Can be summed across **all** dimensions | `sales_amount`, `quantity` | Sum works anywhere | | **Semi-Additive** | Can be summed across some, but not all, dimensions | `account_balance`, `inventory_level` | Cannot sum across time | | **Non-Additive** | Cannot be summed across any dimension | `unit_price`, `ratio`, `temperature` | Must be averaged or computed | ### C. Degenerate Dimensions - Operational transaction IDs (e.g., `invoice_number`, `order_id`) that have no associated dimension table. ### D. Audit/Tracking Columns - `load_date`, `source_system`, `etl_batch_id`. --- ## 2.2 Types of Fact Tables (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 вҖ” Three Canonical Fact Table Types (Kimball):** ### 1пёҸвғЈ Transactional Fact Tables (Most Common) - **Grain**: One row per **business event/transaction**. - **Examples**: POS sales, ATM withdrawals, website clicks, insurance claims. - **Time Stamping**: Each row has a transaction date foreign key. - **Sparsity**: Can be very sparse for some dimension combinations. - **Snapshot Frequency**: Continuously loaded as events occur. ### 2пёҸвғЈ Periodic Snapshot Fact Tables - **Grain**: One row per **dimension combination per time period** (e.g., per day/week/month). - **Examples**: Monthly account balance, daily inventory level. - **Predictability**: Pre-defined rows (no sparsity). - **Time Truncation**: Predetermined periodicity. ### 3пёҸвғЈ Accumulating Snapshot Fact Tables - **Grain**: One row per **business process lifecycle** (one row per pipeline, modified over time). - **Examples**: Order fulfillment pipeline (order_date вҶ’ ship_date вҶ’ deliver_date). - **Multiple Date FKs**: Multiple foreign keys to the same `Dim_Date` (role-playing). - **Row Updates**: Same row updated as the process advances (rare in DW). --- ## 2.3 Anatomy of a Dimension Table A dimension table has four logical column groups: | Group | Purpose | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Surrogate Key (PK)** | System-generated integer | `product_key = 1245` | | **Natural Key** | Real-world business ID | `SKU = "ABC-001"` | | **Outrigger Attributes** | Descriptive texts | `product_name`, `brand`, `color` | | **Hierarchical Attributes** | Rolled-up levels | `category`, `subcategory`, `department` | | **Slowly Changing Attributes** | Versioned over time | `price_history`, `manager_change` | --- ## 2.4 Types of Dimension Tables (KTU High-Yield) ### 1пёҸвғЈ Conformed Dimensions - Shared **across multiple fact tables** (or multiple data marts). - Ensures consistent reporting (e.g., `Dim_Date` shared by Sales Fact and Inventory Fact). - *Most important dimension type in enterprise DW.* ### 2пёҸвғЈ Junk Dimensions - Collects **low-cardinality flags and indicators** that would otherwise clutter the fact table. - Example: `payment_method`, `order_priority`, `promo_flag`, `is_gift_wrap`. ### 3пёҸвғЈ Degenerate Dimensions - A dimension **with no content** (just the transaction ID lives in the fact table). - Example: `invoice_number`, `order_id` in fact table. ### 4пёҸвғЈ Role-Playing Dimensions - A single physical dimension used in **multiple roles** within the same fact table. - Example: `Dim_Date` played as `order_date`, `ship_date`, `delivery_date`. ### 5пёҸвғЈ Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) | Type | Behavior | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **SCD Type 1** | Overwrite old value | Correcting typos, not history-sensitive | | **SCD Type 2** | Add new row, version via effective dates | Tracking historical accuracy (most common) | | **SCD Type 3** | Add "previous" column | Limited history (e.g., previous manager) | | **SCD Type 4** | History in separate mini-dim | Large history tables | | **SCD Type 6** | Hybrid (1+2+3) | Track current + historical + previous | ### 6пёҸвғЈ Outrigger Dimensions - A dimension that is **referenced from another dimension** (snowflaking effect), e.g., `Dim_City` referenced from `Dim_Store`. --- ## 2.5 Grain вҖ” The Most Critical Concept > [!WARNING] > **Grain defines what ONE ROW in a fact table represents.** Once declared, all measures and keys must conform to that grain. Mixed grain = the #1 fatal DW modeling mistake. ### Examples of Grain Statements - *"One row per sales transaction line item per store per day."* - *"One row per student per course per semester."* - *"One row per ATM withdrawal event."* --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Fact Table Cardinality** | $C_{fact} \;\approx\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert \;\times\; S_{sparse}$ | rows; $S_{sparse} \in (0,1]$ | | 2 | **Dimension Hierarchy Aggregation** | $R_{rolled\_up} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} r_i$ | sum of child rows | | 3 | **Snowflake Normalization Depth** | $D_{sf} \;=\; \text{levels of normalization} \;\geq\; 1$ | 1 = star, >1 = snowflake | | 4 | **Surrogate Key Generation** | $SK_n \;=\; SK_{n-1} \;+\; 1$ | integer, monotonically increasing | | 5 | **SCD Type 2 Effective Date** | $E_{end} \;=\; E_{start\,of\,new\,row} \;-\; 1$ | timestamp arithmetic | | 6 | **Storage Size of Fact Table** | $S_{fact} \;=\; N_{rows} \;\times\; (k \cdot 4B + m \cdot 8B)$ | bytes; $k$ FKs, $m$ measures | | 7 | **Additive Measure Aggregation** | $A_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i$ | valid for additive facts | | 8 | **Non-Additive (Ratio) Aggregation** | $R_{agg} \;\neq\; \sum \frac{n_i}{d_i}$ | must use $\frac{\sum n_i}{\sum d_i}$ | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Retail Analytics**: Sales fact + product/store/date dimensions вҶ’ demand forecasting. - **Banking**: Transaction fact + customer/branch dimensions вҶ’ fraud detection, AML. - **Healthcare**: Patient encounter fact + diagnosis/doctor/insurance dimensions вҶ’ outcome analytics. - **Telecom**: Call detail records (CDR) fact + cell/tower/customer dimensions вҶ’ network optimization. - **Manufacturing**: Production fact + machine/shift/material dimensions вҶ’ OEE, quality analytics. - **E-Commerce**: Clickstream fact + session/product/device dimensions вҶ’ recommendation systems. - **Education (KTU context)**: Exam result fact + student/course/faculty dimensions вҶ’ academic analytics. # рҹ“ҳ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Designing a Sales Data Mart ### Step 1 вҖ” Identify the Business Process *"We want to analyze retail sales performance across product, store, time, and promotion."* **Business Process**: Retail Sales Transaction. ### Step 2 вҖ” Declare the Grain > *"One row per sales transaction line item per store per day per promotion."* This single sentence locks: - **FKs required**: `date_key`, `product_key`, `store_key`, `promotion_key`, possibly `cashier_key`. - **Measures allowed**: All facts must be measurable at the line-item level. ### Step 3 вҖ” Identify the Dimensions | Dimension | Type | Key Attributes | Cardinality | |---|---|---|---| | `Dim_Date` | Conformed (role-playing) | day, week, month, quarter, year, holiday_flag | 365 Г— years | | `Dim_Product` | Standard | SKU, name, brand, category, subcategory, dept | 10KвҖ“100K | | `Dim_Store` | Standard with outrigger | store_name, city, state, region, country | 100вҖ“10K | | `Dim_Promotion` | Junk-style | promo_id, discount_pct, ad_type, media | 100вҖ“1K | | `Dim_Customer` | Standard (optional) | cust_id, name, segment, age_band | 1M+ | ### Step 4 вҖ” Identify the Measures (Facts) | Measure | Type | Aggregation Rule | |---|---|---| | `quantity_sold` | Additive (integer) | $\sum m_i$ | | `sales_amount` | Additive (currency) | $\sum m_i$ | | `cost_amount` | Additive (currency) | $\sum m_i$ | | `profit` | Semi-additive | $\sum$ across products/stores, but limited over time | | `unit_price` | Non-additive | must compute as $\frac{\sum sales}{\sum quantity}$ | | `discount_pct` | Non-additive | weighted average required | | `gross_margin_pct` | Non-additive (ratio) | never sum; use $\frac{\sum profit}{\sum sales}$ | ### Step 5 вҖ” Build the Fact Table Schema ``` SALES_FACT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ PK date_key INT вҶ’ Dim_Date PK product_key INT вҶ’ Dim_Product PK store_key INT вҶ’ Dim_Store PK promotion_key INT вҶ’ Dim_Promotion PK cashier_key INT вҶ’ Dim_Cashier DD invoice_number VARCHAR (degenerate dim) quantity_sold INT (additive) unit_price DECIMAL (non-additive) sales_amount DECIMAL (additive) cost_amount DECIMAL (additive) profit DECIMAL (additive) discount_amount DECIMAL (additive) load_date TIMESTAMP ``` --- ## 3.2 Derivation вҖ” Storage Size Estimation > **Problem**: Estimate the size of a `SALES_FACT` table for a chain with **500 stores**, **10,000 products**, and **5 years** of daily transactions, with average **100 line items per store per day**. ### Step 1: Total Rows $$ \begin{aligned} N_{rows} &= N_{stores} \times N_{days} \times N_{lines/store/day} \\ N_{rows} &= 500 \;\times\; (5 \times 365) \;\times\; 100 \\ N_{rows} &= 500 \;\times\; 1825 \;\times\; 100 \\ N_{rows} &= 91{,}250{,}000 \;\text{rows} \;\;(\approx 91.25 \text{ million}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Row Width $$ \begin{aligned} W_{row} &= (\text{FKs}) + (\text{measures}) + (\text{degenerate}) + (\text{audit}) \\ W_{row} &= (5 \times 4B) + (6 \times 8B) + 16B + 8B \\ W_{row} &= 20B + 48B + 16B + 8B \\ W_{row} &= 92 \text{ bytes/row} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Total Fact Table Size $$ \begin{aligned} S_{fact} &= N_{rows} \times W_{row} \\ S_{fact} &= 91{,}250{,}000 \times 92 \\ S_{fact} &= 8{,}395{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\ S_{fact} &\approx 7.82 \text{ GB (uncompressed)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Realistic Production Size (with compression 3:1 and indexes) $$ S_{prod} \approx \frac{7.82}{3} \approx 2.6 \text{ GB (compressed)} \;\;+\;\; \text{indexes} \;\approx\; 5\text{вҖ“}6 \text{ GB} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip**: This kind of storage estimation question is a frequent 7-mark question вҖ” practice declaring grain first, then computing rows Г— row width. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Fact Table Type Selection | Criterion | Transactional | Periodic Snapshot | Accumulating Snapshot | |---|---|---|---| | **Grain** | One event | One period per entity | One process lifecycle | | **Time FKs** | 1 (event time) | 1 (snapshot date) | Multiple (milestone dates) | | **Load Pattern** | Insert only | Insert (often overwrite) | Update + Insert | | **Sparsity** | High | None (predefined) | None | | **Latency** | Real-time / near-RT | Daily / weekly | Process-driven | | **Use Case** | POS, ATM, web logs | Inventory, balances | Order fulfillment, claims | | **Volume Trend** | Grows indefinitely | Grows per period | Grows per process | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Dimension Type Selection | Decision Question | Recommended Dimension Type | |---|---| | Is this dim used in multiple fact tables? | **Conformed Dimension** | | Are these low-cardinality flags polluting the fact? | **Junk Dimension** | | Is this just an operational transaction ID? | **Degenerate Dimension** | | Is the same date dim used multiple times in one fact? | **Role-Playing Dimension** | | Does the attribute change over time and need history? | **SCD Type 2** | | Does the dim reference another dim? | **Outrigger (Snowflake)** | # рҹ“ҳ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 вӯҗ Star Schema вҖ” Fact at Center, Dimensions as Spokes > **Description:** A star schema has a central fact table connected to multiple denormalized dimension tables. Each dimension has a single surrogate key that joins directly to the fact table. The shape resembles a star. ```mermaid graph TD FACT["SALES_FACT_TABLE
date_key, product_key, store_key, promotion_key
quantity_sold, sales_amount, profit"] DD["Dim_Date
date_key, day, month, quarter, year, holiday_flag"] DP["Dim_Product
product_key, sku, name, brand, category"] DS["Dim_Store
store_key, store_name, city, state, region"] DPR["Dim_Promotion
promotion_key, promo_name, discount_pct"] FACT -->|FK date_key| DD FACT -->|FK product_key| DP FACT -->|FK store_key| DS FACT -->|FK promotion_key| DPR ``` --- ## 4.2 вқ„пёҸ Snowflake Schema вҖ” Normalized Dimensions > **Description:** A snowflake schema is a star schema where dimension tables are further normalized into multiple related tables. The shape resembles a snowflake with branches. ```mermaid graph TD FACT2["SALES_FACT_TABLE
date_key, product_key, store_key, promotion_key
sales_amount, profit"] DD2["Dim_Date
date_key, day, month_key"] DM["Dim_Month
month_key, month_name, quarter_key"] DQ["Dim_Quarter
quarter_key, quarter_name, year"] DP2["Dim_Product
product_key, name, subcategory_key"] DSC["Dim_Subcategory
subcategory_key, name, category_key"] DCA["Dim_Category
category_key, name, department"] DS2["Dim_Store
store_key, name, city_key"] DCT["Dim_City
city_key, name, state_key"] DST["Dim_State
state_key, name, region_key"] DRG["Dim_Region
region_key, name, country"] FACT2 -->|date_key| DD2 DD2 -->|month_key| DM DM -->|quarter_key| DQ FACT2 -->|product_key| DP2 DP2 -->|subcategory_key| DSC DSC -->|category_key| DCA FACT2 -->|store_key| DS2 DS2 -->|city_key| DCT DCT -->|state_key| DST DST -->|region_key| DRG ``` --- ## 4.3 рҹ”„ Fact Constellation (Galaxy Schema) вҖ” Multiple Fact Tables Sharing Conformed Dimensions > **Description:** Multiple fact tables share one or more conformed dimension tables, forming a constellation of stars. This is the **enterprise warehouse pattern**. ```mermaid graph TD SF["SALES_FACT
date_key, product_key, store_key, sales_amount"] IF["INVENTORY_SNAPSHOT_FACT
date_key, product_key, store_key, on_hand_qty"] DF["DELIVERY_FACT
date_key, product_key, store_key, supplier_key, qty_delivered"] DDA["Dim_Date
date_key, day, month, year"] DPR2["Dim_Product
product_key, name, brand, category"] DST3["Dim_Store
store_key, name, city"] DSU["Dim_Supplier
supplier_key, name, region"] SF --> DDA SF --> DPR2 SF --> DST3 IF --> DDA IF --> DPR2 IF --> DST3 DF --> DDA DF --> DPR2 DF --> DST3 DF --> DSU ``` --- ## 4.4 рҹ“Ҡ Block-Level Architecture вҖ” ETL Flow for Fact/Dim Loading ```mermaid graph LR SRCS["SOURCE SYSTEMS
OLTP, files, APIs, logs"] STG["STAGING AREA
raw extracts, no transforms"] TRANS["TRANSFORMATION LAYER
clean, dedupe, conform keys"] DDS["DIMENSION PROCESSOR
assign surrogate keys
handle SCD1, SCD2"] DFS["FACT PROCESSOR
lookup dim keys
aggregate measures"] DW["DATA WAREHOUSE
Star / Snowflake schemas"] DM["DATA MARTS
department-specific slices"] BI["BI TOOLS
dashboards, reports, OLAP"] SRCS --> STG STG --> TRANS TRANS --> DDS TRANS --> DFS DDS --> DW DFS --> DW DW --> DM DM --> BI ``` --- ## 4.5 рҹ§© Conceptual Block вҖ” SCD Type 2 Implementation ```mermaid graph TD IN["NEW INCOMING ROW
product_key=NAT, price=120"] LKP["LOOKUP
in Dim_Product
on natural key"] EX["EXISTING ROW FOUND
product_key=55, price=100"] EXO["EXISTING ROW NOT FOUND
insert new"] IN --> LKP LKP -->|FOUND| EX LKP -->|NOT FOUND| EXO EX -->|"STEP A: EXPIRE old row
set effective_end_date=now-1
set is_current=0"| EXR["UPDATE OLD ROW"] EXR -->|"STEP B: INSERT new version
new surrogate key
effective_start=now
is_current=1"| NEW["INSERT NEW ROW"] EXO --> INSERT1["INSERT NEW ROW
new surrogate key
effective_start=1900-01-01
effective_end=9999-12-31
is_current=1"] ``` # рҹ“ҳ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## рҹ“қ PART A вҖ” 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### вқ“ Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024] **Define a fact table. List any two characteristics of a fact table.** **Model Answer:** A **fact table** is the central table in a star or snowflake schema of a data warehouse that stores **quantitative business measurements (facts)** and **foreign keys** referencing dimension tables. **Characteristics:** 1. **Contains numeric, additive (typically) measures** like `sales_amount`, `quantity_sold`, `profit`. 2. **Composite primary key** formed by the set of foreign keys referencing dimension tables; the set of FKs defines the **grain** of the table. 3. **Largest table in the warehouse** (in terms of row count), but with relatively few columns. 4. **Sparse** вҖ” many dimension combinations may have no corresponding fact row. --- ### вқ“ Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023] **Differentiate between fact tables and dimension tables. (Any 4 points)** **Model Answer:** | # | Fact Table | Dimension Table | |---|---|---| | 1 | Stores **numeric measures / quantitative data** | Stores **descriptive attributes / textual data** | | 2 | Contains **foreign keys** to dimension tables | Contains **surrogate primary key** | | 3 | **Highly normalized** (3NF), few columns, many rows | **Highly denormalized**, many columns, fewer rows | | 4 | Largest table by row count (millionsвҖ“billions) | Smaller row count (thousandsвҖ“millions) | | 5 | Examples: `Sales_Fact`, `Inventory_Fact` | Examples: `Dim_Customer`, `Dim_Product` | | 6 | Grain defined by composite of FKs | Describes business entities | --- ## рҹ“қ PART B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > **Note**: As per KTU 2024 scheme, each module question of 14 marks has an internal choice (either-or). Two alternate questions are provided below. --- ### вқ“ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024] **(a)** Explain the **three types of fact tables** with suitable examples. Highlight the differences in their grain, load pattern, and use cases. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat star schema diagram, explain the structure of a **retail sales data mart**. Identify the measures, dimensions, and grain. **(7 Marks)** ### вң… Model Solution вҖ” Question A #### Part (a) вҖ” Three Types of Fact Tables The three canonical types of fact tables, defined by Ralph Kimball, are: **1. Transactional (Transaction) Fact Table** - **Grain**: One row per **business event / transaction**. - **Time FK**: A single transaction date. - **Load Pattern**: Inserts only (append). - **Sparsity**: Can be high вҖ” not all product-store-day combos appear. - **Examples**: - POS: one row per line item sold. - ATM: one row per withdrawal event. - Web logs: one row per click. - Insurance: one row per claim submission. - **Volume Trend**: Continuously grows; never updated or deleted. **2. Periodic Snapshot Fact Table** - **Grain**: One row per **entity per time period** (e.g., per day, week, month). - **Time FK**: A single snapshot-period date. - **Load Pattern**: Insert (or overwrite) at the end of each period. - **Sparsity**: None вҖ” pre-defined rows for every entity-period combination. - **Examples**: - Bank account balance as of end-of-day (one row per account per day). - Inventory level at end of each week (one row per SKU per store per week). - Student attendance per month. - **Volume Trend**: Grows linearly with `(entities Г— periods)`. **3. Accumulating Snapshot Fact Table** - **Grain**: One row per **business process lifecycle** (one row per pipeline / workflow). - **Time FKs**: **Multiple date foreign keys** to the same `Dim_Date` (role-playing). - **Load Pattern**: **Update** the same row as the process advances (rare in DW). - **Sparsity**: None вҖ” one row exists for every in-flight or completed process. - **Examples**: - Order fulfillment: row inserted on order date, updated on ship date, delivery date, return date. - Insurance claim lifecycle: row from claim вҶ’ approval вҶ’ settlement. - Student admission вҶ’ enrollment вҶ’ graduation lifecycle. - **Volume Trend**: One row per pipeline instance, with multiple updates. **Comparative Summary Table:** | Aspect | Transactional | Periodic Snapshot | Accumulating Snapshot | |---|---|---|---| | Grain | One event | One entity per period | One process lifecycle | | # of Date FKs | 1 | 1 | Multiple (role-playing) | | Insert/Update | Insert | Insert | Update + Insert | | Sparsity | High | None | None | | Latency | Real-time | Periodic | Process-driven | | Typical Example | Sales line item | Daily balance | Order pipeline | --- #### Part (b) вҖ” Star Schema for Retail Sales Data Mart **Business Process**: Retail Sales. **Grain Declaration**: *One row per sales transaction line item per store per day per promotion per cashier.* **Star Schema Diagram:** ```mermaid graph TD FACTS["SALES_FACT
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
PK date_key
PK product_key
PK store_key
PK promotion_key
PK cashier_key
DD invoice_no
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
quantity_sold INT
unit_price DEC
sales_amount DEC
cost_amount DEC
profit DEC
discount_amt DEC"] DDATE["Dim_Date
date_key PK
full_date, day, week
month, quarter, year
is_holiday, fiscal_period"] DPROD["Dim_Product
product_key PK
sku, name, brand
category, subcategory
department, package_size"] DSTORE["Dim_Store
store_key PK
store_name, type
city, state, region
country, open_date"] DPROM["Dim_Promotion
promotion_key PK
promo_name
discount_pct
ad_type, media"] DCASH["Dim_Cashier
cashier_key PK
employee_id, name
shift, experience_lvl"] FACTS --> DDATE FACTS --> DPROD FACTS --> DSTORE FACTS --> DPROM FACTS --> DCASH ``` **Measure Classification:** | Measure | Type | Aggregable? | |---|---|---| | `quantity_sold` | Additive | Sum across all dims | | `sales_amount` | Additive (currency) | Sum across all dims | | `cost_amount` | Additive (currency) | Sum across all dims | | `profit` | Additive (semi) | Sum across products/stores, limited time | | `unit_price` | Non-additive | Compute as $\frac{\sum sales}{\sum qty}$ | | `discount_amt` | Additive | Sum | **Key Design Decisions:** - Used **surrogate integer keys** for all dimensions. - `Dim_Date` is a **conformed dimension** (reusable across marts). - `invoice_no` is a **degenerate dimension** in the fact table. - No snowflaking вҖ” all dimensions are denormalized flat tables. - Granularity is **explicitly declared** and locked. --- ### вқ“ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the different **types of dimension tables** used in a data warehouse with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What are **slowly changing dimensions**? Explain **SCD Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3** with examples. **(7 Marks)** ### вң… Model Solution вҖ” Question B #### Part (a) вҖ” Types of Dimension Tables Dimension tables provide the **descriptive context** to numeric facts. The principal types are: **1. Conformed Dimension** - A dimension that is **shared across multiple fact tables / data marts**, ensuring consistent reporting. - **Example**: `Dim_Date` shared by Sales, Inventory, and Delivery facts. `Dim_Customer` used in both Sales and Service. - *Most important in enterprise DW architecture.* **2. Junk Dimension** - A dimension that consolidates **low-cardinality flags, indicators, and miscellaneous text attributes** that would otherwise occupy many sparse foreign-key columns in the fact table. - **Example**: `Dim_Promo_Flags` with combinations of `is_gift_wrap Г— payment_method Г— order_priority Г— delivery_type`. **3. Degenerate Dimension** - A **dimension key that has no associated dimension table** вҖ” typically an operational transaction ID that lives in the fact table itself. - **Example**: `invoice_number`, `order_id`, `transaction_ticket_id` in the fact table. **4. Role-Playing Dimension** - A single physical dimension table **used in multiple roles** within the same fact table, by creating multiple foreign keys to it. - **Example**: `Dim_Date` used as `order_date_key`, `ship_date_key`, `delivery_date_key`, `return_date_key` in a sales fact. **5. Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD)** - A dimension whose attributes **change over time** and require a strategy for tracking history. - **Example**: `Dim_Customer` with address changes вҖ” managed via SCD Type 2. **6. Outrigger Dimension** - A dimension that is **referenced from another dimension** rather than from the fact table (creates snowflaking). - **Example**: `Dim_Store` references `Dim_City` (which itself references `Dim_State`). --- #### Part (b) вҖ” Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) > **Definition**: A Slowly Changing Dimension is one whose attribute values **change slowly and unpredictably over time** rather than on a fixed schedule. The DW must decide how to handle these changes. **SCD TYPE 1 вҖ” Overwrite (No History)** - **Mechanism**: When the attribute changes, **update the value in place**. No history retained. - **Example**: Customer `John` lives in `Kochi`. He moves to `Trivandrum`. The `city` column is **overwritten** to `Trivandrum`. The previous value is lost. - **Use Case**: Corrections, non-critical attributes, when history is not needed. - **SQL Example**: ```sql UPDATE Dim_Customer SET city = 'Trivandrum' WHERE customer_key = 555; ``` **SCD TYPE 2 вҖ” Add New Row (Full History)** - **Mechanism**: On change, **expire the current row** (set `effective_end_date = today - 1`, `is_current = 0`) and **insert a new row** with a new surrogate key and `effective_start_date = today`, `is_current = 1`. - **Example**: Customer `John` (key 555) in Kochi. He moves to Trivandrum. - Old row: `key=555, city='Kochi', eff_start='2020-01-01', eff_end='2024-12-31', is_current=0`. - New row: `key=556, city='Trivandrum', eff_start='2025-01-01', eff_end='9999-12-31', is_current=1`. - **Use Case**: When historical accuracy is required (most common). - **SQL Example**: ```sql -- Step 1: Expire old row UPDATE Dim_Customer SET effective_end_date = CURRENT_DATE - 1, is_current = 0 WHERE customer_key = 555 AND is_current = 1; -- Step 2: Insert new version INSERT INTO Dim_Customer (customer_key, name, city, effective_start_date, effective_end_date, is_current) VALUES (NEXT_SK, 'John', 'Trivandrum', CURRENT_DATE, '9999-12-31', 1); ``` **SCD TYPE 3 вҖ” Add New Column (Limited History)** - **Mechanism**: Add a **"previous value" column** alongside the current value. Stores only the most recent prior value. - **Example**: - Original: `city = 'Kochi'`, `previous_city = NULL`. - After move: `city = 'Trivandrum'`, `previous_city = 'Kochi'`. - **Use Case**: When only the *previous* value matters (e.g., tracking just one prior manager or one prior region). **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | SCD Type 1 | SCD Type 2 | SCD Type 3 | |---|---|---|---| | History Retained | None | Full history | One prior value | | Row Count Impact | No change | Grows on each change | No change | | New Columns | None | `eff_start`, `eff_end`, `is_current` | `previous_value` | | Query for "current" | Always natural key | Filter `is_current = 1` | Direct read | | Query for "as-of date" | Not possible | Filter on date range | Limited to one prior | | Best For | Corrections | Auditable history | Recent-change tracking | --- > [!WARNING] > ### вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > 1. **Never confuse grain with measures.** Grain is *what one row represents*. Measures are *what is being measured*. Examiners deduct marks if grain and measure types are mismatched. > 2. **Non-additive facts must NEVER be summed.** Summing `unit_price` or `discount_pct` across rows is a classic error that loses 2вҖ“3 marks. > 3. **Surrogate keys are integers, not natural keys.** If you write `customer_id = "CUST001"`, expect a deduction. > 4. **Star schema вү  Snowflake schema.** Star = denormalized dim; Snowflake = normalized dim. Drawing a snowflake diagram and calling it a star is a 1вҖ“2 mark penalty. > 5. **SCD Type 2 requires `is_current` flag** (or surrogate key re-assignment), not just `effective_end_date`. Examiners check this flag explicitly. > 6. **Fact table primary key is COMPOSITE of foreign keys**, not an auto-increment ID. Adding a surrogate fact_id is a Kimball anti-pattern. --- ## рҹ§  Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rules вҖ” Memorize These!** - вң… **Fact Table** = the **numbers** (measures + FKs); defines the **grain** via composite PK. - вң… **Dimension Table** = the **context** (descriptive attributes); wide, denormalized, surrogate keyed. - вң… **Grain must be declared FIRST**, before any design. - вң… **3 Types of Fact Tables**: Transactional, Periodic Snapshot, Accumulating Snapshot. - вң… **3 Most Common SCDs**: Type 1 (overwrite), Type 2 (new row + history), Type 3 (previous column). - вң… **6 Types of Dimensions**: Conformed, Junk, Degenerate, Role-Playing, Slowly Changing, Outrigger. - вң… **Additive measures can be summed freely**; **Non-additive measures (ratios, prices) cannot** вҖ” use weighted average. - вң… **Surrogate keys = system-generated integers**, decoupled from natural business keys. - вң… **Star schema** = denormalized dimensions; **Snowflake schema** = normalized dimensions. - вң… **Conformed dimensions are shared** across multiple facts вҖ” critical for enterprise consistency. - вң… **Degenerate dimensions** = transaction IDs living in the fact table (no separate dim). - вң… **Role-playing dimensions** = one physical dim joined to fact via multiple FKs (e.g., date played 4 ways). - вң… **Junk dimensions** = consolidation of low-cardinality flags to avoid sparse FKs. - вң… **Accumulating snapshot fact tables UPDATE rows** вҖ” anomaly, but valid for process pipelines. - вң… **Storage estimation formula**: $S_{fact} = N_{rows} \times W_{row}$ (always derive row width explicitly). - вң… **Star schema is preferred for BI** (fewer joins, faster queries); Snowflake saves space but increases complexity.

topics.title: Data Warehouse Design вҖ“ Top-Down & Bottom-Up, Fact Table Granularity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Design вҖ“ Top-Down & Bottom-Up, Fact Table Granularity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Data Warehouse Design Methodologies A **Data Warehouse (DW)** is a subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile collection of data used to support strategic decision-making. Designing such a warehouse is a complex, non-trivial engineering task, and the academic and industry literature has crystallized around **two canonical, opposing design philosophies** вҖ” the **Top-Down** approach championed by **Bill Inmon** (the "Father of the Data Warehouse") and the **Bottom-Up** approach championed by **Ralph Kimball**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Students must clearly articulate the philosophical differences, procedural steps, advantages, and limitations of *both* approaches, as KTU ESE questions frequently ask for direct comparisons. #### Formal Definition вҖ” Top-Down Design > **Top-Down Design** is a *data-centric*, *enterprise-wide* methodology in which the data warehouse is first conceived as a **normalized (3NF) Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)**, which then feeds multiple subject-specific **Data Marts**. The EDW acts as the single, integrated source-of-truth for the entire organization. #### Formal Definition вҖ” Bottom-Up Design > **Bottom-Up Design** is a *business-requirements-centric* methodology in which individual **Data Marts (dimensional/star-schema)** are constructed first based on departmental reporting needs, and these data marts are subsequently integrated (via conformed dimensions) to form the enterprise data warehouse. The dimensional model *is* the warehouse. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” Building a House vs. Building Room-by-Room | Approach | Real-World Analogy | Mindset | |---|---|---| | **Top-Down** | Building the **entire house first** вҖ” foundation, walls, plumbing, electrical, roof вҖ” *then* furnishing individual rooms. | "Design the whole enterprise, then slice it." | | **Bottom-Up** | **Furnishing one room at a time** (kitchen, bedroom, living room) based on what the family needs *today*, and ensuring consistent paint colors and furniture style so the rooms later *feel* like one unified house. | "Deliver value quickly, integrate later." | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway for KTU Students:** Top-Down = *Blueprint-First Engineering* (Civil-engineering mindset). Bottom-Up = *Agile Iterative Delivery* (Software-engineering mindset). The KTU examiner expects you to mention that **Kimball's Bottom-Up is more widely adopted in industry** because it delivers **early ROI (Return on Investment)** to business users, while **Inmon's Top-Down is preferred in large, highly-regulated enterprises** (e.g., banking, government). --- ### 1.3 Fact Table Granularity вҖ” Formal Definition > **Fact Table Granularity** is the *atomic (lowest) level of detail* at which facts (measurements of business events) are stored in a fact table. It defines *what a single row of the fact table represents*. Once committed to a database, the granularity of a fact table is **immutable** вҖ” it can never be refined to a finer level without reloading the entire warehouse. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kimball's Cardinal Rule:** *"The granularity of the fact table must be decided **before** any dimension or measure is defined. It is the single most important design decision in dimensional modeling."* #### Real-World Analogy вҖ” Granularity Imagine a **CCTV camera in a bank**: - If the camera records **one snapshot per second** вҶ’ **fine-grained** (transaction-level). - If it records **one snapshot per hour** вҶ’ **coarse-grained** (periodic snapshot). - If it records **only the daily total number of customers** вҶ’ **summary-level** (accumulated snapshot or pre-aggregated). You can always **summarize** a fine-grained fact table into a coarser one (roll-up), but you can **never** disaggregate a coarse fact table into a finer one. Hence, KTU expects students to favour the **finest practical granularity**. --- ### 1.4 The Three Canonical Granularities > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Table (Memorize Verbatim):** | Granularity Type | What One Row Represents | Example | Time Dimension | |---|---|---|---| | **Transaction Grain** | A single business event/transaction at a point in time | One line-item of a sales invoice | Event timestamp | | **Periodic Snapshot Grain** | The state of a process at regular, predictable intervals (e.g., daily) | Account balance snapshot at end of every day | Daily / Weekly / Monthly | | **Accumulating Snapshot Grain** | The **lifecycle** of a process with a well-defined start and end | One row per order, updated as it moves from Order вҶ’ Ship вҶ’ Deliver вҶ’ Invoice | Multiple milestone dates | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Top-Down Design вҖ” Operational Steps (Inmon's Methodology) The Top-Down approach is decomposed into the following sequential engineering phases. The KTU 2024 examiner rewards students who list these phases in the **exact canonical order**. 1. **Enterprise Modeling & Requirement Gathering** - Conduct extensive interviews with C-level executives, business analysts, and department heads. - Identify the *enterprise-wide* data entities (e.g., Customer, Product, Account, Transaction). 2. **Conceptual & Logical Modeling of the EDW** - Build a **Corporate Information Factory (CIF)** model вҖ” usually a high-level **ER (Entity-Relationship) diagram** at the conceptual level. 3. **Normalization (3NF) of the EDW** - Apply 1NF вҶ’ 2NF вҶ’ 3NF rules. The EDW is a *normalized* relational schema that eliminates redundancy and preserves data integrity. 4. **Physical Implementation of the EDW** - Deploy the EDW on a high-end RDBMS (e.g., Oracle Exadata, Teradata, IBM Db2). 5. **Derivation of Subject-Specific Data Marts** - Once the EDW is operational, derive *dependent* data marts (often denormalized/star-schema) for departments like Sales, Finance, HR. 6. **End-User Tools & OLAP Layer** - Build reports, dashboards, and OLAP cubes on top of the data marts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Top-Down (The "Why" Behind Each Step):** > - The *normalized* EDW guarantees **no data redundancy** (single source of truth). > - Enterprise scope ensures **cross-functional consistency** (a customer is the same customer across Sales, Marketing, and Finance). > - However, it suffers from a **long time-to-value** вҖ” typically **18вҖ“36 months** before the first data mart is delivered. --- ### 2.2 Bottom-Up Design вҖ” Operational Steps (Kimball's Methodology) Kimball's **Data Warehouse Bus Architecture** reverses the order of delivery: 1. **Identify Business Processes** - Start with one business process (e.g., *Sales*, *Inventory*, *Procurement*, *Claims Processing*). 2. **Declare the Grain** - Choose the *atomic* grain of the fact table (e.g., *one row per sales line-item*). 3. **Identify & Conform the Dimensions** - Define the dimensions (Date, Product, Customer, Store) and ensure they are **conformed** (shared with identical keys and attributes across data marts). 4. **Identify the Facts (Numeric Measures)** - Add the additive numeric measures (e.g., Sales Amount, Quantity Sold, Discount). 5. **Build the Star Schema / Data Mart** - Implement the **fact table** at the centre, surrounded by **dimension tables**. 6. **Iterate & Integrate** - Build the next business process data mart. The **conformed dimensions** allow the data marts to be *automatically* integrated into an enterprise warehouse. 7. **Build the Enterprise Bus Matrix** - A matrix documenting which business processes share which conformed dimensions вҖ” this is the *architectural blueprint*. > [!NOTE] > **Why Bottom-Up (The "Why"):** > - **Fast time-to-value:** a single data mart can be delivered in **8вҖ“12 weeks**. > - **Business engagement:** end-users see results early and provide continuous feedback. > - **Conformed dimensions** are the *glue* that provides enterprise integration without a centralized EDW. > - Risk: *dimensional drift* (inconsistent dimension definitions across marts) and *scope creep*. --- ### 2.3 Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up вҖ” Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** This comparison table is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in either Part A or as a sub-part of Part B. | Parameter | Top-Down (Inmon) | Bottom-Up (Kimball) | |---|---|---| | **Starting Point** | Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) | Individual Data Mart | | **Schema Style** | Normalized (3NF) | Dimensional (Star / Snowflake) | | **Time-to-Value** | Very Long (18вҖ“36 months) | Short (8вҖ“12 weeks per mart) | | **Data Redundancy** | None (normalized) | Some (denormalized dims) | | **Skill Required** | High (ER modeling, 3NF, DBA) | Moderate (business + dimensional) | | **Best Suited For** | Large, regulated enterprises (banking, govt) | Mid-size, fast-moving businesses (retail, e-commerce) | | **Integration Approach** | Top-down propagation | Conformed dimensions | | **Maintenance Cost** | Lower (less redundancy) | Higher (must manage multiple marts) | | **CIF / Bus Matrix** | CIF (Corporate Information Factory) | Bus Matrix | | **Author** | Bill Inmon | Ralph Kimball | | **Risk** | Project may be cancelled before completion | Dimensional drift, scope creep | --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet вҖ” Fact Table Sizing & Granularity These formulas are the *mathematical backbone* of any sizing question in the ESE. > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Table Rule:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown. All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam safe. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Number of Rows in Fact Table** | $$ N_{rows} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert D_{i,keys} \vert \;\times\; \vert T \vert $$ | $\vert D_{i,keys} \vert$ = number of distinct keys in the $i^{th}$ dimension; $\vert T \vert$ = number of time periods | Estimate warehouse size | | 2 | **Storage Size (Bytes)** | $$ S_{fact} \;=\; N_{rows} \;\times\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} W_{j} $$ | $W_j$ = byte-width of the $j^{th}$ column; $m$ = total columns (FKs + measures) | Capacity planning | | 3 | **Total Storage (Fact + Dims)** | $$ S_{total} \;=\; S_{fact} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_{dim,i} $$ | $S_{dim,i}$ = size of $i^{th}$ dimension table | Full DW sizing | | 4 | **Granularity Refinement Factor** | $$ \rho \;=\; \frac{\vert T_{fine} \vert}{\vert T_{coarse} \vert} $$ | $\rho$ = how much finer the new grain is | Impact analysis when refining grain | | 5 | **Cardinality of Time Dimension** | $$ \vert T \vert \;=\; \Delta_{days} \;\times\; \delta_{period} $$ | $\Delta_{days}$ = days in range; $\delta_{period}$ = events per day at chosen grain | Daily / hourly / minutely grains | | 6 | **Additivity Property of a Measure** | A measure $M$ is **fully additive** if $\sum M$ is valid across *all* dimensions | Semi-additive (valid across some), Non-additive (ratios, percentages) | Choosing fact measures | | 7 | **Compression Ratio (typical DW)** | $$ C_{ratio} \;=\; \frac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}} $$ | Columnar compression: typically 5:1 to 10:1 | TCO estimation | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Top-Down (Inmon):** Used by the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)**, **NASA's Earth Observing System Data and Information System (EOSDIS)**, and large banks for **regulatory compliance, audit trails, and enterprise data governance**. - **Bottom-Up (Kimball):** Used by **Amazon Redshift customers, Walmart, Netflix** for **agile analytics, rapid A/B testing, and customer-facing dashboards**. - **Fact Table Granularity:** Directly impacts **query performance, storage cost, and analytical flexibility**. The finest grain (transaction-level) supports **arbitrary drill-downs**, **fraud detection** (per-transaction), and **ML feature engineering**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 вҖ” Choosing the Correct Granularity > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A retail chain wants to build a sales data mart. They sell **~5,000 distinct products** across **120 stores** to **~200,000 registered customers**. On average, the chain processes **30,000 sales line-items per day**. The warehouse must retain **5 years of data**. Compute the estimated number of rows in the fact table at *transaction grain* and the *total uncompressed storage* if each row occupies **80 bytes** on average. Assume the time dimension holds 5 years of daily data. **Given Data:** $$ \vert P \vert = 5{,}000 \quad \text{(products)} $$ $$ \vert S \vert = 120 \quad \text{(stores)} $$ $$ \vert C \vert = 200{,}000 \quad \text{(customers)} $$ $$ \delta = 30{,}000 \quad \text{(line-items per day)} $$ $$ \Delta = 5 \text{ years} \times 365 \text{ days} = 1{,}825 \text{ days} $$ $$ W_{row} = 80 \text{ bytes} $$ --- **Step 1 вҖ” Identify the atomic grain.** The atomic (finest) grain is: *"One row per sales line-item per day per product per store per customer."* > **Logic:** Since 30,000 line-items are *already aggregated at the day level* in the source POS system, the finest grain we can practically achieve is the **daily transaction grain**. --- **Step 2 вҖ” Compute the cardinality of the time dimension.** $$ \vert T \vert \;=\; \Delta_{days} \;=\; 1{,}825 $$ > **Logic:** The time dimension is indexed at the *day* level because the source granularity is daily. --- **Step 3 вҖ” Apply the fact-table row-count formula.** $$ N_{rows} \;=\; \vert T \vert \;\times\; \delta_{period} $$ $$ N_{rows} \;=\; 1{,}825 \times 30{,}000 $$ $$ N_{rows} \;=\; 54{,}750{,}000 \;\text{rows} $$ > **Logic:** Total rows = (number of days) Г— (average line-items per day). Note: The product/store/customer cardinalities ($\vert P \vert$, $\vert S \vert$, $\vert C \vert$) are the *upper bounds* of the dimension tables, not the fact-table row count driver. The fact-table row count is driven by **event volume**, not by dimension cardinality. --- **Step 4 вҖ” Compute uncompressed storage size.** $$ S_{fact} \;=\; N_{rows} \;\times\; W_{row} $$ $$ S_{fact} \;=\; 54{,}750{,}000 \times 80 $$ $$ S_{fact} \;=\; 4{,}380{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} $$ --- **Step 5 вҖ” Convert to human-readable units.** $$ S_{fact} \;=\; \frac{4{,}380{,}000{,}000}{1{,}024^{3}} \;\approx\; 4.08 \text{ GB uncompressed} $$ > **Logic:** Apply the binary-to-decimal conversion ($1 \text{ GB} = 1{,}024^3 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.074 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$). --- **Step 6 вҖ” Estimate compressed storage (Columnar, ~8:1).** $$ S_{compressed} \;=\; \frac{S_{fact}}{C_{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{4.08 \text{ GB}}{8} \;\approx\; 0.51 \text{ GB} $$ > **Logic:** Modern columnar DW engines (Redshift, Snowflake, BigQuery) routinely achieve 5:1вҖ“10:1 compression. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 вҖ” Granularity Refinement Impact > **Problem Statement:** The same retail chain now upgrades its POS to capture **per-minute** sales (60Г— finer). Recompute the row count and storage. **Step 1 вҖ” New time-grain cardinality.** $$ \vert T_{fine} \vert \;=\; 1{,}825 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ minutes} \;=\; 2{,}628{,}000 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Refinement factor.** $$ \rho \;=\; \frac{\vert T_{fine} \vert}{\vert T_{coarse} \vert} \;=\; \frac{2{,}628{,}000}{1{,}825} \;\approx\; 1{,}440 $$ > **Logic:** One day contains 1,440 minutes, so refining from daily to minute-level multiplies the time-cardinality by 1,440. **Step 3 вҖ” New row count.** $$ N_{rows,fine} \;=\; N_{rows,coarse} \;\times\; \rho \;\approx\; 5.475 \times 10^{7} \times 1{,}440 \;\approx\; 7.88 \times 10^{10} $$ **Step 4 вҖ” Verdict (for KTU answer):** > [!WARNING] > Refining granularity from *daily* to *per-minute* increases the fact-table size by **~1,440Г—**, which is **financially and operationally infeasible** for 5 years.
