The KTU answer must conclude: *"Always choose the **finest practical grain justified by business requirements**, not the finest theoretically possible."* --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” When to Use Which Approach | Scenario | Recommended Approach | Justification | |---|---|---| | National bank, 50 branches, strict RBI audit | **Top-Down** | Regulatory compliance, single source of truth, audit trail mandatory | | E-commerce startup, 10-person analytics team, 3-month runway | **Bottom-Up** | Fast ROI, conformed dims allow future integration | | Telecom operator building fraud-detection system | **Bottom-Up + Transaction Grain** | Need per-call/CDR-level grain for anomaly detection | | Government census data warehouse | **Top-Down + Snapshot Grain** | Enterprise scope, periodic (decadal) snapshots | | Hospital patient-journey analytics | **Bottom-Up + Accumulating Snapshot** | Lifecycle grain (Admit вҶ’ Treat вҶ’ Discharge вҶ’ Bill) | --- ### 3.4 Detailed Comparison вҖ” The Four Grains in One Synthesis > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Synthesis Block вҖ” write this in long-answer questions.** - **Transaction Grain** is the *most common* and *most flexible* вҖ” supports any drill-down. - **Periodic Snapshot Grain** is used when there is **no natural transaction** (e.g., account balance, inventory level on hand) but regular status is needed. - **Accumulating Snapshot Grain** is used for **processes with a well-defined pipeline and a finite number of milestones** (order fulfillment, insurance claims). - **Summary/Aggregate Grain** is **almost never stored** in a properly designed warehouse вҖ” it is computed on-the-fly via aggregate navigators or materialized views. Storing summaries wastes storage and creates update anomalies. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Top-Down Design Flow (Inmon) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Enterprise Requirement Analysis] --> B[Conceptual ER Modeling] B --> C[Normalized 3NF EDW Schema] C --> D[Physical EDW Implementation] D --> E[Derive Data Mart 1: Sales] D --> F[Derive Data Mart 2: Finance] D --> G[Derive Data Mart 3: HR] D --> H[Derive Data Mart 4: Inventory] E --> I[OLAP Reports and Dashboards] F --> I G --> I H --> I ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The EDW is the *root*; data marts are *leaves* of the tree. Information flows **downward**. --- ### 4.2 Bottom-Up Design Flow (Kimball Bus Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Business Process 1: Sales] --> M1[Data Mart 1] A2[Business Process 2: Inventory] --> M2[Data Mart 2] A3[Business Process 3: Procurement] --> M3[Data Mart 3] M1 --> CD{Conformed Dimensions: Date, Product, Customer, Store} M2 --> CD M3 --> CD CD --> BW[Enterprise Data Warehouse Bus] BW --> BI[Unified BI and Analytics] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Data marts are built **first**, then **integrated** through *conformed dimensions* into the *bus*. Information flows **upward and sideways**. --- ### 4.3 Fact Table Granularity Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Coarsest: Annual Aggregate] --> F2[Quarterly Snapshot] F2 --> F3[Monthly Snapshot] F3 --> F4[Daily Snapshot] F4 --> F5[Hourly Transaction] F5 --> F6[Per Event Transaction] F6 --> F7[Sub-Second Event] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** As we move right, granularity becomes *finer*. Storage cost and analytical flexibility both increase. The KTU sweet spot is **F4 or F5** for most business scenarios. --- ### 4.4 Star Schema вҖ” Anatomy of a Fact Table at Transaction Grain ```mermaid flowchart TD FT[("FACT_SALES
Grain: 1 row per line-item
Measures: Qty, Amount, Discount")] D1[("DIM_DATE
Key, Day, Month, Quarter, Year")] D2[("DIM_PRODUCT
Key, SKU, Name, Category, Brand")] D3[("DIM_STORE
Key, StoreID, City, State, Region")] D4[("DIM_CUSTOMER
Key, CustID, Segment, Demographics")] FT --> D1 FT --> D2 FT --> D3 FT --> D4 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The fact table sits at the **centre** (a single atomic grain). Dimension tables sit at the **points of the star** and are joined via foreign keys. This is the canonical Kimball star schema. --- ### 4.5 Kimball Four-Step Dimensional Design Process ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Select the Business Process] --> S2[Step 2: Declare the Grain] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify the Dimensions] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify the Facts] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Build the Star Schema] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** This is the **exact sequence** the KTU examiner expects when a question asks *"Explain Kimball's dimensional design process."* The grain **must** be declared in **Step 2**, *before* any measure is selected. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Inmon's Top-Down and Kimball's Bottom-Up data warehouse design approaches. List any three distinguishing features.** **Model Answer (3 Marks вҖ” 60вҖ“80 words):** | Feature | Top-Down (Inmon) | Bottom-Up (Kimball) | |---|---|---| | **Starting point** | Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) | Departmental Data Mart | | **Schema style** | Normalized (3NF) | Dimensional (Star / Snowflake) | | **Time-to-deliver** | Long (18вҖ“36 months) | Short (8вҖ“12 weeks) | **Marking Scheme:** - 1 mark for correct definition of each approach. - 1 mark for any three distinguishing features in tabular form. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]** **Define *fact table granularity* with a suitable example. Why is the choice of granularity considered the most critical design decision in dimensional modeling?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Fact table granularity* is the *atomic (lowest) level of detail* stored in a fact table. It defines what a single row represents вҖ” e.g., in a sales fact table, one row may represent *one sales line-item per day per product per store per customer*. **Criticality (1.5 marks):** 1. Once fixed, granularity is **immutable** вҖ” it cannot be refined without reloading. 2. It determines the **storage size** and **query performance** of the entire warehouse. 3. It governs the **types of analytical questions** the warehouse can answer (a coarse grain cannot answer fine-grained questions like fraud detection). --- ### Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ##### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022]** **Explain Inmon's Top-Down data warehouse design approach in detail. List its six operational phases and discuss two key advantages and two key limitations.** **Model Answer:** **1. Definition (1 Mark):** Inmon's Top-Down approach is an *enterprise-driven, normalized (3NF)* design methodology in which a **centralized Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)** is built first, and **subject-specific data marts** are subsequently derived from it. **2. The Six Operational Phases (3 Marks):** 1. **Enterprise Modeling:** Gather requirements from all business units; build the high-level *Corporate Information Factory (CIF)* model. 2. **Conceptual Data Modeling:** Develop a high-level ER diagram identifying the principal business entities (Customer, Product, Account, Transaction). 3. **Logical Data Modeling (3NF):** Apply 1NF, 2NF, 3NF to produce a *fully normalized* relational schema. This eliminates update, insert, and delete anomalies. 4. **Physical Design & Implementation:** Translate the logical model into a physical schema on a high-end RDBMS (Oracle, Teradata). 5. **Data Mart Derivation:** Build subject-specific data marts (Sales, Finance, HR) by *projecting* subsets of the EDW. These marts may be denormalized/star-schema for query speed. 6. **End-User Access Layer:** Deploy OLAP tools, dashboards, and ad-hoc query interfaces on top of the data marts. **3. Key Advantages (2 Marks):** - **Single source of truth:** Because all data marts derive from one EDW, the organization has one *integrated*, *consistent* view of every business entity. - **No data redundancy:** The 3NF schema eliminates duplication, reducing storage cost and update anomalies in the long term. - **Enterprise-wide consistency:** Cross-functional analytics (e.g., *customer profitability across sales and service*) is straightforward. **4. Key Limitations (1 Mark):** - **Long time-to-value:** A full EDW project may take 18вҖ“36 months; business users see no value until the first data mart is delivered. - **High upfront cost and risk:** A large project may be *cancelled mid-way* if early funding is withdrawn, leaving the enterprise with no usable artifact. - **Demands scarce skills:** Requires expert ER modelers, DBAs, and 3NF normalization specialists. --- ##### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2023]** **Define *fact table granularity*. Describe the three canonical granularities with one real-world example each. Why is the *finest practical* grain always recommended?** **Model Answer:** **1. Definition (1 Mark):** *Granularity* is the level of detail at which facts are recorded in a fact table вҖ” i.e., what a single row represents. It is the *atomic grain* of the warehouse. **2. The Three Canonical Granularities (4 Marks вҖ” 1.5 each + 0.5 for the table):** | Granularity | What a Row Represents | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Transaction** | A single business event at a point in time | One row per *line-item* of a retail invoice | | **Periodic Snapshot** | The state of a process at regular, predictable intervals | One row per *account per day* showing the end-of-day balance | | **Accumulating Snapshot** | The lifecycle of a process with multiple milestone dates | One row per *order*, updated as it progresses from *Order Placed* вҶ’ *Shipped* вҶ’ *Delivered* вҶ’ *Invoiced* | **3. Why Finest Practical Grain is Recommended (2 Marks):** - **Analytical Flexibility:** A fine-grained fact table can always be *rolled up* (aggregated) to answer coarser questions, but the reverse is impossible. - **Future-Proofing:** Business requirements evolve. A fine grain supports *unanticipated* future questions (e.g., fraud detection, ML feature engineering). - **Drill-Down Capability:** Enables users to navigate from a high-level KPI (e.g., *total sales = вӮ№10 Cr*) down to a single transaction (e.g., *invoice #1234, вӮ№500*). - **Storage and performance trade-off:** While fine grain increases storage, modern columnar compression (5:1вҖ“10:1) makes it economically viable. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - вқҢ **Common Mistake:** Writing "granularity is the number of rows in the fact table" вҖ” this is **wrong**. Granularity is *what each row represents*, not *how many* rows. > - вқҢ **Common Mistake:** Confusing *accumulating snapshot* with *periodic snapshot*. A periodic snapshot has *one timestamp*; an accumulating snapshot has *multiple milestone dates*. > - вқҢ **Common Mistake:** Saying "always store at the finest possible grain." The correct phrasing is "the **finest *practical* grain** justified by business requirements" вҖ” the qualifier *practical* is worth 1 mark. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice** ##### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** **Explain Kimball's Bottom-Up design methodology. Describe the *Kimball Bus Architecture* and the role of *conformed dimensions*.** **Model Answer:** **1. Definition (1 Mark):** Kimball's Bottom-Up approach is a *business-requirements-driven* methodology in which individual dimensional data marts are built first and integrated into an enterprise warehouse through *conformed dimensions*. **2. The Kimball Four-Step Process (2 Marks):** 1. **Select the Business Process** (e.g., Sales, Inventory, Claims). 2. **Declare the Grain** (e.g., *one row per sales line-item*). 3. **Identify the Dimensions** (Date, Product, Customer, Store). 4. **Identify the Facts** (Quantity, Sales Amount, Discount, Profit). **3. The Bus Architecture (2 Marks):** The *Data Warehouse Bus Architecture* is Kimball's blueprint for enterprise integration. It is documented as a **Bus Matrix** вҖ” a 2D grid where: - **Rows** = business processes (Sales, Inventory, Procurement). - **Columns** = conformed dimensions (Date, Product, Customer, Store). - **Cells** = marked when a process shares a conformed dimension with another process. **4. Role of Conformed Dimensions (2 Marks):** - A *conformed dimension* is a dimension table that is **shared across multiple data marts** with **identical keys, attribute names, and values**. - Example: The `DIM_DATE` table used by both the Sales mart and the Inventory mart has the *same* `date_key`, *same* `month_name`, *same* `fiscal_year`. This allows **cross-mart joins** without semantic conflict. - Conformed dimensions are the *glue* that makes the Bottom-Up approach *scale* to the enterprise level. --- ##### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** **A retail company records 50,000 sales transactions per day across 200 stores. Each transaction averages 3 line-items. The warehouse must hold 4 years of data. Compute (i) the total number of rows in the fact table, (ii) the uncompressed storage in GB, and (iii) the compressed storage assuming a 7:1 compression ratio. Assume each row is 64 bytes wide.** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Daily transactions: 50,000 - Line-items per transaction: 3 - Duration: 4 years = $4 \times 365 = 1{,}460$ days - Row width: 64 bytes - Compression: 7:1 --- **Step 1 вҖ” Daily line-item count.** $$ \delta_{daily} \;=\; 50{,}000 \times 3 \;=\; 150{,}000 \text{ line-items per day} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Total fact-table row count.** $$ N_{rows} \;=\; \delta_{daily} \times \Delta_{days} $$ $$ N_{rows} \;=\; 150{,}000 \times 1{,}460 $$ $$ N_{rows} \;=\; 219{,}000{,}000 \text{ rows} \;\approx\; 2.19 \times 10^{8} $$ **Step 3 вҖ” Uncompressed storage (Bytes).** $$ S_{uncomp} \;=\; N_{rows} \times W_{row} $$ $$ S_{uncomp} \;=\; 2.19 \times 10^{8} \times 64 $$ $$ S_{uncomp} \;=\; 1.4016 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4 вҖ” Convert to GB (Decimal: $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$ bytes).** $$ S_{uncomp} \;=\; \frac{1.4016 \times 10^{10}}{10^{9}} \;=\; 14.016 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 5 вҖ” Compressed storage (7:1).** $$ S_{comp} \;=\; \frac{S_{uncomp}}{C_{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{14.016}{7} $$ $$ S_{comp} \;=\; 2.00 \text{ GB (approximately)} $$ --- **Final Tabular Answer (for KTU board exam):** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Total rows in fact table | $N_{rows}$ | $2.19 \times 10^{8}$ | | Uncompressed size | $S_{uncomp}$ | $\approx 14.02$ GB | | Compressed size (7:1) | $S_{comp}$ | $\approx 2.00$ GB | **Marking Scheme:** - 2 marks for daily line-items calculation. - 2 marks for total row count. - 1 mark for uncompressed storage in bytes. - 1 mark for correct GB conversion. - 1 mark for compressed size and final answer. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - вқҢ Students often forget to **multiply by 3 (line-items per transaction)**, computing only 50,000 Г— 1,460 = 73 million rows (loses 2 marks). > - вқҢ Students confuse **GB = $10^9$** (decimal, used by storage vendors) with **GiB = $2^{30}$** (binary, used by OS). KTU accepts $10^9$ unless the question specifies otherwise. > - вқҢ Forgetting the **compression ratio** step loses the final 1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Read this the night before the exam.** - вҳҗ **Top-Down (Inmon):** *Enterprise вҶ’ EDW (3NF) вҶ’ Data Marts*. Time-to-value: 18вҖ“36 months. Best for regulated, large enterprises. - вҳҗ **Bottom-Up (Kimball):** *Data Marts вҶ’ Conformed Dimensions вҶ’ Bus Architecture*. Time-to-value: 8вҖ“12 weeks. Best for agile, fast-moving businesses. - вҳҗ **Granularity = what a single row of the fact table represents.** It is **immutable** and the **most important design decision**. - вҳҗ **Three canonical grains:** Transaction, Periodic Snapshot, Accumulating Snapshot. Know the **difference** between periodic and accumulating snapshot (periodic = one timestamp; accumulating = multiple milestone dates). - вҳҗ **Always choose the finest *practical* grain** вҖ” not the finest *theoretical* grain. Refining granularity is **irreversible** and **expensive**. - вҳҗ **Fact-table row count** = (event volume per period) Г— (number of periods). It is driven by **event volume**, not by dimension cardinality. - вҳҗ **Conformed dimensions** are the *architectural glue* of the Bottom-Up approach. Without them, data marts become isolated *silos*. - вҳҗ **Kimball's 4-step process:** Process вҶ’ Grain вҶ’ Dimensions вҶ’ Facts. The grain **must** be declared *before* facts. - вҳҗ **Compression ratio** of 5:1вҖ“10:1 is typical for columnar DW engines. Apply it to estimate *compressed* storage. - вҳҗ **Inmon's EDW is normalized (3NF); Kimball's data marts are denormalized (star schema).** This single sentence often earns 2 free marks. - вҳҗ **Bus Matrix** is the visual artifact that documents which business processes share which conformed dimensions. It is the *blueprint* of the enterprise warehouse. - вҳҗ **Hybrid approach:** In practice, many enterprises use a *hybrid* вҖ” Inmon's EDW backbone for governance + Kimball's data marts for agility. KTU accepts this as a valid 2-mark supplementary point in long answers.

topics.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slowly Changing Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)** are **dimension attributes** in a data warehouse whose values **change slowly over time** (unpredictably, rather than on a fixed schedule), but when they do change, the warehouse must decide **how to record and preserve the historical context** of the change.

According to **Ralph Kimball's Dimensional Modeling methodology** (the standard adopted in the KTU 2024 Data Warehousing syllabus), SCDs are classified into **multiple types** based on how the data warehouse reacts to changes in dimension attribute values during the **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** process.

### The Fundamental Problem Statement

In a data warehouse, a **Dimension Table** stores descriptive attributes of business entities (e.g., Customer, Product, Geography). A **Fact Table** stores measurable business events (sales, transactions) that reference these dimensions via **foreign keys**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Challenge**: When a customer's address changes, the analyst must choose whether to:
> 1. **Overwrite** the old value (losing history),
> 2. **Preserve** the old value as a new record (keeping history),
> 3. **Track** only the previous value (partial history), or
> 4. **Use a hybrid** approach combining multiple techniques.

The word **"Slowly"** refers to the fact that these changes happen **over weeks, months, or years** вҖ” not in real-time вҖ” as opposed to **Rapidly Changing Dimensions (RCDs)** that update continuously.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Library Membership Card" Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian maintaining a **physical register of members** (this is your dimension table).

| Scenario | What You Do | SCD Type |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| A member changes their phone number | You **erase** the old number and write the new one | **Type 1** (Overwrite) |
| A member **moves to a new city** | You **cancel the old card** and **issue a new card** with the new address, keeping the old card in archives | **Type 2** (Add New Row) |
| You want to know both the **old city** and **new city** for analytics | You **add a new column** "Previous City" and update it | **Type 3** (Add New Column) |
| You want full history **plus** current values in a single row | You create **multiple effective date columns** and/or **flags** | **Type 6** (Hybrid) |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway**: The SCD type you choose depends on the **business requirement** вҖ” *Do you need history, or do you only need the current state?*

---

## 1.3 Why SCDs Matter in Data Warehousing

| Metric / Standard | Industry Standard Value | Significance |
|-------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| Typical change rate of customer attributes | **10%вҖ“20% per year** | SCDs are the norm, not the exception |
| Recommended update window for ETL | **Off-peak hours (nightly batch)** | Changes are absorbed in batch loads |
| Fact-to-Dimension ratio in star schemas | **1:N (millions of facts per dimension)** | One dimension change can affect **millions of fact rows** if not properly handled |
| Acceptable history retention | **3вҖ“10 years** for most compliance regimes | SCD Type 2 is often legally required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: In Module 2, students are expected to understand **SCD Types 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6** with their **ETL implementation strategies**, **advantages**, **disadvantages**, and **real-world use cases**.

---

## 1.4 Position of SCDs in the ETL Pipeline

The SCD handling logic sits inside the **Load phase of ETL**, specifically within the **Staging вҶ’ Dimension** transformation:

1. **Extract** data from source OLTP systems.
2. **Transform** вҖ” Compare incoming dimension rows with existing warehouse dimensions.
3. **Load** вҖ” Apply the SCD rule to determine INSERT vs UPDATE behavior.

This decision is governed by the **SCD Strategy** configured for each dimension attribute.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Recognized SCD Types вҖ” Comprehensive Breakdown

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 0 вҖ” Passive Dimension (No Change Allowed)

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Dimension attributes are **never updated or changed** once loaded. |
| **Use Case** | Birth date, original country of origin, initial subscription date. |
| **History Tracking** | **Not applicable** вҖ” the value is considered immutable. |
| **Storage Overhead** | **None** |
| **Implementation** | Simply do not include the attribute in any UPDATE statement. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Type 0 is the **easiest to implement** but is rarely discussed in KTU exams beyond definition-level questions.

---

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 1 вҖ” Overwrite (No History)

**Operational Logic**:
1. The ETL process identifies the dimension key (e.g., `CustomerID`).
2. When a change is detected in a Type 1 attribute, the **old value is overwritten** in-place.
3. The dimension **key remains the same**; only attribute values change.
4. Historical fact rows now **silently point to the new value**.

**Step-by-Step Transformation Example**:

$$
\text{Initial State (Row 1):} \quad (\text{CustID}=101, \text{City}="Kochi", \text{Phone}="1111")
$$

$$
\text{After Update (Type 1):} \quad (\text{CustID}=101, \text{City}="Trivandrum", \text{Phone}="2222")
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Side Effect**: All historical sales facts that referenced `City="Kochi"` will now incorrectly report `City="Trivandrum"` in retrospective analysis. **History is permanently lost**.

**SQL Implementation Skeleton**:
```sql
-- Conceptual only вҖ” KTU 2024 does not mandate code in non-CS theory papers
UPDATE DIM_CUSTOMER
SET    CITY = :new_city,
       PHONE = :new_phone,
       UPDATED_AT = SYSDATE
WHERE  CUSTOMER_ID = :cust_id;
```

---

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 2 вҖ” Add New Row (Versioning / Full History)

**Operational Logic**:
1. Detect change in a Type 2 attribute.
2. **Do not modify** the existing row.
3. **Insert a new row** with the same natural/business key but a **new surrogate key**.
4. Mark the old row with `End_Date = TODAY` and `Is_Current = 'N'`.
5. Mark the new row with `Start_Date = TODAY`, `End_Date = '9999-12-31'`, `Is_Current = 'Y'`.

**Auxiliary Columns Required**:

| Column | Purpose | Data Type |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| `Surrogate_Key` | Unique synthetic primary key | `INTEGER / BIGINT` |
| `Natural_Key` | Business identifier (e.g., CustomerID) | `VARCHAR / INT` |
| `Start_Date` | Effective date of this version | `DATE` |
| `End_Date` | Expiration date of this version | `DATE` |
| `Is_Current_Flag` | Marks the active row | `CHAR(1)` or `BOOLEAN` |
| `Version_Number` | Optional sequential counter | `INTEGER` |

**Transformation Example**:

$$
\text{Row 1 (Old):} \quad (\text{SK}=1, \text{CustID}=101, \text{City}="Kochi", \text{Start}=2018-01-01, \text{End}=2024-06-15, \text{Current}=N)
$$

$$
\text{Row 2 (New):} \quad (\text{SK}=2, \text{CustID}=101, \text{City}="Trivandrum", \text{Start}=2024-06-15, \text{End}=9999-12-31, \text{Current}=Y)
$$

**Date Overlap Validation Constraint** (must be enforced):

$$
\forall \, r_i, r_j \text{ where } r_i.\text{NaturalKey} = r_j.\text{NaturalKey} \text{ and } r_i \neq r_j:
$$

$$
(r_i.\text{End\_Date} < r_j.\text{Start\_Date}) \lor (r_j.\text{End\_Date} < r_i.\text{Start\_Date})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The above constraint guarantees that no two version rows for the same business entity have overlapping effective date ranges** вҖ” a critical data integrity rule in SCD Type 2.

---

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 3 вҖ” Add New Column (Limited History / Previous Value)

**Operational Logic**:
1. Add **two columns** for each tracked attribute: `Current_Value` and `Previous_Value`.
2. On change, the old `Current_Value` shifts to `Previous_Value`, and the new value becomes `Current_Value`.
3. **Only one level of history is preserved**.

**Schema Layout**:

$$
\text{DIM\_CUSTOMER} = (\text{CustID}, \text{City\_Current}, \text{City\_Previous}, \text{Effective\_Date})
$$

**Example**:

$$
\text{Before Change:} \quad (\text{CustID}=101, \text{City\_Current}="Kochi", \text{City\_Previous}=NULL)
$$

$$
\text{After Change:} \quad (\text{CustID}=101, \text{City\_Current}="Trivandrum", \text{City\_Previous}="Kochi")
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation**: Only **one prior value** is retained. If the customer moves again, the Kochi value is **permanently lost**.

---

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 4 вҖ” History Table (Satellite / Mini-Dimension)

**Operational Logic**:
1. The **base dimension table** stores only the **current state** (similar to Type 1).
2. A **separate history table** stores all historical changes.
3. The two tables are linked by the **natural key**.

**Two-Table Schema**:

$$
\text{DIM\_CUSTOMER\_CURRENT} = (\text{SK}, \text{CustID}, \text{Name}, \text{City}, \text{...current attributes...})
$$

$$
\text{DIM\_CUSTOMER\_HISTORY} = (\text{HistoryID}, \text{CustID}, \text{Attribute\_Name}, \text{Old\_Value}, \text{New\_Value}, \text{Change\_Date})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy**: Think of the base table as a **whiteboard** (current info) and the history table as a **diary** (audit log of every change).

---

### рҹ”№ SCD Type 6 вҖ” Hybrid (1 + 2 + 3 Combined)

**Operational Logic** вҖ” combines **three techniques**:
1. **Type 1 behavior** on the `Current_Value` column (always shows the latest value).
2. **Type 2 behavior** with surrogate keys and effective dates (preserves full history).
3. **Type 3 behavior** with a `Previous_Value` column (easy "what changed?" queries).

**Schema**:

$$
\text{DIM\_CUSTOMER\_TYPE6} = (\text{SK}, \text{CustID}, \text{City\_Current}, \text{City\_Previous}, \text{Start\_Date}, \text{End\_Date}, \text{Is\_Current}, \text{Version})
$$

**Type 6 Query Pattern** (reconstructing history at any point in time):

$$
\text{For facts at time } t, \text{ join to the SCD version where } t \in [\text{Start\_Date}, \text{End\_Date}]
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Decision Matrix

### 2.2.1 Storage Cost Formula

$$
\text{Total\_Storage} = N_{\text{rows}} \times \left( S_{\text{fixed}} + S_{\text{history}} \times N_{\text{versions}} \right)
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $N_{\text{rows}}$ | Number of distinct dimension members | rows |
| $S_{\text{fixed}}$ | Fixed metadata per row | bytes |
| $S_{\text{history}}$ | Additional storage per historical version | bytes |
| $N_{\text{versions}}$ | Average number of versions per member | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: SCD Type 2 has the **highest storage cost** because $N_{\text{versions}}$ grows linearly with time.

---

### 2.2.2 Master Decision Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| SCD Type | History Preserved | Storage Cost | Query Complexity | Best For |
|:--------:|:-----------------:|:------------:|:----------------:|:---------|
| **Type 0** | None (immutable) | **Lowest** | **Lowest** | Immutable attributes (DOB) |
| **Type 1** | None | **Low** | **Low** | Correctable data (typos, phone) |
| **Type 2** | **Full** | **Highest** | **Medium** | Customer, Product, Geography migrations |
| **Type 3** | **One prior value** | **Low** | **Low** | Comparing old vs. new (limited) |
| **Type 4** | **Full (in separate table)** | **Medium** | **Medium** | Audit-heavy environments |
| **Type 6** | **Full + denormalized** | **Highest** | **Highest** | Mixed analytical needs |

---

### 2.2.3 ETL Row Count Formulas

For a dimension with $N$ natural keys and an average of $V$ versions per key:

$$
\text{Rows}_{\text{Type 1}} = N
$$

$$
\text{Rows}_{\text{Type 2}} = N \times V
$$

$$
\text{Rows}_{\text{Type 4}} = N + (N \times (V - 1)) = N \times V
$$

$$
\text{Growth\_Ratio}_{\text{Type2 vs Type1}} = V
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Reality**: SCD Type 2 is the **most frequently asked** SCD type. Always remember: **"Add a new row, do not update."**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | SCD Type Used | Attribute Tracked | Reason |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Banking & Finance** | **Type 2** | Customer address, marital status, account tier | Regulatory compliance (RBI, SEBI) |
| **E-Commerce** | **Type 1** | Phone number, email | Minor corrections, no history needed |
| **Healthcare** | **Type 2** | Patient diagnosis, insurance provider | Clinical history preservation |
| **Telecom** | **Type 6** | Plan subscription, tariff | Both current state and full history needed |
| **Retail (Supply Chain)** | **Type 2** | Product category, supplier | Vendor migrations and rebranding |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Pattern**: *"A bank wants to track all address changes of customers for the past 5 years for audit. Recommend the SCD type with justification."* вҖ” The answer is **SCD Type 2**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: SCD Type 2 Implementation on `EMPLOYEE` Dimension

### Problem Statement

A company maintains an `EMPLOYEE` dimension. The following source record arrives via ETL on **2024-07-01**:

$$
(\text{EmpID}=501, \text{Name}="Anjali", \text{Department}="Finance", \text{Salary}=50000, \text{Location}="Kochi"})
$$

On **2025-01-15**, the same employee (EmpID 501) is transferred to the **HR** department in **Trivandrum**. Apply **SCD Type 2** and show the resulting dimension table.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the natural key.**

$$
\text{Natural Key} = \text{EmpID} = 501
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Check existing rows for this natural key.**

Initial dimension state (pre-2024-07-01):

| Surrogate_Key (SK) | EmpID | Department | Location | Salary | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:------------------:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------:|:------:|:----------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 1001 | 501 | Finance | Kochi | 50000 | 2020-03-01 | 9999-12-31 | Y |

**Step 3 вҖ” Compare the incoming record with the existing `Is_Current = 'Y'` row.**

Since both `Department` and `Location` have changed, SCD Type 2 is triggered.

**Step 4 вҖ” Expire the old row.**

$$
\text{Update Row 1001:} \quad \text{End\_Date} = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}15, \quad \text{Is\_Current} = N
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Insert a new row with a fresh surrogate key.**

$$
\text{New Row SK}=1002, \quad \text{Start\_Date}=2025\text{-}01\text{-}15, \quad \text{End\_Date}=9999\text{-}12\text{-}31, \quad \text{Is\_Current}=Y
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Final Dimension State.**

| SK | EmpID | Department | Location | Salary | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:--:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------:|:------:|:----------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 1001 | 501 | Finance | Kochi | 50000 | 2020-03-01 | 2025-01-15 | N |
| 1002 | 501 | HR | Trivandrum | 50000 | 2025-01-15 | 9999-12-31 | Y |

**Step 7 вҖ” Verify the date-overlap constraint.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1001 range:} & \quad [2020\text{-}03\text{-}01, \; 2025\text{-}01\text{-}15) \\
\text{Row 1002 range:} & \quad [2025\text{-}01\text{-}15, \; 9999\text{-}12\text{-}31) \\
\text{Overlap check:} & \quad 2025\text{-}01\text{-}15 \notin [2020\text{-}03\text{-}01, \; 2025\text{-}01\text{-}15) \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **End_Date is exclusive** in the old range and **Start_Date is inclusive** in the new range. This boundary convention prevents double-counting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: SCD Type 3 вҖ” Limited History Tracking

### Problem Statement

A retail company tracks product `Price`. Apply **SCD Type 3** to record the **current and previous price** for each product.

### Solution

**Initial State**:

| ProductID | Price_Current | Price_Previous | Effective_Date |
|:---------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|
| P001 | 100 | NULL | 2024-01-01 |

**Change Event**: On 2024-09-01, price increases to 120.

**After Update (Type 3)**:

| ProductID | Price_Current | Price_Previous | Effective_Date |
|:---------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|
| P001 | 120 | 100 | 2024-09-01 |

**Mathematical Representation of the Shift Operation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Price\_Previous}_{\text{new}} & = \text{Price\_Current}_{\text{old}} \\
\text{Price\_Current}_{\text{new}} & = \text{Price\_Current}_{\text{old}} + \Delta P
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\Delta P$ is the price delta (here, $\Delta P = +20$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: If the price changes a **third time**, the original price of 100 is **permanently overwritten**. Type 3 supports **only one level of history**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Computing Storage Growth for SCD Type 2

### Problem Statement

A `CUSTOMER` dimension has $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ distinct customers. On average, each customer has $V = 1.4$ versions (i.e., 40% of customers have changed address once). Each row requires:

- Fixed metadata: $S_{\text{fixed}} = 200$ bytes
- Per-version overhead (dates, flags): $S_{\text{history}} = 50$ bytes

Calculate the total storage for SCD Type 1 vs SCD Type 2.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 вҖ” SCD Type 1 Storage** (no history, one row per customer):

$$
\text{Storage}_{\text{Type1}} = N \times S_{\text{fixed}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 200 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Storage}_{\text{Type1}} = 200{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 190.7 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” SCD Type 2 Storage** (multiple versions per customer):

$$
\text{Storage}_{\text{Type2}} = N \times V \times (S_{\text{fixed}} + S_{\text{history}})
$$

$$
\text{Storage}_{\text{Type2}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1.4 \times (200 + 50) \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Storage}_{\text{Type2}} = 1{,}400{,}000 \times 250 \text{ bytes} = 350{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 333.8 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Growth Ratio**:

$$
\text{Growth Ratio} = \frac{\text{Storage}_{\text{Type2}}}{\text{Storage}_{\text{Type1}}} = \frac{350{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000{,}000} = 1.75
$$

**Conclusion**: SCD Type 2 consumes **75% more storage** than Type 1 in this scenario.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight**: Use this exact formula structure in numerical questions worth 7вҖ“14 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” When to Use Which SCD Type

| Business Scenario | Recommended SCD | Justification |
|-------------------|:---------------:|---------------|
| Customer changes spelling of name (typo fix) | **Type 1** | No historical value in preserving the typo |
| Employee gets promoted (Department change) | **Type 2** | Need to know prior department for performance history |
| Product price changes weekly | **Type 3** | Compare current vs. immediate-prior price |
| Compliance audit requiring 7-year log | **Type 4** | Separation of operational and historical data |
| Mixed: need history + current view in reports | **Type 6** | Combine benefits of all three |
| Date of birth of an employee | **Type 0** | Immutable attribute, never changes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 вҖ” SCD Type 2 Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source OLTP System] -->|Extract| B[Staging Area]
    B -->|Transform Compare| C{Attribute Changed?}
    C -->|No Change| D[Skip No Update Needed]
    C -->|Yes Change Detected| E[Expire Old Row Set End Date and Is Current to N]
    E --> F[Insert New Row Fresh Surrogate Key]
    F --> G[Set New Row Start Date Today and Is Current to Y]
    G --> H[Dimension Table Updated]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Fact Tables Auto Reference via FK]
    I --> J[Analytical Reports and Dashboards]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart illustrates the **decision logic** of an SCD Type 2 ETL job. The **fact tables do not need modification** вҖ” they continue to reference the appropriate surrogate key based on the transaction date.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 вҖ” Comparative Architecture of SCD Types 1, 2, 3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TYPE1[SCD Type 1 Overwrite]
        T1A[Row 101 City Kochi] -->|Update| T1B[Row 101 City Trivandrum]
    end

    subgraph TYPE2[SCD Type 2 Versioning]
        T2A[Row 1 SK 1 City Kochi Current Y] -->|Expire and Insert| T2B[Row 1 SK 2 City Trivandrum Current Y]
        T2A --> T2C[Row 1 SK 1 City Kochi Current N Archived]
    end

    subgraph TYPE3[SCD Type 3 Previous Column]
        T3A[Row 101 Current Kochi Previous NULL] -->|Shift| T3B[Row 101 Current Trivandrum Previous Kochi]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 вҖ” Block-Level Functional Architecture of SCD-Enabled Data Warehouse

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SOURCE_LAYER[OLTP Source Systems]
        S1[CRM Database]
        S2[ERP System]
        S3[Legacy Mainframe]
    end

    subgraph ETL_LAYER[ETL Engine with SCD Logic]
        E1[Extraction Module]
        E2[Staging Tables]
        E3[SCD Processor]
        E3A[SCD Type 1 Handler]
        E3B[SCD Type 2 Handler]
        E3C[SCD Type 3 Handler]
        E3D[SCD Type 6 Handler]
    end

    subgraph DW_LAYER[Data Warehouse]
        D1[Dimension Table Current]
        D2[Dimension Table History Type 2]
        D3[History Satellite Table Type 4]
        D4[Fact Table Sales]
        D5[Fact Table Inventory]
    end

    subgraph CONSUMPTION[BI and Analytics Layer]
        C1[OLAP Cubes]
        C2[Ad Hoc SQL Reports]
        C3[Dashboard Tools]
    end

    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> E1
    S3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E3A
    E3 --> E3B
    E3 --> E3C
    E3 --> E3D
    E3A --> D1
    E3B --> D2
    E3C --> D1
    E3D --> D1
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D1 --> D5
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> C1
    D5 --> C1
    D4 --> C2
    D4 --> C3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block Diagram Reading Guide**: Arrows indicate **data flow direction** during the nightly ETL batch. The SCD Processor is the **central decision engine** that classifies each dimension attribute and dispatches it to the appropriate handler.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 вҖ” Temporal Query Pattern with SCD Type 2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fact Table Sales] -->|Foreign Key SK| B[Join on Date Range]
    B --> C[Dimension Customer Type 2]
    C -->|Filter Start Date to End Date covers transaction date| D[Result Correct Historical Snapshot]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal Join Logic**: To retrieve the dimension values **as they were at the time of the fact**, the join condition is:
>
> $$ \text{Fact\_Date} \in [\text{Dim.Start\_Date}, \, \text{Dim.End\_Date}) $$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]

> **Q: Define Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD). List any three types of SCDs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark)**: Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) are dimension attributes in a data warehouse whose values change slowly and unpredictably over time, requiring deliberate ETL strategies to manage historical context.
>
> **Three Types (2 marks)**:
> 1. **SCD Type 1** вҖ” Overwrite the old value; no history preserved.
> 2. **SCD Type 2** вҖ” Add a new row with a new surrogate key; full history preserved using effective dates and flags.
> 3. **SCD Type 3** вҖ” Add a new column to store the previous value; only one level of history maintained.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024]

> **Q: What is the role of a surrogate key in SCD Type 2?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **surrogate key** is a **system-generated, artificial primary key** (typically an auto-increment integer) assigned to each version row of a dimension. Its roles in SCD Type 2 are:
>
> 1. **Uniquely identifies each historical version** of a dimension member (1 mark).
> 2. **Decouples the warehouse from source-system keys**, allowing the natural key to repeat across versions (1 mark).
> 3. **Enables fact tables to point to the correct version** via foreign key joins using date-range logic (1 mark).
>
> **Example**: `Surrogate_Key = 1001, 1002` for two versions of `CustomerID = 501`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]

> **Q (a)** Explain **SCD Type 2** in detail. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A `PRODUCT` dimension table has the following initial record: `(ProductID = 901, ProductName = "Laptop", Category = "Electronics", Price = 60000)`. On `2025-03-10`, the product is reclassified to category `"Computing"` and price changes to `58000`. Apply **SCD Type 2** and show the updated dimension table with proper surrogate keys, effective dates, and current flags. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) SCD Type 2 вҖ” Detailed Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark)**: SCD Type 2 is a dimensional modeling technique that preserves **complete historical changes** of dimension attributes by inserting a **new row** for each change, rather than overwriting existing rows.

**Mechanism (2 marks)**:
- Each version row is identified by a **surrogate key** (SK).
- **Natural/business key** (e.g., ProductID) repeats across versions.
- The currently active row has `Is_Current = 'Y'` and `End_Date = '9999-12-31'`.
- Historical rows have `Is_Current = 'N'` and a finite `End_Date`.

**Advantages (2 marks)**:
1. **Full historical traceability** вҖ” every change is preserved.
2. **Audit and compliance-friendly** вҖ” essential for banking, healthcare, and regulatory domains.
3. **Point-in-time analysis** вҖ” reports can reconstruct the dimension state at any past date.
4. **No loss of analytical granularity**.

**Disadvantages (2 marks)**:
1. **Storage overhead** вҖ” table grows with each change.
2. **Query complexity** вҖ” joins must use date-range conditions.
3. **Slower ETL** вҖ” requires additional UPDATE + INSERT operations.
4. **Fact tables grow** if a snowflake approach is used (rare).

---

**(b) Worked Example (7 Marks)**

**Initial State (Step 1 вҖ” 1 mark)**:

| SK | ProductID | ProductName | Category | Price | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:--:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 5001 | 901 | Laptop | Electronics | 60000 | 2023-01-01 | 9999-12-31 | Y |

**Step 2 вҖ” Detect Change (1 mark)**: Category changed from `Electronics` вҶ’ `Computing`; Price changed from `60000` вҶ’ `58000`.

**Step 3 вҖ” Expire Old Row (2 marks)**: Update `SK = 5001`:

| SK | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:--:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 5001 | 2025-03-10 | N |

**Step 4 вҖ” Insert New Row (2 marks)**: New surrogate key `SK = 5002`:

| SK | ProductID | ProductName | Category | Price | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:--:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 5002 | 901 | Laptop | Computing | 58000 | 2025-03-10 | 9999-12-31 | Y |

**Final Dimension Table (1 mark)**:

| SK | ProductID | ProductName | Category | Price | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current |
|:--:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 5001 | 901 | Laptop | Electronics | 60000 | 2023-01-01 | 2025-03-10 | N |
| 5002 | 901 | Laptop | Computing | 58000 | 2025-03-10 | 9999-12-31 | Y |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024]

> **Q (a)** Compare **SCD Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3** with a suitable example for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Explain **SCD Type 4 and Type 6**. Under what business conditions would you recommend each? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of SCD Types 1, 2, 3 (7 Marks)**

| Feature | SCD Type 1 | SCD Type 2 | SCD Type 3 |
|:--------|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| **Mechanism** | Overwrite old value | Add new row with new SK | Add new column for previous value |
| **History** | None | Full history | One previous value only |
| **Storage** | Low | Highest | Low |
| **Example Attribute** | Phone number | Customer address | Product price |
| **Use Case** | Typo correction | Employee promotion | Price comparison |
| **Query Complexity** | Simple | Moderate (date-range join) | Simple |

**Example for Type 1 (1 mark)**: Customer phone number changes from `"9876543210"` to `"9123456789"` вҖ” old value is **overwritten in place**; no history retained.

**Example for Type 2 (1 mark)**: Customer `CustID = 201` moves from `"Kochi"` to `"Delhi"`. A new row is inserted with a fresh surrogate key; old row is expired with `End_Date` and `Is_Current = N`.

**Example for Type 3 (1 mark)**: Product `P001` price changes from `100` to `120`. The `Price_Previous` column is updated to `100`, and `Price_Current` becomes `120`.

**Distinguishing Summary (1 mark)**: Type 1 prioritizes **simplicity**, Type 2 prioritizes **history**, Type 3 prioritizes **comparative analytics with limited history**.

---

**(b) SCD Type 4 and Type 6 (7 Marks)**

**SCD Type 4 вҖ” History Table (3 marks)**:

- The **base dimension** holds only the **current state** (Type 1 style).
- A **separate history table** records every change as an audit trail.
- **Schema**: `DIM_CUSTOMER_CURRENT (SK, CustID, Name, City, ...)` and `DIM_CUSTOMER_HIST (HistID, CustID, AttrName, OldValue, NewValue, ChangeDate)`.
- **Business Use Case**: **Regulatory audit environments** (e.g., pharmaceutical, financial compliance) where full change logs are mandatory and must be **separated** from operational reporting tables.
- **Advantage**: Clean current table for fast queries; comprehensive history preserved.
- **Disadvantage**: Requires JOINs for historical analysis.

**SCD Type 6 вҖ” Hybrid (1+2+3) (3 marks)**:

- Combines **Type 1** (current value column always latest), **Type 2** (surrogate key + effective dates), and **Type 3** (previous value column).
- **Schema**: `(SK, Natural_Key, Attr_Current, Attr_Previous, Start_Date, End_Date, Is_Current, Version)`.
- **Business Use Case**: **Telecom and retail** companies that need both **point-in-time historical reporting** (Type 2) and **quick "what is the previous value?"** lookups (Type 3) within the **same row** for fast dashboard queries.
- **Advantage**: Best of all three worlds; single-row access to history.
- **Disadvantage**: Most complex to maintain; potential data integrity issues if ETL is not rigorous.

**Decision Rule (1 mark)**:

$$
\text{Choose Type 4 if Audit is the dominant need.}
$$

$$
\text{Choose Type 6 if Mixed analytical access is the dominant need.}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU SCD Questions**:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to expire the old row in SCD Type 2** вҖ” many students only INSERT a new row and forget to UPDATE the old row's `End_Date` and `Is_Current` flag. This results in **two active rows** for the same natural key, violating the integrity constraint. **Deduction: 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Surrogate Key with Natural Key** вҖ” the surrogate key is **generated by the warehouse** (e.g., `1001, 1002`); the natural key comes from the source system and **repeats across versions**. Mixing these up loses 1вҖ“2 marks.
>
> 3. **Date overlap errors** вҖ” students often set `End_Date = Start_Date` of the next row **on the same date**, causing ambiguity. The convention is **End_Date is exclusive, Start_Date is inclusive**.
>
> 4. **Recommending SCD Type 1 when history is needed** вҖ” this is a classic conceptual error. Always check the business requirement: *"Does the user need history?"* If yes, **Type 2 is almost always correct**.
>
> 5. **Writing `NULL` for previous value in SCD Type 3** without explaining **why it is initially NULL** вҖ” you must state that the first record has no prior value, hence the **initial NULL is expected**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” SCD (Module 2)**:
>
> - **SCD = Slowly Changing Dimensions** вҖ” dimension attributes that change slowly and unpredictably.
> - **Type 0** вҶ’ immutable attributes (Date of Birth). **Never updates.**
> - **Type 1** вҶ’ **Overwrite** in place. No history. Lowest storage.
> - **Type 2** вҶ’ **Insert new row**, expire old row with `End_Date` and `Is_Current = N`. **Full history preserved.** Uses **Surrogate Keys**.
> - **Type 3** вҶ’ **Add a column** for previous value. **Only one prior value retained**.
> - **Type 4** вҶ’ **Separate history table** linked to a current-state dimension. Best for **audit/compliance**.
> - **Type 6** вҶ’ **Hybrid (1+2+3)** with `Current`, `Previous`, surrogate key, and effective dates. Best for **mixed reporting needs**.
> - **Surrogate Key** = warehouse-generated PK; **Natural Key** = business identifier from source.
> - **Storage Growth Formula** for Type 2: $\text{Storage} = N \times V \times (S_{\text{fixed}} + S_{\text{history}})$.
> - **Date-Range Join** is the standard query pattern for Type 2: $\text{FactDate} \in [\text{Start\_Date}, \text{End\_Date})$.
> - **End_Date is exclusive**, **Start_Date is inclusive** вҖ” never overlap.
> - **Most frequently asked SCD in KTU** = **Type 2** вҖ” master its mechanics, schema, advantages, and disadvantages.
> - **Real-world decision rule**: Choose Type 1 for **typos/corrections**; Type 2 for **migrations/promotions**; Type 3 for **immediate-prior comparisons**; Type 4 for **regulatory audit**; Type 6 for **dashboard + historical analytics** in one row.
> - **ETL Position**: SCD logic operates in the **Load phase**, comparing staging rows with existing dimension rows.
> - **Star schema fact tables are unaffected** by SCD changes вҖ” they simply hold the **surrogate key** that was valid at the time of the transaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advances in Dimensional Modeling вҖ“ Aggregates & Roll-Up, Time Dimension, Junk Dimensions, Degenerate Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advances in Dimensional Modeling вҖ“ Aggregates & Roll-Up, Time Dimension, Junk Dimensions, Degenerate Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ MODULE 2 вҖ” ADVANCES IN DIMENSIONAL MODELING

Welcome to one of the most **exam-favourite** corners of the KTU Data Warehousing syllabus. Dimensional modeling (popularized by **Ralph Kimball**) revolves around separating numeric *measurements* (facts) from *descriptive context* (dimensions). But real-world business scenarios are messy вҖ” invoices have weird flags, transactions don't follow a calendar perfectly, and management demands *instant* summary reports.

This module covers the **four advanced techniques** used by data warehouse designers to handle these realities:
**Aggregates & Roll-Up**, **Time Dimension**, **Junk Dimensions**, and **Degenerate Dimensions**.

---

## 1.1 вҖ” AGGREGATES & ROLL-UP

### рҹ“Ң Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus)
An **Aggregate** in a data warehouse is a pre-computed summary table that stores data rolled up to a higher level of granularity (e.g., monthly totals instead of daily transactions). **Roll-Up** is the navigational/analytical operation (or design technique) that moves from detailed (atomic) fact data to summarized data вҖ” typically traversing the dimension hierarchy from a lower level (day) to a higher level (month вҶ’ quarter вҶ’ year).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll-Up** can mean TWO things depending on context:
> 1. **Design-time concept** вҶ’ Pre-built aggregate fact tables (physical summary tables).
> 2. **Query-time concept** вҶ’ Aggregation operation (SUM, AVG) performed by SQL GROUP BY or OLAP tools (e.g., going from City вҶ’ State вҶ’ Country).

### рҹҢҶ Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you run a coffee chain with 5,000 daily transactions across 50 outlets. Every morning your manager wants to know the **city-wide monthly sales**. Instead of scanning 150,000 receipts every time, you maintain a small **"summary notebook"** with just 12 rows per city per year (144 cells). The notebook = **aggregate table**. The act of flipping from the detailed transaction log to the summary notebook = **roll-up**.

**Key concept вҖ” the Aggregation Function (for numeric facts):**
$$
\text{RollUp}(m) = \bigotimes_{r \in R} \; \mathbf{Agg}(f_r)
$$
where $m$ is the measure, $R$ is the row set within the new group, and $\mathbf{Agg} \in \{ \text{SUM}, \text{AVG}, \text{MIN}, \text{MAX}, \text{COUNT} \}$. In KTU exams, **SUM** is the most common aggregation.

### Why Aggregates?
- **Query performance** вҖ” Pre-calculated summaries return in milliseconds vs. scanning billions of rows.
- **Storage cost trade-off** вҖ” Aggregates are small; they cost more storage but save massive compute.
- **Drill-down readiness** вҖ” The aggregator tool (or BI layer) automatically picks aggregate vs. atomic table based on query granularity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity Warning**: Don't build an aggregate over a *sparse* combination of dimensions. Example: aggregating (Product Г— Day Г— Customer) is dense; aggregating (Product Г— Day Г— Store Г— Promotion Г— Weather) may yield mostly NULL cells вҖ” **useless aggregate**.

---

## 1.2 вҖ” TIME DIMENSION

### рҹ“Ң Formal Definition
The **Time Dimension** (also called *Date Dimension*) is a dedicated dimension table that captures every business-relevant temporal attribute of a fact: day, week, month, quarter, fiscal year, holiday flag, working day flag, season, etc. It is virtually **mandatory** in every star/snowflake schema because fact tables need a foreign key to interpret "when" the event occurred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fact table is useless without a Time Dimension. **Every fact row must carry a date foreign key** вҖ” even real-time streaming events.

### рҹ“… Conceptual Analogy
Think of the Time Dimension as the **calendar on your office wall** вҖ” but a *smart* one. It doesn't just say "12th March"; it also tells you "12th March 2024 is a Tuesday, falls in Week 11, Q1 of Fiscal Year 2023-24, and is a working day after Holi festival in Kerala". This calendar is then *linked* to every sale, every login, every shipment вҖ” letting you ask "How did *Week 11 sales in Q1 of FY24 on working days* compare to the same week last year?".

### Key Attributes of a Time Dimension
| Category | Example Attributes |
|---|---|
| **Day-level** | `date_key`, `full_date`, `day_of_week`, `day_num_in_month`, `day_num_in_year` |
| **Calendar hierarchy** | `week_num`, `month_name`, `month_num`, `quarter`, `calendar_year` |
| **Fiscal hierarchy** | `fiscal_week`, `fiscal_month`, `fiscal_quarter`, `fiscal_year` |
| **Business flags** | `is_holiday`, `is_working_day`, `is_weekend`, `is_payday` |
| **Relative metrics** | `days_since_today`, `days_to_year_end`, `same_day_last_year` |

---

## 1.3 вҖ” JUNK DIMENSIONS

### рҹ“Ң Formal Definition
A **Junk Dimension** is a consolidation of *low-cardinality flags and indicators* (e.g., payment status, order type, gender, yes/no flags, transaction channel) into a **single dimension table**, instead of cluttering the fact table with multiple tiny foreign keys or sparse boolean columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardinality rule of thumb**: If an attribute has fewer than ~50 distinct values and is not part of an existing dimension, pack it into a Junk Dimension.

### рҹ—ӮпёҸ Conceptual Analogy
Imagine your billing system generates an invoice with **six tiny checkboxes**: Paid/Unpaid, Online/Cash, Domestic/International, New/Returning, GST/PAN, Gift Card/Credit Card/Promo. If you keep them as 6 separate columns in the fact table, the table becomes wide and many analytics queries become awkward. Instead, build a **single lookup table** with 64 rows (2Г—2Г—2Г—2Г—2Г—8) where each row represents a unique *combination* of these flags. The fact table now has **one** foreign key pointing into this combined "miscellaneous" table вҖ” that's the Junk Dimension.

---

## 1.4 вҖ” DEGENERATE DIMENSIONS

### рҹ“Ң Formal Definition
A **Degenerate Dimension** is a dimension attribute (typically a transaction or document number such as *Invoice Number, Order ID, Ticket Number, Transaction ID*) that exists **in the fact table itself** without being joined to a separate dimension table вҖ” because it carries no additional descriptive context beyond being a unique identifier of the transaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The name "**degenerate**" comes from the fact that this dimension *de-generates* (loses its dimensional nature) and becomes a column physically residing in the fact table. It is **not** a key to any other table.

### рҹ§ҫ Conceptual Analogy
When you buy groceries, the bill itself has a **Bill Number** printed on it. That bill number is *the* identifier of the transaction. There is no separate "Bill Details" table that describes that bill number further вҖ” the bill *is* the transaction. The bill number is **born and dies inside the fact row**, so it lives there. That's a degenerate dimension.

### Comparison Snapshot (Frequently Tested!)
| Property | Degenerate Dimension | Junk Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardinality** | Very HIGH (one per row) | Very LOW (вүӨ ~100) |
| **Purpose** | Transaction/document identifier | Collection of small flags |
| **Stored in** | Fact table column | Separate dimension table |
| **Joined to dimension?** | No | Yes, via foreign key |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Iron Rule of Degenerate Dimensions**: If the dimension has *zero* descriptive attributes besides the ID, and the ID is unique per row, it is degenerate. If it has descriptive context, model it as a proper dimension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# рҹ“— DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 вҖ” AGGREGATES & ROLL-UP: DEEP DIVE

### The Aggregation Pipeline (How Roll-Up Actually Works)
1. **Atomic Fact Table** stores data at the lowest grain (e.g., one row per line item per transaction).
2. **Identify query patterns** вҖ” which summaries are queried repeatedly? (e.g., monthly sales by region).
3. **Build aggregate fact table** with reduced dimensionality and pre-summed measures.
4. **BI/Olap tool** transparently routes the user's query to either atomic or aggregate table based on grouping level.
5. **Drill-down** allows navigating *back* from aggregate to atomic.

### The Cardinal Rule: Consistency
Aggregates MUST be **fully consistent** with the atomic table. If your atomic table says total sales for Q1 = вӮ№10,00,000, then the sum of the three monthly aggregate rows for Q1 must *also* equal вӮ№10,00,000 вҖ” exactly, down to the paisa.

$$
\text{Total}(\text{Atomic}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Total}(\text{Aggregate}_i)
$$

where $n$ is the number of aggregate partitions combined.

### Aggregation Function Reference (High-Yield!)
| Function | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **SUM** | Additive measures (sales, quantity) | Total sales per month |
| **COUNT** | Counting transactions/rows | Number of orders per day |
| **MIN / MAX** | Performance envelopes | Fastest delivery time |
| **AVG** | Semi-additive (price, rating) | Average order value |
| **DISTINCT COUNT** | Unique entity counts | Distinct customers per month |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additivity Classification (Board Exam Favourite!):**
> - **Additive**: Can be summed across **all** dimensions (e.g., Revenue, Quantity).
> - **Semi-additive**: Can be summed across **some** dimensions, not time (e.g., Account Balance).
> - **Non-additive**: Cannot be summed across any dimension (e.g., Ratios, Percentages, Unit Prices).

### Why Aggregates Speed Up Queries вҖ” Intuitive Math
Suppose the atomic fact table has $N$ rows and each query does a full scan costing $O(N)$.

After building an aggregate with $M$ rows where $M \ll N$:
- **Aggregate scan** cost: $O(M)$.
- **Speedup factor**:
$$
S = \frac{N}{M}
$$

**Example**: $N = 10^9$ rows (one year's daily transactions), $M = 10^4$ rows (daily summary by store-product). Speedup $S = 10^5$ вҖ” a **100,000Г—** improvement.

---

## 2.2 вҖ” TIME DIMENSION: DEEP DIVE

### Date Key Design Patterns
1. **Integer surrogate key (YYYYMMDD)** вҶ’ e.g., `20240315`. Easy to sort lexicographically, small (4 bytes).
2. **Smart key** вҶ’ Uses calendar date, never changes.
3. **Day-of-week alignment** вҶ’ Some warehouses use a "day of week" repeated across weeks to enable "same-day-last-year" comparisons.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exam tip: Always recommend the **surrogate integer key** (e.g., `20240315`) over using the actual `DATE` data type. Why? Integers are smaller, faster to index, and immune to date-format issues across BI tools.

### Multiple Time Stamps in a Fact Row
A real-world transaction (e.g., a shipment) has multiple meaningful timestamps:
- `order_date_key` (when ordered)
- `ship_date_key` (when shipped)
- `delivery_date_key` (when delivered)
- `invoice_date_key` (when billed)

Each becomes a **separate foreign key to the SAME Time Dimension table** вҖ” a *role-playing dimension* (advanced topic, but related).

### Fiscal vs. Calendar Hierarchy
- **Calendar Year** = JanвҖ“Dec.
- **Fiscal Year** = depends on business (e.g., India: AprвҖ“Mar; US Fed Gov: OctвҖ“Sep).
- The Time Dimension should contain **both** hierarchies so users can pivot on either.

---

## 2.3 вҖ” JUNK DIMENSIONS: DEEP DIVE

### Construction Logic
Given $k$ low-cardinality flags, each with cardinality $c_i$, the Junk Dimension will have:
$$
\text{Cardinality}_{\text{Junk}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} c_i
$$

**Worked example** (KTU favourite): 3 flags, each with 4 values:
$$
4 \times 4 \times 4 \;=\; 64 \text{ rows}
$$

This is **manageable**. Now consider 10 flags with 5 values each:
$$
5^{10} = 9{,}765{,}625 \text{ rows} \;\;\; (\text{NOT manageable вҖ” split it!})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Cartesian explosion trap**: If the product of flag cardinalities exceeds ~1000 rows, consider splitting into 2вҖ“3 junk dimensions or modelling the flags as separate satellite tables.

### Why NOT Just Keep Flags in the Fact Table?
1. **Index inefficiency** вҖ” A boolean column is a poor index candidate.
2. **Schema clutter** вҖ” Wide fact tables slow down full-table scans.
3. **Limited metadata** вҖ” A separate dimension lets the BI tool display "Cash Payment" instead of cryptic `PMT_CD = 1`.
4. **Extensibility** вҖ” Add a new flag = add a new junk column in the dimension; fact table is unaffected.

---

## 2.4 вҖ” DEGENERATE DIMENSIONS: DEEP DIVE

### When to Use (and When NOT to Use)
вң… **USE** when:
- The identifier is per-row unique (transaction number, order number, ticket number).
- The identifier carries **no descriptive context** that needs to be sliced/diced.
- It is needed for **drill-through to operational systems** (e.g., clicking a fact row to view the original invoice).

вқҢ **DO NOT use** when:
- The identifier has *meaningful* attributes (e.g., a Salesperson has Name, Region, Manager вҖ” that should be a real dimension).
- The identifier has a hierarchy (e.g., a Region Code implies a Region which implies a Country).

### Operational Linkage
Degenerate dimensions are the **bridge back to the OLTP system**. The BI report lists "Invoice #INV-2024-00123" вҖ” clicking it passes the ID to the operational system which retrieves the full invoice.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, mention this **drill-through** use case to earn full marks on degenerate dimensions.

---

## 2.5 вҖ” KTU FORMULA / CONCEPT CHEAT SHEET (HIGH-YIELD)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aggregate row count reduction | $M \ll N$ (atomic) | Query Speedup |
| 2 | Query speedup factor | $S = N/M$ | Performance |
| 3 | Aggregate consistency | $\sum \text{Agg} = \text{Atomic}$ | Integrity |
| 4 | Junk dim cardinality | $\prod_{i=1}^{k} c_i$ | Explosion check |
| 5 | Time dim grain | 1 row per **date** | Daily snapshot |
| 6 | Degenerate dim test | ID unique per row, no descriptive attrs | Drill-through |
| 7 | Additivity | SUM across all dims | Additive fact |
| 8 | Semi-additive | SUM across non-time dims | Balance facts |
| 9 | Non-additive | Cannot SUM across any dim | Ratio facts |
| 10 | Time dim surrogate key | `YYYYMMDD` (4-byte int) | Smart key |

> [!TIP]
> The 10-row cheat sheet above covers ~70% of what KTU examiners test on this module. Memorize the formulas in rows 2, 3, and 4 вҖ” they appear almost every year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# рҹ§® STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This module is largely **design-oriented**, so the "derivations" below are **design walkthroughs** вҖ” step-by-step transformations of raw business requirements into proper dimensional structures. KTU examiners love this format because it tests *reasoning*, not memorization.

---

## 3.1 вҖ” DERIVATION: Designing an Aggregate from an Atomic Fact Table

### рҹҺҜ Business Problem
A retail DW has an atomic fact table `FACT_SALES_ATOMIC` with the following schema:

- **Grain**: 1 row per product per customer per day per store
- **Dimensions**: `date_key`, `product_key`, `customer_key`, `store_key`, `promotion_key`
- **Measures**: `units_sold`, `revenue_inr`, `discount_inr`, `tax_inr`

Management runs the following query **20 times per day**:
> *"Show total revenue and units sold by product category and month, for the last 24 months."*

### рҹ”Қ Step-by-Step Design Derivation

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the queried dimensions**
The query groups by **product category** and **month**. These are higher levels than `product_key` and `date_key` respectively. The other dimensions (`customer`, `store`, `promotion`) are *not* used in this query.

**Step 2 вҖ” Drop the unused dimensions**
The aggregate will NOT have `customer_key`, `store_key`, or `promotion_key`.

**Step 3 вҖ” Pre-aggregate the measures**
Using the aggregation function for each measure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{units\_sold\_agg} &= \sum_{r \in \text{group}} \text{units\_sold} \\
\text{revenue\_agg} &= \sum_{r \in \text{group}} \text{revenue\_inr} \\
\text{discount\_agg} &= \sum_{r \in \text{group}} \text{discount\_inr} \\
\text{tax\_agg} &= \sum_{r \in \text{group}} \text{tax\_inr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Replace low-level keys with higher-level attributes**
- `date_key` вҶ’ `month_key` (or `year_month_key`).
- `product_key` вҶ’ `product_category_key` (or join to product dim and pick `category_name`).

**Step 5 вҖ” Build the aggregate table schema**

```
FACT_SALES_MONTHLY_CATEGORY
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
PK: (year_month_key, product_category_key)
year_month_key        INT       (FK -> dim_month)
product_category_key  INT       (FK -> dim_product_category)
units_sold            INT       (SUM)
revenue_inr           DECIMAL   (SUM)
discount_inr          DECIMAL   (SUM)
tax_inr               DECIMAL   (SUM)
```

**Step 6 вҖ” Populate the aggregate** (one-time ETL + periodic refresh)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INSERT INTO FACT\_SALES\_MONTHLY\_CATEGORY} \\
\text{SELECT} \;\; d.year\_month\_key,\; p.category\_key, \\
\quad \text{SUM}(f.units\_sold),\; \text{SUM}(f.revenue\_inr), \\
\quad \text{SUM}(f.discount\_inr),\; \text{SUM}(f.tax\_inr) \\
\text{FROM} \;\; FACT\_SALES\_ATOMIC \; f \\
\text{JOIN} \;\; DIM\_DATE \; d \; \text{ON} \; f.date\_key = d.date\_key \\
\text{JOIN} \;\; DIM\_PRODUCT \; p \; \text{ON} \; f.product\_key = p.product\_key \\
\text{GROUP BY} \;\; d.year\_month\_key,\; p.category\_key
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Estimate the size reduction**

Suppose:
- Atomic table: $N = 10^9$ rows (3 years Г— 365 days Г— 1000 products Г— 1000 customers Г— 1 store вүҲ 10вҒ№).
- Aggregate groups: 36 months Г— 20 categories = $M = 720$ rows.

Speedup:
$$
S = \frac{N}{M} = \frac{10^9}{720} \approx 1.39 \times 10^6
$$

A **~1.4 million-fold** query speedup. вң…

**Step 8 вҖ” Verify consistency (testing step)**
$$
\text{Total}_{Atomic} = \text{Total}_{Aggregate} \;\; \pm \; 0
$$
Aggregate must reconcile to atomic вҖ” this is checked during ETL.

---

## 3.2 вҖ” DERIVATION: Junk Dimension Cardinality Explosion Check

### рҹҺҜ Business Problem
A banking fact table `FACT_TRANSACTION` has 6 small flags/indicators that the analytics team wants to slice by:

| Flag Name | Values | Cardinality $c_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| `transaction_type` | {Credit, Debit, Transfer, Fee} | 4 |
| `channel` | {ATM, Online, Branch, Mobile, POS} | 5 |
| `currency` | {INR, USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, AUD} | 6 |
| `is_international` | {Yes, No} | 2 |
| `is_high_risk` | {Yes, No} | 2 |
| `customer_segment` | {Retail, HNI, Corporate, Trust} | 4 |

### рҹ”Қ Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the cardinality formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Junk cardinality} &= \prod_{i=1}^{6} c_i \\
&= 4 \times 5 \times 6 \times 2 \times 2 \times 4 \\
&= 1920 \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Decision rule**
- If result $\leq 1000$ вҶ’ **Single junk dimension** вң…
- If $1000 < \text{result} \leq 5000$ вҶ’ **Acceptable, but consider splitting** вҡ пёҸ
- If result $> 5000$ вҶ’ **MUST split into multiple junk dimensions** вқҢ

**Result = 1920 вҶ’ ACCEPTABLE, OPTIMAL SPLIT RECOMMENDED:**

**Step 3 вҖ” Optimal split into TWO junk dimensions**

Split semantically:
- **Junk A** (transaction context): `transaction_type` (4) Г— `channel` (5) Г— `currency` (6) = 120 rows
- **Junk B** (customer/risk context): `is_international` (2) Г— `is_high_risk` (2) Г— `customer_segment` (4) = 16 rows

Total rows = 120 + 16 = **136 rows** (vs. 1920 in single junk dim).

**Step 4 вҖ” Fact table foreign keys**
Original fact table had 6 flag columns (wide). Now it has 2 FK columns to the junk dimensions (narrow):
- `junk_a_key` (FK to Junk A)
- `junk_b_key` (FK to Junk B)

**Step 5 вҖ” Benefit calculation**
- **Storage saved** in fact table: 6 columns вҶ’ 2 columns = 4 columns Г— 4 bytes Г— $N$ rows.
- **Junk dim storage cost**: (1920 - 136) Г— ~50 bytes вүҲ negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> **General rule derived**: When the product exceeds ~1000 rows, **split junk dimensions semantically** (group related flags together) to keep cardinality under control.

---

## 3.3 вҖ” DERIVATION: Building a Time Dimension Table

### рҹҺҜ Business Problem
Build the `DIM_TIME` table for a data warehouse covering **5 years** (2020-2024), with both calendar and fiscal (Apr-Mar) hierarchies, plus Indian holiday flags.

### рҹ”Қ Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 вҖ” Determine grain and total rows**
- **Grain**: 1 row per **date**.
- **Total rows**: $5 \text{ years} \times 365.25 \text{ days/year} \approx 1827$ rows.

**Step 2 вҖ” Generate the surrogate key**
Using the YYYYMMDD integer format:
$$
\text{date\_key} = 10000 \times \text{year} + 100 \times \text{month} + \text{day}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute day-of-week using Zeller's congruence**

For Gregorian date (year $Y$, month $M$, day $D$), treating Jan/Feb as months 13/14 of the previous year:

$$
h = (q + \left\lfloor \frac{13(m+1)}{5} \right\rfloor + K + \left\lfloor \frac{K}{4} \right\rfloor + \left\lfloor \frac{J}{4} \right\rfloor - 2J) \bmod 7
$$

where:
- $q = D$ (day)
- $m = M$ (with Jan=13, Feb=14, Mar=3, ..., Dec=12)
- $K = Y \bmod 100$ (year within century)
- $J = \lfloor Y/100 \rfloor$ (zero-based century)
- $h = 0$ вҶ’ Saturday, $1$ вҶ’ Sunday, $2$ вҶ’ Monday, ..., $6$ вҶ’ Friday.

**Step 4 вҖ” Map to fiscal hierarchy**
For Indian fiscal year (Apr-Mar), given calendar month $M_{cal}$:
$$
M_{fiscal} = ((M_{cal} - 4 + 12) \bmod 12) + 1
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Populate holiday flags**
Cross-reference with an official Indian holiday list (Republic Day Jan 26, Independence Day Aug 15, etc.) and set `is_holiday = 1`.

**Step 6 вҖ” Final schema**
```
DIM_TIME
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
date_key           INT        (PK, YYYYMMDD)
full_date          DATE
day_of_week        VARCHAR(10)
day_num_in_month   TINYINT
day_num_in_year    SMALLINT
week_num           TINYINT
month_name         VARCHAR(10)
month_num          TINYINT
quarter            TINYINT
calendar_year      SMALLINT
fiscal_month_num   TINYINT
fiscal_quarter     TINYINT
fiscal_year        SMALLINT
is_holiday         BOOLEAN
is_weekend         BOOLEAN
is_working_day     BOOLEAN
days_to_year_end   SMALLINT
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners expect you to mention **at least 8 attributes** in the Time Dimension schema. Don't just list `date_key` and `full_date` вҖ” show the hierarchies and flags.

---

## 3.4 вҖ” ANALYTICAL MATRIX: When to Use Which Advanced Technique

This decision matrix is a high-yield KTU exam answer.

| Scenario | Use This Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 5 flags each with 3 values on a fact table | **Junk Dimension** | 3вҒө = 243 rows (manageable), avoids 5 wide columns |
| Need monthly sales by region in 1 second | **Aggregate** | Pre-compute; query becomes trivial |
| Need to slice by "Is this a working day?" | **Time Dimension** | Add `is_working_day` flag in DIM_TIME |
| Fact row carries an Invoice Number | **Degenerate Dimension** | No descriptive context; ID lives in fact |
| "Day" + "Month" + "Quarter" + "Year" hierarchies | **Time Dimension** | Multi-level calendar hierarchy |
| Fact has `order_date` AND `ship_date` | **Time Dimension** (role-playing) | Two FKs to same DIM_TIME |
| Need "all cash transactions by male customers" | **Junk Dimension** | Combine `payment_type` + `gender` |
| Need to drill from monthly report to specific invoice | **Degenerate Dimension** | `invoice_no` enables drill-through |

---

## 3.5 вҖ” DERIVATION: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Aggregates

### When to Build an Aggregate (Decision Formula)

A useful rule of thumb used in KTU exam answers:

$$
\text{Build aggregate} \iff \big( Q \times C_q \big) \;>\; C_{\text{build}} + (C_{\text{storage}} \times T)
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = number of queries served per refresh cycle
- $C_q$ = cost (CPU time) of a single query on atomic data
- $C_{\text{build}}$ = cost of one ETL refresh
- $C_{\text{storage}}$ = cost of storing aggregate per cycle
- $T$ = number of cycles in the analysis window

**Worked example**:
- $Q = 5000$ queries/day, $C_q = 0.5$ seconds, $C_{\text{build}} = 60$ sec, $C_{\text{storage}} = 1$ sec/day, $T = 30$ days.

Without aggregate:
$$
\text{Total} = 5000 \times 0.5 \times 30 = 75{,}000 \text{ seconds}
$$

With aggregate:
$$
\text{Total} = 60 + (1 \times 30) + 5000 \times 0.05 \times 30 = 60 + 30 + 7500 = 7590 \text{ seconds}
$$

Savings:
$$
\text{Savings} = 75{,}000 - 7{,}590 = 67{,}410 \text{ seconds} \approx 90\% \;\; вң…
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Magic number from practice**: If a query is run >100Г— per day against a fact table with >10 million rows, the aggregate almost always pays for itself within one refresh cycle.
