topics.title: Data Quality and Data Governance in ETL вҖ“ Handling Incomplete and Inconsistent Data, Ensuring Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Quality and Data Governance in ETL вҖ“ Handling Incomplete and Inconsistent Data, Ensuring Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Quality (DQ)** in the context of ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) refers to the measure of fitness of data to serve its intended purpose in business operations, decision-making, and analytics. According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 framing, Data Quality encompasses the **completeness, accuracy, consistency, validity, timeliness, and uniqueness** of data as it traverses through extraction, transformation, cleansing, and loading pipelines into a data warehouse.

**Data Governance (DG)** is the formalized framework of policies, standards, processes, roles, responsibilities, and metrics that ensures enterprise-wide data is managed as a strategic asset. Within ETL pipelines, Data Governance dictates *who* owns the data, *how* it is validated, *what* rules govern its transformation, and *when* exceptions are escalated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Data Quality and Data Governance in ETL is a high-weightage topic. Students are expected to articulate the dimensions of DQ, describe cleansing techniques for incomplete and inconsistent data, and outline governance frameworks that enforce accuracy, consistency, and integrity throughout the data warehouse lifecycle.

### Combined Definition for ETL

In ETL pipelines specifically, Data Quality and Data Governance work in tandem:
- **Data Quality** is the *operational outcome* вҖ” clean, validated, reliable data.
- **Data Governance** is the *strategic control mechanism* вҖ” the rules, stewardship, and accountability that *produce* and *sustain* that outcome.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital Patient Records System**
>
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital receiving patient data from dozens of clinics, labs, and pharmacies:
>
> 1. **Extraction (The Intake Desk):** Patient names, diagnoses, test results, and prescriptions flow in from heterogeneous sources. Some forms have missing phone numbers, others use different date formats (DD/MM/YYYY vs MM-DD-YY), and two clinics may record the same patient under slightly different spellings ("Mohammed" vs "Muhammed").
>
> 2. **Transformation (The Quality Control Department):** Before this data enters the central medical records system, a team of trained nurses (the *Cleansing Layer*) standardizes formats, deduplicates patient records, validates diagnostic codes against an official ICD registry, and flags incomplete records for follow-up. Hospital administrators (the *Governance Council*) have established rules: every prescription MUST have a dosage, every diagnosis MUST use ICD-10 codes, and lab values outside reference ranges MUST be flagged.
>
> 3. **Loading (The Central Records Vault):** Clean, standardized, validated data is now safely stored, and the Chief Medical Information Officer (the *Data Steward*) can confidently query "How many diabetic patients are on metformin?" вҖ” knowing the answer is trustworthy.
>
> **In this analogy:**
> - **Data Quality** = The cleanliness and reliability of the patient record.
> - **Data Governance** = The hospital's policies, the QC department, and the medical officers who enforce the rules.

---

## 1.3 Core Dimensions of Data Quality (The Six Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Universally Accepted Dimensions of Data Quality** вҖ” these are the standard pillars tested in KTU examinations.

| # | Dimension | Formal Definition | ETL Implementation Example |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Completeness** | The proportion of stored data that is not missing or null. | Checking that all required fields (Customer_ID, Order_Date) are populated. |
| 2 | **Accuracy** | The degree to which data correctly describes the real-world entity or event. | Verifying postal codes against an official postal directory. |
| 3 | **Consistency** | The absence of contradiction between data values stored in different systems for the same entity. | Reconciling the same customer's address between the CRM and Billing system. |
| 4 | **Timeliness** | The degree to which data is up-to-date and available when needed. | Ensuring sales data is loaded into the warehouse within 1 hour of transaction. |
| 5 | **Validity** | The degree to which data conforms to defined formats, types, and domain rules. | Date must be in `YYYY-MM-DD`; Age must be a positive integer < 150. |
| 6 | **Uniqueness** | The degree to which no entity is recorded more than once. | Deduplicating customer records using fuzzy matching. |

---

## 1.4 What are Incomplete and Inconsistent Data?

**Incomplete Data** refers to records where one or more attribute values are missing, null, unknown, or marked with placeholders (e.g., "N/A", "?", "9999", or empty strings). In ETL, this originates from:
- Source system limitations (legacy systems not capturing optional fields)
- Network failures during data transfer
- Human data-entry omissions
- Schema evolution (new columns added that old records don't populate)

**Inconsistent Data** refers to records where the same real-world entity is represented differently across systems, or where data violates defined business rules. Examples include:
- *Syntactic inconsistency:* "Kerala" vs "KERALA" vs "KL"
- *Semantic inconsistency:* Same customer ID linked to two different birth dates
- *Referential inconsistency:* An Order record referencing a Customer_ID that does not exist in the Customers table
- *Temporal inconsistency:* End date occurring before start date

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ETL Data Quality Framework вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames Data Quality in ETL as a **four-stage lifecycle** embedded within the ETL pipeline itself. Understanding this lifecycle is critical for exam answers.

### Stage 1: Data Profiling (Pre-ETL)
- **Goal:** Discover the current state of the source data.
- **Activities:** Statistical analysis, pattern recognition, frequency distributions, outlier detection.
- **Why it matters:** You cannot cleanse what you have not measured. Profiling reveals the *actual* data quality baseline before any transformation.

### Stage 2: Data Cleansing (During Transformation)
- **Goal:** Rectify incomplete, inconsistent, and inaccurate records.
- **Activities:** Imputation, standardization, deduplication, validation against reference data.
- **How:** Apply business rules, lookup tables, regex patterns, and machine-learning-based anomaly detection.

### Stage 3: Data Validation (Post-Transformation, Pre-Load)
- **Goal:** Confirm that transformed data meets all defined quality thresholds.
- **Activities:** Rule-based checks, constraint enforcement, sampling audits.
- **KTU Focus:** This stage is where DQ metrics are computed and gates are enforced вҖ” bad data is quarantined.

### Stage 4: Data Monitoring (Post-Load, Continuous)
- **Goal:** Detect quality degradation over time and trigger remediation workflows.
- **Activities:** Drift detection, scheduled audits, dashboards, exception reports.
- **Why it matters:** Data quality is not a one-time event; sources evolve, and the pipeline must adapt.

---

## 2.2 Handling Incomplete Data вҖ” Strategy Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Examiners frequently ask "How does ETL handle missing values?" вҖ” Memorize the following tiered approach.

**Tier 1 вҖ” Detection:** Identify missing values using NULL checks, empty-string checks, sentinel value detection (e.g., 9999, 0000-00-00, "UNKNOWN").

**Tier 2 вҖ” Strategy Selection:** Choose based on data type and business context:
- *Deletion:* Drop records if missingness is minimal and the record is not critical.
- *Imputation:* Fill missing values using statistical methods.
- *Default Values:* Substitute with domain-appropriate defaults.
- *Flag-and-Pass:* Retain NULL and flag downstream consumers.

**Tier 3 вҖ” Imputation Techniques (the most-tested subtopic):**
1. **Mean/Median/Mode Imputation** вҖ” Replace missing numerical values with the column's central tendency.
2. **Hot-Deck Imputation** вҖ” Use values from a similar record (e.g., fill missing income from a demographically similar customer).
3. **Regression Imputation** вҖ” Predict the missing value using a regression model trained on complete records.
4. **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Imputation** вҖ” Use the values of the *k* most similar records.
5. **Multiple Imputation** вҖ” Generate multiple plausible values, run analysis on each, and combine results to capture uncertainty.

**Tier 4 вҖ” Documentation:** Every imputation decision is logged for audit and governed under Data Governance policies.

---

## 2.3 Handling Inconsistent Data вҖ” Resolution Techniques

| Inconsistency Type | Example | ETL Resolution Technique |
|--------------------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Format Inconsistency** | Date: "12/03/2024" vs "March 12, 2024" | Standardization using format functions (TO_DATE, REGEX) |
| **Code Inconsistency** | Gender: "M"/"F"/"Male"/"female"/"1"/"0" | Lookup table mapping to canonical values |
| **Naming Inconsistency** | "Kerala" vs "KERALA" vs "kerala" | Lowercasing + master data reference |
| **Unit Inconsistency** | Distance in km vs miles | Unit conversion functions |
| **Referential Inconsistency** | Foreign key pointing to non-existent primary key | Outer joins, referential integrity checks, rejection of orphans |
| **Duplicate Inconsistency** | Same customer appearing twice with slight variations | Fuzzy matching (Levenshtein, Soundex), survivorship rules |

---

## 2.4 Data Governance Framework вҖ” The Five Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Governance in ETL is the systemic enabler of Data Quality.** KTU 2024 Module 3 expects students to identify and describe the following five pillars.

1. **Data Stewardship** вҖ” Designated individuals (Data Stewards) own specific data domains (e.g., Customer Data Steward, Product Data Steward) and are accountable for quality within their domain.

2. **Data Policies & Standards** вҖ” Formalized rules (e.g., "All customer emails must be RFC 5322 compliant", "Country codes must follow ISO 3166-1 alpha-2").

3. **Data Quality Metrics & SLAs** вҖ” Quantitative thresholds (e.g., "Customer address completeness must be вүҘ 98%", "Daily load must complete by 06:00 AM with zero critical errors").

4. **Data Lineage & Auditability** вҖ” End-to-end traceability of every data element from source to consumption; critical for root-cause analysis and regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA).

5. **Data Security & Privacy** вҖ” Role-based access, encryption, masking, and consent management integrated into the ETL process.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas вҖ” they form the quantitative backbone of Part B 14-mark answers.**

| Metric | Formula | Boundary / Range | Unit |
|--------|---------|------------------|------|
| **Completeness Ratio** | $C = \frac{N_{\text{present}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \leq C \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio (often expressed as %) |
| **Accuracy Ratio** | $A = \frac{N_{\text{correct}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Consistency Ratio** | $K = \frac{N_{\text{consistent pairs}}}{N_{\text{total pairs}}}$ | $0 \leq K \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Uniqueness Ratio** | $U = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{duplicates}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \leq U \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Composite DQ Score (weighted)** | $DQ = w_1 C + w_2 A + w_3 K + w_4 U + w_5 T + w_6 V$ | $0 \leq DQ \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Error Rate** | $E = 1 - DQ$ | $0 \leq E \leq 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Records Per Million Errors (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \frac{N_{\text{errors}}}{N_{\text{opportunities}}} \times 10^{6}$ | $DPMO \geq 0$ | Errors per million opportunities |
| **Sigma Level (Six Sigma)** | $\sigma = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times \frac{N_{\text{successes}}}{N_{\text{units}}}$ | $1.5 \leq \sigma \leq 6$ | Sigma units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** The weight values $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_6$ in the Composite DQ Score must satisfy:
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 1$$
> and typically $w_i \in [0, 1]$ for all $i$. This constraint is a common follow-up question in KTU exams.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Banking ETL Pipelines:** Composite DQ scores gate regulatory reporting; dropping below 0.95 triggers manual review before submission to the central bank.
- **Healthcare Data Warehousing:** Accuracy and completeness scores are HIPAA-audit-defining metrics.
- **E-Commerce Analytics:** Timeliness (freshness) directly determines dashboard SLAs for inventory and pricing decisions.
- **Telecommunications CDR Warehousing:** Consistency and uniqueness scores detect fraudulent SIM cloning through duplicate device fingerprinting.

---

## 2.6 Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity вҖ” The Trinity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically calls out these three terms вҖ” they are NOT synonymous.** Examiners often test this distinction.

| Term | Focus | Example |
|------|-------|---------|
| **Accuracy** | *Correctness of value* with respect to ground truth. | A customer's date of birth is "1985-07-12" and this matches their passport. |
| **Consistency** | *Agreement of values* across systems/replicas for the same entity. | The customer's email is "alice@example.com" in both CRM and Billing system. |
| **Integrity** | *Validity and completeness of relationships* between data entities (referential, domain, entity integrity). | Every Order row references an existing Customer_ID, and Customer_ID is never null. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Composite Data Quality Score (Weighted Aggregation)

**Problem Statement:** Given raw counts of present, correct, consistent, unique, timely, and valid records out of a total of $N$ records, derive the weighted Composite Data Quality Score that satisfies the KTU 2024 Module 3 assessment framework.

### Step 1: Define the Universe of Records

Let the ETL pipeline ingest a total of $N$ records from heterogeneous sources into the data warehouse. Each record is evaluated against $k = 6$ quality dimensions.

### Step 2: Define the Per-Dimension Counts

Let:
- $N_p$ = number of records where the required attribute is **present** (not null, not empty).
- $N_c$ = number of records where the value is **correct** (matches ground truth / reference).
- $N_s$ = number of records where the value is **consistent** across systems.
- $N_u$ = number of records that are **unique** (not duplicated).
- $N_t$ = number of records deemed **timely** (loaded within SLA).
- $N_v$ = number of records that are **valid** (conform to domain rules).

### Step 3: Define Per-Dimension Ratios

Each dimension's score is its count divided by $N$:

$$
C_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_p}{N}, \quad A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_c}{N}, \quad K_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_s}{N}
$$

$$
U_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_u}{N}, \quad T_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_t}{N}, \quad V_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_v}{N}
$$

**Logic:** We normalize raw counts into unitless ratios bounded in $[0, 1]$ so that the dimensions can be aggregated on a common scale.

### Step 4: Assign Weights Reflecting Business Priority

For a healthcare data warehouse, accuracy may be weighted higher than timeliness. Let weights $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5, w_6$ correspond to $C, A, K, U, T, V$ respectively, such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0 \quad \forall i
$$

### Step 5: Linear Weighted Aggregation

The Composite Data Quality Score is defined as the linear weighted sum:

$$
DQ = w_1 \cdot C_{\text{ratio}} + w_2 \cdot A_{\text{ratio}} + w_3 \cdot K_{\text{ratio}} + w_4 \cdot U_{\text{ratio}} + w_5 \cdot T_{\text{ratio}} + w_6 \cdot V_{\text{ratio}}
$$

**Logic:** This is the standard weighted arithmetic mean of the per-dimension ratios, producing a single scalar in $[0, 1]$ that quantifies overall data fitness.

### Step 6: Show DQ is Bounded in [0, 1]

**Lower bound (worst case):** All $N$ records fail all dimensions, so $N_p = N_c = \ldots = N_v = 0$:

$$
DQ_{\min} = 0
$$

**Upper bound (best case):** All $N$ records pass all dimensions, so each ratio equals 1:

$$
DQ_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot 1 = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
0 \leq DQ \leq 1
$$

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A retail ETL pipeline processes $N = 10{,}000$ records. The counts are:
- Present ($N_p$): 9,800
- Correct ($N_c$): 9,500
- Consistent ($N_s$): 9,700
- Unique ($N_u$): 9,900
- Timely ($N_t$): 9,850
- Valid ($N_v$): 9,600

Weights (for retail): $w_1 = 0.20, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.15, w_4 = 0.10, w_5 = 0.10, w_6 = 0.20$

**Step 7.1 вҖ” Compute the per-dimension ratios:**

$$
C_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9800}{10000} = 0.9800
$$

$$
A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9500}{10000} = 0.9500
$$

$$
K_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9700}{10000} = 0.9700
$$

$$
U_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9900}{10000} = 0.9900
$$

$$
T_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9850}{10000} = 0.9850
$$

$$
V_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{9600}{10000} = 0.9600
$$

**Step 7.2 вҖ” Apply the weighted sum:**

$$
DQ = (0.20 \times 0.9800) + (0.25 \times 0.9500) + (0.15 \times 0.9700) + (0.10 \times 0.9900) + (0.10 \times 0.9850) + (0.20 \times 0.9600)
$$

**Step 7.3 вҖ” Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= 0.20 \times 0.9800 = 0.1960 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= 0.25 \times 0.9500 = 0.2375 \\
\text{Term}_3 &= 0.15 \times 0.9700 = 0.1455 \\
\text{Term}_4 &= 0.10 \times 0.9900 = 0.0990 \\
\text{Term}_5 &= 0.10 \times 0.9850 = 0.0985 \\
\text{Term}_6 &= 0.20 \times 0.9600 = 0.1920
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7.4 вҖ” Final summation:**

$$
DQ = 0.1960 + 0.2375 + 0.1455 + 0.0990 + 0.0985 + 0.1920
$$

$$
DQ = 0.9685
$$

**Step 7.5 вҖ” Verify weight constraint:**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The retail data warehouse has a Composite DQ Score of **0.9685 (96.85%)**, which exceeds the typical enterprise SLA threshold of 95% and is fit for analytical consumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Error Rate and Its Relationship to DQ

The Error Rate $E$ is the complement of the Composite DQ Score:

$$
E = 1 - DQ
$$

**Derivation logic:** If a fraction $DQ$ of the data is "good", the remaining fraction must be "bad" (assuming a binary pass/fail aggregation), giving $E = 1 - DQ$.

**Substituting the worked example:**

$$
E = 1 - 0.9685 = 0.0315
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **3.15% of records** in the pipeline exhibit at least one quality defect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sigma Level for ETL Quality Control

The Six Sigma methodology quantifies process capability. For an ETL pipeline:

**Step 1 вҖ” Count successful units:**

Let $N_{\text{success}}$ = number of records that pass all DQ dimensions; $N_{\text{units}}$ = total records processed.

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute success yield:**

$$
Y = \frac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{units}}}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Apply the Sigma Level formula (with 1.5-sigma shift correction):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times \frac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{units}}}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Worked example:** If $N_{\text{success}} = 9{,}685$ out of $N_{\text{units}} = 10{,}000$ (i.e., DQ = 0.9685):

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times \frac{9685}{10000}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times 0.9685
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = 0.8406 + 28.4398 = 29.2804
$$

**Saturation note:** In real-world ETL practice, the sigma level is **capped at 6.0** because the formula is calibrated for that range; values beyond 6 indicate near-perfect processes and are practically indistinguishable. The KTU-typical expectation is a sigma level between **3.0 and 6.0**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing an Imputation Method

| Scenario | Missing Rate | Data Type | Recommended Method | Rationale |
|----------|--------------|-----------|--------------------|-----------|
| < 1% missing, MCAR (Missing Completely at Random) | Very low | Any | **Listwise Deletion** | Negligible information loss; simplest approach. |
| 1вҖ“5% missing, MCAR | Low | Numerical | **Mean/Median Imputation** | Preserves central tendency; low bias if MCAR. |
| 1вҖ“5% missing, MAR (Missing at Random) | Low | Numerical | **Regression Imputation** | Leverages correlations with other variables. |
| 5вҖ“20% missing, mixed pattern | Moderate | Mixed | **KNN Imputation** | Captures multivariate similarity; robust. |
| > 20% missing | High | Mixed | **Multiple Imputation** | Quantifies imputation uncertainty; statistically sound. |
| Categorical with missing | Any | Categorical | **Mode Imputation** or **"Missing" category** | Preserves distribution; explicit category aids interpretation. |
| Time-series with missing | Any | Time-series | **Forward Fill / Interpolation / Seasonal Decomposition** | Respects temporal autocorrelation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which imputation method should be used?", always justify your choice using the **missingness mechanism (MCAR, MAR, MNAR)** and the **missing rate**. This is the single most-discriminating factor in the KTU marking scheme for 7-mark sub-parts.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Data Quality Lifecycle in an ETL Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Systems OLTP CRM ERP IoT Logs] --> B[Stage 1 Data Profiling]
    B --> C{Quality Baseline Assessment}
    C -->|Pass Profile| D[Stage 2 Data Cleansing]
    C -->|Fail Profile| E[Quarantine and Alert Stewards]
    E --> B

    D --> F[Imputation for Missing Values]
    D --> G[Standardization and Normalization]
    D --> H[Deduplication via Fuzzy Matching]
    D --> I[Validation Against Reference Data]

    F --> J[Stage 3 Post-Cleansing Validation]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K{All DQ Thresholds Met}
    K -->|Yes| L[Stage 4 Load to Data Warehouse]
    K -->|No| M[Reject and Route to Error Table]

    L --> N[Continuous DQ Monitoring Dashboard]
    N --> O{Drift Detected}
    O -->|No| N
    O -->|Yes| P[Trigger Governance Workflow]
    P --> B

    Q[Data Governance Council] -->|Policies and SLAs| B
    Q -->|Stewardship Rules| D
    Q -->|Audit Standards| J
    Q -->|Access Controls| L
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The pipeline is **governed end-to-end** by the Data Governance Council, which injects policies at every stage.
- **Stage 1 (Profiling)** establishes the baseline; failure here prevents downstream waste.
- **Stage 2 (Cleansing)** uses four parallel sub-processes: Imputation, Standardization, Deduplication, and Validation.
- **Stage 3 (Validation)** acts as a gatekeeper; non-conforming data is quarantined, not silently passed.
- **Stage 4 (Load + Monitoring)** closes the loop with continuous monitoring that triggers re-profiling upon drift detection.

---

## 4.2 Data Governance вҖ” Five-Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PILLAR1[Stewardship and Ownership]
        S1[Data Stewards] --> S2[Domain Ownership]
    end

    subgraph PILLAR2[Policies and Standards]
        P1[Data Quality Rules] --> P2[Master Data Definitions]
    end

    subgraph PILLAR3[Metrics and SLAs]
        M1[DQ Score Thresholds] --> M2[Load Timeliness Targets]
    end

    subgraph PILLAR4[Lineage and Auditability]
        L1[Source to Target Mapping] --> L2[Transformation Audit Logs]
    end

    subgraph PILLAR5[Security and Privacy]
        X1[Role Based Access Control] --> X2[Encryption and Masking]
    end

    PILLAR1 --> CORE[Governed ETL Pipeline]
    PILLAR2 --> CORE
    PILLAR3 --> CORE
    PILLAR4 --> CORE
    PILLAR5 --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT[Trusted Analytical Data]
```

**Interpretation:** Each of the five governance pillars feeds the central ETL pipeline. The output is "Trusted Analytical Data" вҖ” the practical deliverable of mature Data Governance.

---

## 4.3 DQ Dimension-to-Cleansing-Technique Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Completeness] --> R1[Imputation: Mean Median Mode KNN]
    D2[Accuracy] --> R2[Reference Data Lookup and Cross Verification]
    D3[Consistency] --> R3[Reconciliation Joins and Master Data Hubs]
    D4[Timeliness] --> R4[Incremental Load and Real Time Streaming]
    D5[Validity] --> R5[Schema Constraints and Regex Validation]
    D6[Uniqueness] --> R6[Fuzzy Matching and Survivorship Rules]
    R1 --> ETL[ETL Cleansing Layer]
    R2 --> ETL
    R3 --> ETL
    R4 --> ETL
    R5 --> ETL
    R6 --> ETL
    ETL --> WH[High Quality Warehouse]
```

**Interpretation:** Each DQ dimension maps to one or more concrete ETL cleansing techniques. The convergence point is the Cleansing Layer inside the Transformation stage of ETL.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Data Quality. List and briefly explain any four dimensions of Data Quality relevant to an ETL pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data Quality (DQ) is the measure of the fitness of data to serve its intended purpose in business operations, analytics, and decision-making, evaluated across multiple dimensions during the ETL process.

**Four Dimensions (2 Marks вҖ” 0.5 each):**
1. **Completeness:** The degree to which all required data values are present (i.e., not null or missing). ETL detects nulls and applies imputation strategies.
2. **Accuracy:** The degree to which data correctly represents the real-world entity. ETL validates against reference/master data.
3. **Consistency:** The agreement of data values for the same entity across different systems. ETL reconciles mismatches using join logic and standardization.
4. **Timeliness:** The degree to which data is up-to-date at the time of use. ETL enforces load windows and uses incremental or real-time streaming to maintain freshness.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Data Governance? Mention the role of a Data Steward in an ETL pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data Governance is a formalized framework of policies, standards, processes, roles, and metrics that ensure enterprise data is managed as a trusted, strategic asset throughout its lifecycle, including in ETL operations.

**Role of a Data Steward (2 Marks):**
A Data Steward is a domain-specific accountable individual who:
- Owns the quality, definition, and business meaning of a specific data domain (e.g., Customer, Product, Finance).
- Defines and enforces DQ rules applied during ETL transformation.
- Resolves data quality exceptions, escalates breaches of governance SLAs, and collaborates with IT to refine cleansing logic.
- Ensures compliance with regulatory standards (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) within their domain.

---

## 5.2 PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 3 carries 14 marks via a single question (with internal choice between two 7-mark sub-parts). Both Question A and Question B are provided for full practice coverage.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the six dimensions of Data Quality in detail. For each dimension, give one example of an ETL cleansing technique used to enforce it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A banking ETL pipeline processes $N = 50{,}000$ loan application records per day. The DQ audit reports the following per-dimension counts of defect-free records:

| Dimension | Defect-free Count |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Completeness | 49,200 |
| Accuracy | 48,500 |
| Consistency | 49,000 |
| Uniqueness | 49,800 |
| Timeliness | 47,500 |
| Validity | 49,400 |

The bank's DQ weights are: $w_1 = 0.20, w_2 = 0.30, w_3 = 0.15, w_4 = 0.10, w_5 = 0.15, w_6 = 0.10$ for Completeness, Accuracy, Consistency, Uniqueness, Timeliness, and Validity respectively.

**Compute (i) the Composite DQ Score, (ii) the Error Rate, and (iii) comment on whether the pipeline meets the bank's 95% SLA.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Distribution:** 1 mark per dimension (definition) + 0.17 mark per example. Examiners expect a tabular or structured answer.

The six dimensions of Data Quality as applied in ETL are:

1. **Completeness:** The proportion of records where all mandatory attributes are populated (non-null). In ETL, **NULL detection and imputation** (e.g., mean, median, KNN) is applied to fill missing values.

2. **Accuracy:** The degree to which data values conform to verified ground truth. ETL enforces this using **reference data lookups** (e.g., validating postal codes against an official postal database).

3. **Consistency:** The absence of contradictions for the same entity across heterogeneous sources. ETL applies **master data reconciliation** and **cross-system joins** to harmonize values.

4. **Uniqueness:** The assurance that each real-world entity appears exactly once. ETL uses **fuzzy matching algorithms** (Levenshtein distance, Soundex) and **survivorship rules** to deduplicate.

5. **Timeliness:** The degree to which data is current at the point of consumption. ETL enforces **load-window SLAs** and uses **incremental/real-time streaming** to reduce latency.

6. **Validity:** Conformance of data to defined formats, types, and domain rules. ETL applies **schema constraints, regex pattern matching, and domain range checks** to reject invalid records.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) вҖ” 7 Marks

**Step 1: Verify the weight constraint (0.5 Mark):**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Compute per-dimension ratios (1.5 Marks):**

$$
C_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{49200}{50000} = 0.9840
$$

$$
A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{48500}{50000} = 0.9700
$$

$$
K_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{49000}{50000} = 0.9800
$$

$$
U_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{49800}{50000} = 0.9960
$$

$$
T_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{47500}{50000} = 0.9500
$$

$$
V_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{49400}{50000} = 0.9880
$$

**Step 3: Apply the weighted aggregation (2.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DQ &= (0.20 \times 0.9840) + (0.30 \times 0.9700) + (0.15 \times 0.9800) \\
&\quad + (0.10 \times 0.9960) + (0.15 \times 0.9500) + (0.10 \times 0.9880)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute each term (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= 0.20 \times 0.9840 = 0.1968 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= 0.30 \times 0.9700 = 0.2910 \\
\text{Term}_3 &= 0.15 \times 0.9800 = 0.1470 \\
\text{Term}_4 &= 0.10 \times 0.9960 = 0.0996 \\
\text{Term}_5 &= 0.15 \times 0.9500 = 0.1425 \\
\text{Term}_6 &= 0.10 \times 0.9880 = 0.0988
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the terms (0.5 Mark):**

$$
DQ = 0.1968 + 0.2910 + 0.1470 + 0.0996 + 0.1425 + 0.0988
$$

$$
DQ = 0.9757
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Error Rate (0.5 Mark):**

$$
E = 1 - DQ = 1 - 0.9757 = 0.0243
$$

**Step 7: SLA Comment (1 Mark):**

The Composite DQ Score is **0.9757 (97.57%)**, which exceeds the bank's 95% SLA threshold. The pipeline **meets the SLA** and the data is fit for downstream regulatory reporting. However, the **Timeliness dimension (95.00%) is the weakest link** and should be a focus for continuous improvement to avoid future SLA breaches.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice-2]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the five pillars of Data Governance in an ETL context. Explain how Data Lineage and Auditability support regulatory compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce ETL pipeline loads $N = 1{,}00{,}000$ customer order records. A profiling pass reveals:
- 8,000 records have missing customer email IDs.
- 5,000 records have inconsistent date formats (e.g., "12-03-2024", "March 12, 2024", "20240312").
- 3,000 records are duplicate customer entries (fuzzy-matched).

**Compute (i) the Completeness Ratio, (ii) the Consistency Ratio (assume the entire inconsistent-date set violates consistency), and (iii) the Uniqueness Ratio. State one ETL cleansing strategy for each issue.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

The five pillars of Data Governance as applied in an ETL pipeline are:

1. **Data Stewardship (1.25 Marks):**
   Designated individuals (Data Stewards) are accountable for specific data domains. In ETL, a Customer Data Steward defines what constitutes a "valid customer", approves the matching rules used for deduplication, and signs off on cleansing exceptions.

2. **Data Policies and Standards (1.25 Marks):**
   Formalized rules governing data structure, format, and semantics. Example: "All dates must conform to ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD)", "Country codes must follow ISO 3166-1 alpha-2", "Email must be RFC 5322 compliant". ETL transformations codify these policies in lookup tables and validators.

3. **Data Quality Metrics and SLAs (1 Mark):**
   Quantitative thresholds and service-level agreements. Example: "Customer master completeness must remain вүҘ 98% at all times; daily load must complete by 03:00 AM; any DQ breach triggers an automatic alert to the steward". ETL tools (Informatica, Talend, SSIS) implement these as rule-based gates.

4. **Data Lineage and Auditability (1.5 Marks):**
   Lineage is the end-to-end traceability of every data element from its source system through every transformation, cleansing operation, and load to its final destination in the warehouse. Auditability ensures that every transformation is logged with timestamp, operator, and rule applied.

   **How it supports regulatory compliance:**
   - Regulations like **GDPR (Articles 15 & 20)**, **HIPAA**, and **SOX** require organizations to demonstrate *what data was collected, how it was processed, who accessed it, and for what purpose*. Lineage provides the technical evidence.
   - In the event of a data breach or audit, lineage enables **root-cause analysis in minutes rather than weeks** by tracing a defective output back to its source.
   - Audit logs serve as **forensic evidence** in legal proceedings and satisfy "right to explanation" clauses in GDPR.
   - Lineage also enables **impact analysis** before schema changes вҖ” knowing which downstream reports depend on a column before modifying it prevents compliance gaps.

5. **Data Security and Privacy (1 Mark):**
   Role-based access controls, encryption in transit and at rest, and PII masking integrated into ETL. Example: Credit card numbers are tokenized before they reach the warehouse; only privileged roles can decrypt them.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) вҖ” 7 Marks

**Step 1: Establish the total record count (0.5 Mark):**

$$
N = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Completeness Ratio (1.5 Marks):**

Missing emails affect 8,000 records, so present emails = 100,000 вҲ’ 8,000 = 92,000.

$$
C = \frac{N_{\text{present}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{92000}{100000} = 0.9200
$$

**Cleansing strategy for missing emails:** Apply **KNN Imputation** using customer demographics (age, region, purchase history) to predict likely email domains, or **flag-and-pass** for manual follow-up by the Customer Data Steward.

**Step 3: Compute the Consistency Ratio (1.5 Marks):**

Inconsistent date formats affect 5,000 records, so consistent records = 100,000 вҲ’ 5,000 = 95,000.

$$
K = \frac{N_{\text{consistent}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{95000}{100000} = 0.9500
$$

**Cleansing strategy for inconsistent dates:** Apply a **standardization function** (e.g., `TO_DATE` with multiple format masks) that parses any input format ("12-03-2024", "March 12, 2024", "20240312") and outputs a canonical ISO 8601 format ("2024-03-12"). Records that fail all parse attempts are quarantined.

**Step 4: Compute the Uniqueness Ratio (1.5 Marks):**

Duplicates affect 3,000 records, so unique records = 100,000 вҲ’ 3,000 = 97,000.

$$
U = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{duplicates}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \frac{3000}{100000} = 1 - 0.0300 = 0.9700
$$

**Cleansing strategy for duplicates:** Apply **fuzzy matching** (Levenshtein distance or Soundex) on customer name + address combinations, cluster similar records, and apply **survivorship rules** (e.g., "keep the record with the most recent update timestamp and the most complete address") to merge duplicates into a single golden record.

**Step 5: Final summary table (2 Marks):**

| Dimension | Ratio | Value | Recommended Cleansing |
|-----------|-------|-------|------------------------|
| Completeness | $C$ | **0.9200** | KNN Imputation or Flag-and-Pass |
| Consistency | $K$ | **0.9500** | Date Standardization to ISO 8601 |
| Uniqueness | $U$ | **0.9700** | Fuzzy Matching + Survivorship Rules |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls вҖ” Avoid These Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing Accuracy with Validity:** Accuracy compares to ground truth; Validity compares to format/domain rules. Marking schemes deduct 1 mark for using them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the weight sum constraint:** Always explicitly state $\sum w_i = 1$ in composite DQ answers; students frequently lose 0.5 marks by omitting it.
> 3. **Using vertical pipes in tables:** Writing $\vert x \vert$ in a markdown table breaks the table syntax; use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead.
> 4. **Skipping the SLA comment:** For any DQ calculation, always end with a one-line interpretation against a stated threshold (e.g., "97.57% exceeds the 95% SLA").
> 5. **Omitting cleansing strategy:** A DQ problem-solving answer without a cleansing recommendation is marked down by 1вҖ“2 marks, because the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the ETL action, not just the metric.
> 6. **Mixing up the imputation hierarchy:** Students often jump to "regression imputation" for any missing data; always justify based on missing rate and mechanism (MCAR/MAR/MNAR).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Read this section on the morning of the KTU exam.**

- **Definition Anchor:** *Data Quality* is the *outcome* (clean data); *Data Governance* is the *control mechanism* (rules, stewards, SLAs) that produces it. They are **not** synonymous.

- **Six DQ Dimensions (Memorize Order):** Completeness, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity, Uniqueness.

- **Trinity of Quality Terms (Often Confused):**
  - **Accuracy** = correctness vs. ground truth
  - **Consistency** = agreement across systems
  - **Integrity** = valid relationships between entities (FK, PK, domain)

- **Four-Stage DQ Lifecycle in ETL:** Profile вҶ’ Cleanse вҶ’ Validate вҶ’ Monitor (continuous loop).

- **Five Pillars of Data Governance:** Stewardship, Policies/Standards, Metrics/SLAs, Lineage/Auditability, Security/Privacy.

- **Composite DQ Score Formula (HIGH-YIELD):**
  $$DQ = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot \text{ratio}_i \quad \text{where} \quad \sum w_i = 1, \quad 0 \leq DQ \leq 1$$

- **Error Rate:** $E = 1 - DQ$.

- **Incomplete Data Strategies (Tiered):** Deletion вҶ’ Imputation (Mean/Median/Mode, Hot-Deck, KNN, Regression, Multiple) вҶ’ Default Values вҶ’ Flag-and-Pass.

- **Inconsistent Data Strategies:** Standardization (format), Lookup Tables (codes), Reconciliation (cross-system), Fuzzy Matching (duplicates), Unit Conversion (units).

- **Imputation Selection Heuristic:**
  - < 1% missing + MCAR вҶ’ Deletion
  - 1вҖ“5% missing + MCAR вҶ’ Mean/Median
  - 1вҖ“5% missing + MAR вҶ’ Regression
  - 5вҖ“20% missing вҶ’ KNN
  - > 20% missing вҶ’ Multiple Imputation
  - Categorical вҶ’ Mode or "Missing" category
  - Time-series вҶ’ Forward Fill / Interpolation

- **Always State the SLA Threshold** when interpreting any DQ score; a number without business context loses marks.

- **Always Verify the Weight Sum Constraint** ($\sum w_i = 1$) before computing a Composite DQ Score.

- **Mermaid Diagrams:** When asked to "draw the DQ framework", use a four-stage ETL pipeline with Profiling, Cleansing, Validation, and Monitoring, governed end-to-end by a Data Governance Council injecting policies at every stage.

- **Lineage = Compliance Gold:** GDPR, HIPAA, and SOX all rely on lineage. Memorize this for any "regulatory compliance" sub-question.

- **Timeliness is the Forgotten Dimension:** Most students remember the first three; timeliness (freshness, load-window SLAs) is frequently the differentiator in 14-mark answers.

- **Never Confuse Completeness with Accuracy:** A field can be present but wrong (complete but inaccurate), or correct but missing (inaccurate-by-omission). KTU examiners test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Optimization & OLAP: Query Optimization in Data Warehouses вҖ“ Indexing Strategies, Partitioning, Performance Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Optimization & OLAP: Query Optimization in Data Warehouses вҖ“ Indexing Strategies, Partitioning, Performance Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Query Optimization in Data Warehouses вҖ” Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Query Optimization in a Data Warehouse (DW)** is the process of selecting the most efficient *execution plan* (access path + join order + aggregation strategy) for a given analytical SQL query, evaluated against a multi-dimensional, read-mostly, voluminous factвҖ“dimension schema, with the objective of minimizing total response time, I/O cost, CPU cost, and memory footprint while maximizing parallelism and throughput.

Unlike OLTP optimization (which favours short, indexed, transactional look-ups), **DW query optimization** is dominated by:
- **Full-table / range scans** of fact tables (often billions of rows).
- **Multi-way star/snowflake joins** (1 fact + N dimensions).
- **Heavy aggregations** (SUM, COUNT, AVG, RANK, GROUPING SETS, CUBE, ROLLUP).
- **Ad-hoc, unpredictable workloads** from decision-support users.

The three pillars of DW query optimization (the **3 Ps of Performance**) are:

| Pillar | Definition | Primary Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Indexing** | Auxiliary data structures that accelerate row-location and column-projection operations | Reduce **I/O amplification** |
| **Partitioning** | Physical division of large tables into smaller, independently accessible units | Enable **partition pruning** and parallel scan |
| **Performance Tuning** | Holistic reconfiguration of schema, parameters, and materializations | Maximize **throughput per query** and **QphH** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The National Library of India

Imagine the **National Library** which holds **50 million books** across **30,000 aisles** (the fact table). A researcher walks in and asks: *"Show me every book on Quantum Computing published after 2010, sorted by citation count."*

A naive librarian (un-optimized query) would walk down **all 30,000 aisles** scanning **every shelf** вҖ” taking **weeks**.

A *smart librarian* uses three techniques together:
1. **Indexing** вҖ” A digital catalogue (Bitmap / B-Tree index) that instantly tells *which aisles* contain the topic "Quantum Computing" published after 2010.
2. **Partitioning** вҖ” The library is physically split into 50 **Subject Halls** (horizontal partitions). The librarian walks only to Hall-7 (Physics) and skips the other 49.
3. **Performance Tuning** вҖ” Pre-printed "Top 100 Cited Quantum Books" pamphlets (materialized views) are kept at the entrance, so the answer is delivered in **seconds** for the most common question.

This is exactly how a modern DW engine (Teradata, Snowflake, Oracle Exadata, SQL Server PDW) optimizes a multi-dimensional analytical query.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams** (memorize verbatim):
> - **Selectivity (S):** Fraction of rows satisfying a predicate; $S \in [0,1]$.
> - **Cardinality (C):** Estimated number of rows output by an operator.
> - **Fan-out (f):** Number of child pointers a B-Tree node can hold.
> - **Clustering Factor (CF):** Measure of row-order correlation with index key; $CF \in [0,N]$.
> - **Star Join:** A join between one fact table and multiple dimension tables on surrogate keys.
> - **Star Transformation:** A CBO technique that rewrites a star join into a sequence of bitmap-indexed semi-joins.
> - **Partition Pruning (a.k.a. Partition Elimination):** The optimizer's ability to skip entire partitions whose partition-key value cannot satisfy the query predicate.
> - **Materialized View (MV):** A physically stored, pre-computed result of a SQL query, maintained by the DW engine.
> - **Query Rewrite:** Transparent substitution of a query to use an MV instead of recomputing aggregations.

## 1.4 Mathematical Preliminaries

The total cost of a query plan, in the classical **System-R cost model** extended for DWs, is:

$$
\text{TotalCost}(Q) = w_1 \cdot C_{I/O} + w_2 \cdot C_{CPU} + w_3 \cdot C_{Network} + w_4 \cdot C_{Latency}
$$

where $w_i$ are platform-specific weights (in commercial engines, $w_1 \gg w_2 \gg w_3 \gg w_4$ for DWs). The optimizer seeks:

$$
P^{\star} = \arg\min_{P \in \text{Plans}(Q)} \; \text{TotalCost}(P)
$$

This is an **NP-hard** combinatorial problem in the number of joins $N$; hence, modern optimizers use **dynamic programming with pruning** (System-R style) or **genetic/evolutionary search** for $N > 7$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Query Optimization Pipeline

The modern DW optimizer executes a **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Parsing & Normalization** вҖ” SQL вҶ’ Parse tree вҶ’ Logical Relational Algebra tree.
2. **Query Rewrite (Logical Optimization)** вҖ” Predicate push-down, sub-query flattening, MV substitution, star transformation, view merging.
3. **Plan Enumeration (Logical-to-Physical)** вҖ” Access-path selection, join-order enumeration, join-method selection, aggregation placement.
4. **Cost Estimation** вҖ” Cardinality, selectivity, and resource cost are computed for every plan node.
5. **Plan Selection & Execution** вҖ” Minimum-cost plan is shipped to the executor (which may further parallelize).

## 2.2 Pillar 1 вҖ” Indexing Strategies in Data Warehouses

DW indexing is fundamentally different from OLTP indexing. The five canonical DW index types are:

### 2.2.1 B-Tree Index (Balanced Tree)
- **Structure:** Hierarchical, self-balancing tree with sorted key values; each internal node holds up to $f$ child pointers.
- **Best For:** High-cardinality columns (e.g., `customer_id`, `product_sku`) and range queries (`BETWEEN`, `<`, `>`).
- **Tree Height:** $h = \lceil \log_f(N) \rceil$ where $N$ = number of leaf rows, $f$ = fan-out (typically 100вҖ“200).
- **DW Drawback:** Inefficient for low-cardinality columns (e.g., `gender`, `region`) вҖ” wastes pages.

### 2.2.2 Bitmap Index
- **Structure:** For each distinct value $v_i$ of a column with $k$ distinct values, a bit-vector of length $N$ is stored; bit $j$ is 1 iff row $j$ equals $v_i$.
- **Best For:** Low-cardinality columns ($k \le 1000$) in star schemas (`region`, `quarter`, `product_category`).
- **Storage Cost:** $k \times N$ bits = $kN/8$ bytes. Optimal when $k \ll N$.
- **Star Transformation Enabler:** Bitmap AND/OR operations across dimension tables produce candidate row-id sets for the fact table.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bitmap vs B-Tree Decision Rule (KTU favourite 14-marker):**
> - If $k \le 100$ AND $N > 10^6$ вҶ’ **Bitmap**.
> - If $k > 1000$ AND frequent range scans вҶ’ **B-Tree**.

### 2.2.3 Bitmap Join Index (BJI)
- A **pre-computed join** between a fact table and a dimension, materialized as a bitmap on the fact table. Eliminates the join at runtime. Storage = $k_d \times N_f$ bits, where $k_d$ = distinct values in the dimension and $N_f$ = fact-table rows.

### 2.2.4 Star Index (Oracle-specific) & Multi-dimensional Cluster (MDC) вҖ” DB2
- **Star Index:** A B-Tree on `(dimension_key_1, dimension_key_2, ..., fact_measure)` to pre-cluster the fact table for star queries.
- **MDC (DB2):** A physical clustering on a multi-dimensional composite key; blocks are organized as an $n$-dimensional array вҶ’ enables **block-level** index look-ups in O(1) per dimension.

### 2.2.5 Clustered vs Non-Clustered Index
- **Clustered Index:** Physically re-orders the table rows according to the index key (1 per table). Improves range-scan locality.
- **Non-Clustered:** Separate structure, original row order preserved (up to 999 per table in SQL Server).
- **DW Recommendation:** Cluster on the **time-dimension surrogate key** to accelerate the most common "last $N$ months" query.

## 2.3 Pillar 2 вҖ” Partitioning Strategies

Partitioning is the **physical horizontal, vertical, or hybrid** decomposition of a giant table. The five canonical schemes:

### 2.3.1 Horizontal Partitioning (Sharding by Rows)
| Strategy | Hash Function | Best Use Case | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | `p = вҢҠ(v вҲ’ v_min) / wвҢӢ` | Time-series, date ranges | Skew if data is uneven |
| **Hash** | `p = h(v) mod K` | Even distribution, parallel join | Cannot do range pruning |
| **List** | Lookup table | Discrete values (`region IN ('APAC','EMEA')`) | Manual maintenance |
| **Round-Robin** | `p = i mod K` | Pure parallel scan | No pruning at all |
| **Composite** | Range + Hash | Sub-partitioning for time + region | Complex SQL |

### 2.3.2 Vertical Partitioning
- Table is split **column-wise** into multiple narrow tables, each holding a subset of columns plus the primary key.
- **Use Case:** Sparse fact tables where only a few columns are accessed per query (e.g., wide fact with 200 columns; one report uses only 4).
- **Reconstruction:** Joins on PK are required; often infeasible for OLAP.

### 2.3.3 Hybrid (Mixed) Partitioning
- Combines horizontal + vertical; e.g., a fact table is range-partitioned by `sale_date` and within each partition vertically split into "active columns" and "cold-history columns".

### 2.3.4 Partition Pruning вҖ” The "Free Lunch"
When the query predicate contains the partition key, the optimizer reads only the relevant partitions:

$$
\text{Partitions\_Read} = \big\lvert \{ p \in \mathcal{P} : \text{predicate}(p) \text{ is satisfiable} \} \big\rvert
$$

If the predicate is **sargable** (Search-ARGument-able) and uses the partition-key column, pruning can reduce I/O by a factor of $K = \vert \mathcal{P} \vert$ (the total partition count).

## 2.4 Pillar 3 вҖ” Performance Tuning

The optimization loop has **four levers**:

1. **Schema-level:** Star/snowflake design, surrogate keys, slowly-changing dimensions (SCD Type 2), degenerate dimensions.
2. **Index-level:** Add/drop B-Tree, Bitmap, BJI based on workload (use `DBA_HIST_SQL_PLAN` / `sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats`).
3. **Materialization-level:** Create MVs for the top 20% of queries (Pareto's Law).
4. **Parameter-level:** Tune `db_file_multiblock_read_count`, `pga_aggregate_target`, `optimizer_dynamic_sampling`, `parallel_max_servers`, `work_mem`.

### 2.4.1 Materialized Views & Query Rewrite
A MV is a **physically stored query result**. The query rewriter transparently substitutes a user query with an equivalent query against the MV:

$$
Q_{\text{user}}(T_1, T_2, \ldots) \;\longrightarrow\; Q'_{\text{user}}(MV) \quad \text{if} \quad Q_{\text{user}} \equiv Q'_{\text{user}} \circ MV
$$

Maintenance strategies:
- **ON COMMIT** (immediate, expensive).
- **ON DEMAND** (manual / scheduled, cheap).
- **FAST REFRESH** (incremental using materialized view logs).
- **COMPLETE REFRESH** (recompute from scratch).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE вҖ” memorize for KTU ESE**

| Concept | Formula | Variable Legend | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| B-Tree height | $h = \lceil \log_{f}(N / f) \rceil$ | $N$=rows, $f$=fan-out | levels |
| Bitmap storage | $\text{Size} = (k \cdot N) / 8$ | $k$=distinct values, $N$=rows | bytes |
| Selectivity | $S = \vert \sigma_{\theta}(R) \vert / \vert R \vert$ | $\sigma$=selection | ratio $[0,1]$ |
| Estimated rows | $\text{Card} = S \times \vert R \vert$ | after predicate | rows |
| Cluster factor | $CF \approx \text{blocks\_to\_scan} / \text{total\_blocks}$ | 0 to 1 (normalized) | ratio |
| Hash partition | $p = h(v) \bmod K$ | $K$ = #partitions | index |
| Range partition | $p = \lfloor (v - v_{min}) / w \rfloor$ | $w$ = partition width | index |
| Pruning ratio | $\eta = \text{Parts\_read} / \text{Parts\_total}$ | $0 < \eta \le 1$ | ratio |
| Speedup factor | $\text{Speedup} = 1 / \eta$ | via pruning | $\times$ |
| Cost of FTS | $C_{FTS} = \lceil \vert R \vert / B \rceil \cdot c_{io}$ | $B$ = block size (rows), $c_{io}$ = I/O cost | I/Os |
| Cost of Index Scan | $C_{idx} = h + \lceil S \cdot \vert R \vert / B \rceil$ | h = tree height | I/Os |
| Parallel speedup | $T_p = (T_s + T_o) / D + T_c$ | $T_s$=serial, $T_o$=overhead, $D$=DOP, $T_c$=comm | seconds |
| Amdahl fraction | $\text{Spd}_{\infty} = 1 / (1-P)$ | $P$ = parallel portion | $\times$ |
| Storage savings (MV) | $\text{Saved} = C_{\text{recompute}} - C_{\text{refresh}}$ | query cost | I/Os |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

These three pillars are deployed in:
- **Snowflake / BigQuery** вҖ” micro-partitioning + columnar MV (auto-managed).
- **Oracle Exadata** вҖ” Smart Scan + Storage Indexes (automatic, transparent).
- **Teradata** вҖ” Partitioned Primary Index (PPI) + Join Indexes.
- **Amazon Redshift** вҖ” Sort keys (compound & interleaved) + distribution keys (KEY/ALL/EVEN).
- **Apache Doris / ClickHouse** вҖ” Sparse primary index + partitioning + MV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” B-Tree Search Cost for a Range Query

**Problem:** Given a fact table $F$ with $\vert F \vert = N = 10^9$ rows, stored in blocks of $B = 100$ rows each, indexed by a B-Tree with fan-out $f = 200$. Compute the I/O cost of the range query `SELECT SUM(amount) FROM F WHERE cust_id BETWEEN 5000 AND 5500;` assuming 1% selectivity.

**Step 1 вҖ” Number of leaf pages containing the data:**

$$
\text{Matching rows} = S \times N = 0.01 \times 10^9 = 10^7 \text{ rows}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Convert rows to blocks (leaf I/Os):**

$$
\text{Leaf I/Os} = \lceil \text{Matching rows} / B \rceil = \lceil 10^7 / 100 \rceil = 10^5 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the B-Tree height:**

$$
h = \lceil \log_f(N / f) \rceil = \lceil \log_{200}(10^9 / 200) \rceil
$$

$$
\log_{200}(5 \times 10^6) = \frac{\ln(5 \times 10^6)}{\ln 200} = \frac{15.4249}{5.2983} \approx 2.911
$$

$$
\Rightarrow h = \lceil 2.911 \rceil = 3 \text{ levels}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Total I/O cost (index scan):**

$$
C_{idx} = h + \text{Leaf I/Os} = 3 + 10^5 = 100{,}003 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Compare to Full Table Scan (FTS):**

$$
C_{FTS} = \lceil N / B \rceil = \lceil 10^9 / 100 \rceil = 10^7 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Compute Speedup:**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{idx} = \frac{C_{FTS}}{C_{idx}} = \frac{10^7}{1.00003 \times 10^5} \approx 99.997 \times
$$

**Conclusion:** The B-Tree index delivers a **~100Г— speedup** for this high-cardinality range query, justifying the index-creation cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Bitmap Index Size & AND-Operation Cost

**Problem:** A `region` column has $k = 5$ distinct values (`NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTRAL`), and the fact table has $N = 10^8$ rows. Compute:
1. Bitmap storage in MB.
2. The cost of a 3-way AND across `region`, `quarter (k=4)`, and `product_category (k=50)`.

**Step 1 вҖ” Bitmap storage:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bitmap}} = \frac{k \cdot N}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{8} = 6.25 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes} \approx 59.6 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Cardinality of bitmaps:** Each bitmap has $N = 10^8$ bits.

**Step 3 вҖ” Cost of pairwise AND (CPU, in 64-bit words):**

$$
\text{Words} = \lceil N / 64 \rceil = \lceil 10^8 / 64 \rceil = 1{,}562{,}500 \text{ 64-bit words}
$$

One AND = 1 CPU cycle per word (with SIMD, even faster):

$$
T_{\text{AND}} = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} \approx 0.47 \text{ ms at 3.3 GHz}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Three-way AND cost (two pairwise operations):**

$$
T_{3\text{-way}} = 2 \times 0.47 \text{ ms} = 0.94 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Result-set row count (assuming uniform independence):**

$$
\text{Result} = N \times (1/5) \times (1/4) \times (1/50) = 10^8 \times 0.001 = 10^5 \text{ rows}
$$

**Conclusion:** Bitmap AND-merge is **sub-millisecond** and produces a 100 K-row candidate set; the subsequent fact-table probe reads only these 100 K rows = 1000 blocks = **3 orders of magnitude** cheaper than FTS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Partition Pruning ROI

**Problem:** A fact table is range-partitioned by `sale_date` into 36 monthly partitions ($K = 36$). A query asks: `SELECT SUM(revenue) FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31';`

**Step 1 вҖ” Partitions satisfying the predicate:**

$$
\text{Parts\_read} = 3 \text{ (Jan, Feb, Mar 2024)}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Pruning ratio:**

$$
\eta = \text{Parts\_read} / K = 3 / 36 = 1/12 \approx 0.0833
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Speedup factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = 1 / \eta = 12 \times
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” With 10Г— intra-partition index on `customer_id` (1% selectivity), further:**

$$
\text{End-to-end I/Os} = 3 \times \text{PartSize} \times S = 3 \times (N/36) \times 0.01 = 8.33 \times 10^5 \text{ blocks}
$$

vs. FTS $= N = 10^7$ blocks вҶ’ **12Г— speedup** with just partitioning, **1200Г—** with combined pruning + bitmap.

**Step 5 вҖ” CostвҖ“Benefit Decision:** Maintain partitioning only if $\eta \le 0.5$ for the top 80% of queries (Pareto).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 вҖ” Materialized View Refresh Cost

**Problem:** A MV `mv_monthly_sales` aggregates $10^9$ fact rows into $120$ monthly buckets. Each day, $10^6$ new rows arrive. Compare:
- **Complete refresh** cost.
- **Fast refresh** cost (using MV log).

**Step 1 вҖ” Complete refresh I/O:**

$$
C_{\text{complete}} = \lceil 10^9 / 100 \rceil = 10^7 \text{ I/Os (full re-scan + re-aggregate)}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Fast refresh I/O (read delta + apply to MV):**

$$
C_{\text{fast}} = \underbrace{\lceil 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{read delta}} + \underbrace{\lceil 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{write delta to MV log}} + \underbrace{\lceil 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{merge into MV}} = 3 \times 10^4 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Speedup:**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{fast}} = 10^7 / (3 \times 10^4) \approx 333 \times
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Amortization:** If a query is asked 1000Г— per day, the fast-refresh cost is recovered by the 333Г— query-speedup within the first ~3 queries.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” Which Index/P-Combination to Use?

| Workload Pattern | Recommended Pillar 1 (Index) | Recommended Pillar 2 (Partition) | Recommended Pillar 3 (Tuning) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ad-hoc BI dashboard (last 12 months, multi-dim) | **Bitmap** on low-cardinality dims + **B-Tree** on time | **Range** on `date_key` (monthly) | **MV** on top-10 dashboards |
| Ad-hoc customer-360 lookup | **B-Tree** on `cust_id` | **Hash** on `cust_id` | Increase `pga_aggregate_target` |
| Slowly changing dim Type-2 history load | **B-Tree** on `(cust_id, eff_from)` | **Range** on `eff_from` | Partition `EXCHANGE` for fast loads |
| Pure star-schema cube query | **Star index / BJI** | **Composite** (range + hash) | Enable **star transformation** hint |
| Bulk historical load (ETL) | None (drop during load) | **List / Range** with `EXCHANGE PARTITION` | Set `ALTER SESSION SET parallel_dml` |
| Sub-second OLAP response | **Columnar + Bitmap** | **Micro-partitioning** (auto) | In-memory MV (HeatWave, Snowflake) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
