# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023, Similar Model]`
**Differentiate between B-Tree Index and Bitmap Index in a Data Warehouse context. When would you prefer a Bitmap Join Index over a simple Bitmap Index?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | B-Tree Index | Bitmap Index |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Sorted tree hierarchy | Bit-vector per distinct value |
| Cardinality | High (k > 1000) | Low (k вүӨ 100) |
| Best for | Range scans, equality on unique keys | Equality, low-cardinality categorical columns |
| Storage | $\approx (N / f) \cdot \text{ptr\_size}$ | $(k \cdot N) / 8$ bytes |
| Operation | Tree traversal (log f N) | Bitwise AND/OR (O(N/64)) |

A **Bitmap Join Index (BJI)** is preferred when the query repeatedly joins a large fact table to a low-cardinality dimension (e.g., `region`). BJI pre-materializes the join, eliminating it at runtime, whereas a plain Bitmap Index still requires the join to be executed. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Similar Model]`
**What is partition pruning? Explain with an example how range partitioning enables sub-second response in a 5-year historical fact table.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Partition pruning** is the optimizer's ability to skip entire physical partitions whose partition-key values cannot satisfy the query predicate, drastically reducing I/O.

**Example:** A `sales` fact table with 60 monthly range partitions. Query:
```sql
SELECT SUM(revenue) FROM sales
WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31';
```
Only **3 of 60** partitions are read вҶ’ pruning ratio $\eta = 3/60 = 1/20$ вҶ’ **20Г— speedup** over full scan. Combined with columnar storage and intra-partition bitmap indexes, response time drops to sub-second. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks вҖ” Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the five major indexing strategies used in Data Warehouses with suitable examples. Compare their storage and access costs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail fact table `SALES_FACT` has 500 million rows. It is range-partitioned by `sale_date` into 60 monthly partitions and bitmap-indexed on `region_id` (4 values) and `product_category` (30 values). A query asks: total revenue for `region = 'SOUTH'` AND `product_category = 'Electronics'` for `Q1-2024`. Compute the bitmap AND cost, partition pruning benefit, and end-to-end I/O saving versus full table scan. Assume block size = 200 rows. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) вҖ” Five Indexing Strategies (7 marks)

1. **B-Tree Index (1.5 marks):** Hierarchical, sorted; $h = \lceil \log_f(N/f) \rceil$ levels. Best for high-cardinality keys (e.g., `customer_id`) and range queries. Cost: $h + \lceil S \cdot N / B \rceil$ I/Os.

2. **Bitmap Index (1.5 marks):** One bit-vector per distinct value; storage = $(k \cdot N) / 8$ bytes. Best for $k \le 100$ in DWs (e.g., `gender`, `region`). Operations are bitwise AND/OR on 64-bit words = $\lceil N/64 \rceil$ cycles.

3. **Bitmap Join Index (1 mark):** Pre-computed fact-dimension join materialized as a bitmap on the fact table. Eliminates runtime join cost. Storage = $k_d \times N_f$ bits.

4. **Star Index / MDC (1 mark):** Composite B-Tree on multiple dimension keys (Oracle) or physical clustering on multi-dim composite (DB2 MDC). Enables one-pass star join.

5. **Clustered Index (1 mark):** Physically reorders the table (one per table). In DWs, cluster on `time_key` to accelerate the most common "last N periods" query. Clustering factor $CF \in [0, 1]$.

6. **(Bonus 0.5 marks)** Function-based, domain, reverse-key indexes can be mentioned.

#### Part (b) вҖ” Quantitative Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 5 \times 10^8$ rows, $B = 200$ rows/block, $k_{region} = 4$, $k_{cat} = 30$, $K_{partitions} = 60$, query targets 3 monthly partitions (Jan, Feb, Mar 2024).

**Step 1 вҖ” Partition pruning (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = 3 / 60 = 0.05 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Speedup}_{part} = 20\times
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Bitmap storage (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Region bitmap} = (4 \times 5 \times 10^8) / 8 = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} = 238 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\text{Category bitmap} = (30 \times 5 \times 10^8) / 8 = 1.875 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} = 1.79 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Bitmap AND cost (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{AND words} = \lceil 5 \times 10^8 / 64 \rceil = 7.8125 \times 10^6 \text{ words}
$$

At 1 cycle/word вүҲ 2.4 ms on a 3.3 GHz core. Resulting row count:

$$
N_{result} = 5 \times 10^8 \times (1/4) \times (1/30) \approx 4.17 \times 10^6 \text{ rows}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” End-to-end I/O (1.5 marks):**

- FTS cost: $\lceil 5 \times 10^8 / 200 \rceil = 2.5 \times 10^6$ block I/Os.
- Optimized cost (3 partitions Г— 1/4 region Г— 1/30 cat selectivity):

$$
C_{opt} = 3 \times \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{60} \times \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{30} = 1041 \text{ I/Os}
$$

- Speedup: $2.5 \times 10^6 / 1041 \approx 2400 \times$.

**Step 5 вҖ” Conclusion (1 mark):** Combined partitioning + bitmap yields a **~2400Г— I/O reduction**, taking the query from minutes to sub-second.

---

### Question B (14 Marks вҖ” Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the four horizontal partitioning strategies (Range, Hash, List, Composite) with formula-based partition-id computation. Discuss the trade-off between **partition pruning benefit** and **partition maintenance overhead**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a **Materialized View (MV)**. A bank creates an MV `mv_daily_txn` aggregating $2 \times 10^9$ transaction rows into 365 daily buckets. Daily delta = $2 \times 10^6$ rows. Compute and compare the I/O cost of **complete refresh** vs **fast refresh**. When is a fast refresh infeasible? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) вҖ” Horizontal Partitioning Strategies (7 marks)

| Strategy | Formula for Partition ID | Best Use Case | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $p = \lfloor (v - v_{min}) / w \rfloor$ | Time-series (`sale_date` months) | Excellent pruning, but data skew risk |
| **Hash** | $p = h(v) \bmod K$ | Even distribution, parallel join | No range pruning |
| **List** | $p = \text{LUT}(v)$ | Discrete categories (regions) | Manual, no skew tolerance |
| **Composite (Range + Hash)** | $p_{outer} = \text{range}(v_t); \; p_{inner} = h(v_r) \bmod K$ | Sub-partitioning time + region | Two-level pruning, complex SQL |

**Trade-off (1 mark):**
- $\uparrow$ partitions вҶ’ $\uparrow$ pruning benefit (smaller $\eta$) but $\uparrow$ maintenance cost (more DDL, more partition-level statistics, possible small-table overhead).
- Sweet spot: $K$ such that $\eta_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.1$вҖ“$0.2$ for the top 80% of queries (Pareto).

#### Part (b) вҖ” Materialized View Refresh (7 marks)

**MV definition (1 mark):** A physically stored, pre-computed result of a SQL query, automatically maintained by the DBMS and transparently substituted via query rewrite.

**Step 1 вҖ” Complete refresh cost (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_{complete} = \lceil 2 \times 10^9 / B \rceil = \lceil 2 \times 10^9 / 100 \rceil = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ I/Os}
$$

(assuming $B = 100$ rows/block).

**Step 2 вҖ” Fast refresh cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{fast} = \underbrace{\lceil 2 \times 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{read delta}} + \underbrace{\lceil 2 \times 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{write MV log}} + \underbrace{\lceil 2 \times 10^6 / 100 \rceil}_{\text{merge into MV}} = 6 \times 10^4 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Speedup (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{fast} = 2 \times 10^7 / 6 \times 10^4 \approx 333 \times
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” When fast refresh is infeasible (1.5 marks):**
- Query contains non-deterministic functions (e.g., `SYSDATE`, `RANK` over the entire set).
- Aggregation lacks proper `GROUP BY` or includes `DISTINCT` over large sets.
- Underlying tables use external/flat-file sources with no MV log.
- Schema changes (column added/dropped from base table).
- After `TRUNCATE` on base table (MV log is purged).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Bitmap vs BJI:** Bitmap Index is on a single column of one table; Bitmap Join Index is on a fact table but pre-encodes a *joined dimension's* distinct values. Mixing them up costs 2вҖ“3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `+ h` term in B-Tree cost:** Cost is $h + \lceil S \cdot N / B \rceil$, NOT just the leaf scan. Many students drop the tree-traversal I/Os.
> 3. **Wrong partition-pruning formula:** It is $\text{Parts\_read} / \text{Parts\_total}$, not $1 / N$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **MV "fast refresh" requires an MV log** on the base table; stating that fast refresh always works costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Star transformation вү  Star join:** Star transformation is a CBO *rewrite* that converts a star join into bitmap semi-joins; many students write the reverse.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (print-friendly)**
>
> вҳҗ **3 Pillars of DW Optimization:** Indexing + Partitioning + Performance Tuning.
>
> вҳҗ **B-Tree** вҶ’ high cardinality, range scans, $h = \lceil \log_f(N/f) \rceil$ levels.
>
> вҳҗ **Bitmap** вҶ’ low cardinality ($k \le 100$), storage $(k \cdot N)/8$ bytes, sub-ms AND/OR.
>
> вҳҗ **Bitmap Join Index (BJI)** вҶ’ pre-joined fact+dim bitmap, eliminates join at runtime.
>
> вҳҗ **Star Index / MDC** вҶ’ composite multi-dim clustering for one-pass star join.
>
> вҳҗ **Clustered Index** вҶ’ physical reordering; in DWs, cluster on time-dim.
>
> вҳҗ **Range Partition** вҶ’ $p = \lfloor (v - v_{min})/w \rfloor$; best for time-series.
>
> вҳҗ **Hash Partition** вҶ’ $p = h(v) \bmod K$; best for even parallel join.
>
> вҳҗ **List Partition** вҶ’ lookup table; best for discrete categories.
>
> вҳҗ **Composite Partition** вҶ’ range + hash; two-level pruning.
>
> вҳҗ **Partition Pruning** вҶ’ $\eta = \text{Parts\_read}/\text{Parts\_total}$, speedup $= 1/\eta$.
>
> вҳҗ **MV** = pre-computed query result; query rewrite is transparent.
>
> вҳҗ **Fast refresh** requires MV log; otherwise complete refresh.
>
> вҳҗ **Star Transformation** = bitmap-rewrite of star join (CBO technique).
>
> вҳҗ **Selectivity** $S \in [0,1]$; **Cardinality** after predicate $= S \times \vert R \vert$.
>
> вҳҗ **Cluster Factor** $CF \in [0,1]$; 0 = perfectly ordered, 1 = random.
>
> вҳҗ **Star Schema** = 1 fact + N dimensions; **Snowflake** = normalized dims.
>
> вҳҗ **SCD Type 2** = preserve history via `(eff_from, eff_to, is_current)`.
>
> вҳҗ **Pareto's Law** вҶ’ 20% of queries consume 80% of resources вҶ’ optimize that 20%.
>
> вҳҗ **Amdahl's Law** вҶ’ max speedup $= 1/(1-P)$ for parallel portion $P$.
>
> вҳҗ **Cost formula:** $\text{TotalCost} = w_1 C_{I/O} + w_2 C_{CPU} + w_3 C_{Net} + w_4 C_{Lat}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OLAP вҖ“ Architectures: MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** OLAP вҖ“ Architectures: MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP ## SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is OLAP? **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** is a category of software technology that enables analysts, managers, and executives to gain insight into data through fast, consistent, interactive access to a wide variety of possible views of information that has been transformed from raw data to reflect the real dimensionality of the enterprise as understood by the users. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Codd's OLAP Rule Context):** > OLAP is a multi-dimensional, query-and-analysis-oriented data processing environment designed to support **decision-support queries** вҖ” typically against a **data warehouse** or **data mart**. The three fundamental operations are **Slice, Dice, Drill-Down/Roll-Up**, executed across a **data cube**. ### 1.2 The Three OLAP Server Architectures Because OLAP must deliver **sub-second query response** for multi-dimensional queries, the *physical storage* of the data cube becomes a critical design decision. The three dominant architectural choices are: | Architecture | Full Form | Core Storage Principle | |---|---|---| | **MOLAP** | Multidimensional OLAP | Stores data in a **proprietary, sparse multi-dimensional array** (a pre-built cube file) | | **ROLAP** | Relational OLAP | Stores data in **relational DBMS tables** (star/snowflake schema) and generates SQL on the fly | | **HOLAP** | Hybrid OLAP | Stores **detailed data in relational tables** + **aggregations in a multi-dimensional cube** | > [!NOTE] > **Why three architectures?** > There is a classic **storage-vs-speed trade-off**: > - MOLAP вҶ’ *fastest query*, *lowest storage efficiency* (lots of zeros stored) > - ROLAP вҶ’ *best storage efficiency* (sparse data), *slower query* (SQL generation overhead) > - HOLAP вҶ’ *best of both worlds* вҖ” base data in RDBMS, aggregates in cube --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Library Bookstore" Metaphor Imagine you are preparing for an exam. You have **3,000 pages of notes** spanning 8 subjects. There are **three ways** to organize them: 1. **MOLAP = Photocopy and Index Cards**: You read every note once, then create a pre-organized set of **index cards** grouped by *Subject вҶ’ Topic вҶ’ Concept*. When the examiner asks a question, you flip the right card in **one second**. The catch? You have to **pre-build every possible grouping** (every combination of subject, topic, sub-topic), even ones you'll never need. The cards consume a lot of paper. 2. **ROLAP = Search the Original Notes**: You keep the original **3,000 pages in a binder** indexed by date. When asked a question, you **construct a SQL-like search** ("find every note where subject=Physics AND topic=Optics"), and it works вҖ” but **takes 30 seconds** to scan. **No wasted paper** (no aggregates stored), but the search is slower. 3. **HOLAP = Original Notes + Summary Cards**: You keep the **3,000 original pages** in a binder, but you also create **summary cards only for the most important groupings** (likely exam questions). For detailed lookups you search the binder; for common aggregates you flip the card. **Balanced cost and speed.** This is precisely the trade-off in **MOLAP vs ROLAP vs HOLAP**. --- ### 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist (KTU Exam Favorites) - **Data Cube** вҖ” A multi-dimensional array of values organized along *n* dimensions - **Dimension** вҖ” A categorical axis (e.g., Time, Product, Geography) - **Measure** вҖ” A numerical value to be analyzed (e.g., Sales, Profit, Quantity) - **Cell** вҖ” A single (Dimension-Value, Measure) intersection - **Slice** вҖ” Fixing one dimension to a single value - **Dice** вҖ” Selecting a sub-cube by bounding multiple dimensions - **Drill-Down / Roll-Up** вҖ” Moving to finer / coarser granularity - **Aggregation** вҖ” Pre-computed summary (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT) - **Sparsity** вҖ” The fraction of cells that are empty in the cube - **Pre-computation** вҖ” Strategy of computing and storing aggregates *before* queries arrive > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to be able to **compare** these three architectures across **storage, query speed, scalability, sparsity handling, and maintenance overhead** вҖ” this is a guaranteed 14-mark question. ## SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 MOLAP вҖ” Multidimensional OLAP (The "Pre-Built Cube" Approach) **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. The **ETL process** loads fact and dimension data from the warehouse into a **multi-dimensional array**. 2. The MOLAP server **pre-computes ALL possible aggregates** along every dimension hierarchy and stores them physically on disk in a proprietary file structure. 3. At query time, the user request is mapped directly to **cell coordinates** in the cube вҖ” no SQL is generated. 4. The pre-computed value is fetched and returned in **sub-second time**. **Strengths:** - Excellent query performance (consistent sub-second response) - Handles complex multi-dimensional calculations natively - Compact storage for *dense* sub-cubes **Weaknesses:** - Poor storage efficiency for **sparse** data (most real-world cubes are 90%+ sparse) - Cube rebuild is expensive when data changes - Limited scalability (typically < 50 GB) **The Sparsity Problem (with math):** If a cube has $n$ dimensions, where dimension $i$ has $d_i$ distinct values, the total number of cells (including the ALL-level) is: $$C_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (d_i + 1)$$ A dimension with a 5-level hierarchy actually contributes 6 levels (5 + ALL), hence the **+1** for the grand total. --- ### 2.2 ROLAP вҖ” Relational OLAP (The "SQL-on-the-Fly" Approach) **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. Data remains in **star-schema relational tables** in the RDBMS (one central FACT table + N DIMENSION tables). 2. The ROLAP engine sits between the user and the RDBMS. It contains a **middleware layer** that: - Parses the user's MDX or OLAP query - Translates it into **optimized SQL** (with GROUP BY, JOINs, sub-queries) - Pushes computation down to the RDBMS 3. The RDBMS executes the SQL and returns the result. 4. ROLAP *may* maintain a **summary table** (aggregate navigator) but the bulk of data is at the base fact level. **Strengths:** - Leverages existing RDBMS technology, security, and backup - Handles **enormous data volumes** (terabytes to petabytes) - Excellent for **sparse** data (RDBMS never stores empty cells вҖ” `NULL` is skipped) - Real-time updates to base data are reflected immediately **Weaknesses:** - Slowest of the three for complex multi-dimensional queries - Performance depends heavily on **RDBMS optimizer** and **indexing strategy** - SQL generation overhead for cross-dimensional analysis --- ### 2.3 HOLAP вҖ” Hybrid OLAP (The "Best of Both Worlds" Approach) **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Base / detail data** is stored in **relational tables** (ROLAP style) вҖ” no pre-aggregation, no wasted storage. 2. **Frequently-queried aggregates** (e.g., quarterly totals, regional rollups) are **extracted and stored in a multi-dimensional cube** (MOLAP style). 3. Query routing logic: - If the query targets **aggregated data** вҶ’ route to **MOLAP cube** вҶ’ fast response - If the query needs **drill-down to detail** вҶ’ route to **ROLAP tables** вҶ’ slower but accurate 4. The ROLAP engine and the MOLAP engine are integrated as a single front-end to the user. **Strengths:** - Best balance of **scalability** (from ROLAP) and **speed** (from MOLAP) - Storage-efficient вҖ” only "hot" aggregates are pre-computed - Industry standard for **large enterprise warehouses** **Weaknesses:** - Most complex to implement and maintain - Data consistency between ROLAP and MOLAP parts must be managed - Slightly slower than pure MOLAP for purely aggregate queries --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas вҖ” they appear regularly in KTU module-end and university exams.** | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes | |---|---|---| | Total cells in a cube | $C_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (d_i + 1)$ | $n$ = number of dimensions, $d_i$ = cardinality of dimension $i$, **+1** counts the ALL-level | | Total cells without ALL | $C_{noALL} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | Pure product, no +1 | | Sparsity coefficient | $S = 1 - \dfrac{N_{actual}}{C_{total}}$ | $N_{actual}$ = non-empty cells; $S \to 1$ means highly sparse | | Density coefficient | $D = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{C_{total}} = 1 - S$ | $D \to 0$ means mostly empty | | MOLAP storage | $M_{molap} = C_{total} \times \text{sizeof(cell)}$ | Wastes memory on empty cells | | ROLAP storage (base) | $M_{rolap} = N_{actual} \times (\text{sizeof(fact)} + \text{join overhead})$ | Only stores real facts | | Number of distinct aggregates | $A = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (L_i + 1) - 1$ | $L_i$ = levels in dimension $i$; **-1** excludes the empty aggregate | | Compression ratio (sparse cube) | $CR = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{actual}}$ | Larger CR вҶ’ more wasted space in MOLAP | | Query time (rough model) | $T_{query} \approx T_{io} + T_{cpu} + T_{network}$ | MOLAP minimizes $T_{cpu}$ via pre-computation | > **Units Convention:** Cell size in **bytes**, storage in **MB/GB**, query time in **milliseconds (ms)**, sparsity is a dimensionless ratio in $[0, 1]$. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Retail / E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** HOLAP for sales analysis вҖ” base transactions in ROLAP, daily/weekly rollups in MOLAP - **Banking (Transaction Risk Analysis):** ROLAP for regulatory compliance (huge fact tables, sparse) - **Telecom (Call Detail Records):** MOLAP for small, dense network-quality cubes - **Healthcare Analytics:** HOLAP for patient outcome dashboards ## SECTION 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Total Number of Cells in an n-Dimensional Cube **Problem Statement:** A data warehouse analyst is designing a 3-D cube with dimensions: *Time* (24 months), *Product* (50 SKUs), and *Geography* (15 regions). The cube will contain a single measure (Sales). Compute the total number of cells including the ALL-level rollup. **Given:** - Number of dimensions: $n = 3$ - Cardinalities: $d_1 = 24$ (Time), $d_2 = 50$ (Product), $d_3 = 15$ (Geography) - Include ALL-level: use $d_i + 1$ per dimension **Step 1 вҖ” State the general formula:** $$C_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (d_i + 1)$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Expand the product explicitly:** $$C_{total} = (d_1 + 1) \times (d_2 + 1) \times (d_3 + 1)$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Substitute the values:** $$C_{total} = (24 + 1) \times (50 + 1) \times (15 + 1)$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Evaluate each factor:** $$C_{total} = 25 \times 51 \times 16$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Multiply in sequence:** $$C_{total} = 25 \times 51 = 1275$$ $$C_{total} = 1275 \times 16 = 20400$$ **Final Answer:** $C_{total} = \mathbf{20{,}400 \text{ cells}}$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Sparsity and Storage Impact **Problem Statement:** Continuing from Derivation 1, suppose the analyst surveys the warehouse and finds that **only 3,825 cells** actually contain non-zero sales data (due to limited product availability in certain regions during certain months). Compute the sparsity, density, and wasted storage if each cell requires 8 bytes in a MOLAP cube. **Step 1 вҖ” Apply the sparsity formula:** $$S = 1 - \frac{N_{actual}}{C_{total}}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Substitute values:** $$S = 1 - \frac{3825}{20400}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Simplify the fraction:** $$S = 1 - 0.1875$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Compute:** $$S = 0.8125$$ **Interpretation:** The cube is **81.25% sparse** вҖ” a classic dense data island in an ocean of zeros. **Step 5 вҖ” Compute density:** $$D = 1 - S = 1 - 0.8125 = 0.1875$$ **Step 6 вҖ” Compute MOLAP storage (including all cells):** $$M_{molap} = C_{total} \times \text{sizeof(cell)} = 20400 \times 8 \text{ bytes}$$ $$M_{molap} = 163{,}200 \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{159.375 \text{ KB}}$$ **Step 7 вҖ” Compute ROLAP storage (only real facts):** $$M_{rolap} = N_{actual} \times \text{sizeof(fact row)} = 3825 \times 64 \text{ bytes (incl. overhead)}$$ $$M_{rolap} = 244{,}800 \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{239.06 \text{ KB}}$$ **Step 8 вҖ” Wasted storage in MOLAP:** $$M_{waste} = (C_{total} - N_{actual}) \times \text{sizeof(cell)} = (20400 - 3825) \times 8 = 132{,}600 \text{ bytes} \approx 129.5 \text{ KB}$$ **Final Answer:** Sparsity $S = 0.8125$; wasted MOLAP storage $\approx 129.5$ KB (79.4% of MOLAP allocation is zeros). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** When $S > 0.90$, MOLAP storage becomes prohibitively wasteful, and **ROLAP or HOLAP** is the recommended choice. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Number of Distinct Aggregates **Problem Statement:** A 3-D cube has dimensions with hierarchy levels as follows: Time (4 levels: Day вҶ’ Month вҶ’ Quarter вҶ’ Year), Product (3 levels: SKU вҶ’ Category вҶ’ Department), Geography (3 levels: City вҶ’ State вҶ’ Country). Compute the number of distinct non-empty aggregates the cube can produce. **Step 1 вҖ” State the aggregate formula:** $$A = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (L_i + 1) - 1$$ The **-1** removes the empty (no-aggregation) combination. **Step 2 вҖ” Substitute the level counts:** $$A = (4 + 1) \times (3 + 1) \times (3 + 1) - 1$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Evaluate the product:** $$A = 5 \times 4 \times 4 - 1$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Multiply:** $$A = 80 - 1 = 79$$ **Final Answer:** $A = \mathbf{79 \text{ distinct non-empty aggregates}}$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” When to Use Which? > [!NOTE] > This matrix is the **most-reproduced diagram in KTU model answers** for the "compare MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP" question. | Decision Criterion | MOLAP | ROLAP | HOLAP | |---|---|---|---| | **Query Speed** | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (sub-second) | вҳ…вҳ… (seconds to minutes) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (fast for aggregates) | | **Storage Efficiency** | вҳ…вҳ… (poor for sparse) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (only facts) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (mixed) | | **Scalability (data size)** | вҳ…вҳ… (up to ~50 GB) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (TB to PB) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (TB+) | | **Sparsity Handling** | вҳ…вҳ… | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… | | **Real-time Update** | вҳ…вҳ… (rebuild needed) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (immediate) | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (eventual) | | **Implementation Complexity** | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… | вҳ…вҳ… | вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ… (highest) | | **Best Use Case** | Dense cubes, dashboards | Sparse, large warehouses | Enterprise mixed workloads | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Do NOT present this as a "one is best" comparison. Always state the **trade-off** explicitly in your answer. --- ### 3.5 Worked Example вҖ” Choosing the Architecture **Scenario:** A Kerala-based FMCG distributor has 2 years of sales data (730 days), 8 sales regions, and 120 product categories. The cube is moderately sparse (~70% empty). Dashboard queries dominate. **Step 1 вҖ” Estimate total cells:** $$C_{total} = (730 + 1) \times (8 + 1) \times (120 + 1) = 731 \times 9 \times 121$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Evaluate:** $$C_{total} = 731 \times 1089 = 795{,}759 \text{ cells}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Sparsity analysis:** $$S = 0.70 \implies \text{Moderately sparse, not extreme}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Decision:** - Not sparse enough to demand ROLAP - Cube size manageable for MOLAP - Dashboard queries favor MOLAP - **вҶ’ Recommend HOLAP** (base in ROLAP for drill-down, popular aggregations in MOLAP) ## SECTION 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level OLAP Architecture Choice Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD user1[End User / Analyst] --> query[OLAP Query - MDX or SQL-like] query --> router{Query Type Router} router -->|Aggregate Heavy| molap[MOLAP Engine] router -->|Detail / Drill-Down| rolap[ROLAP Engine] router -->|Mixed| holap[HOLAP Engine] molap --> cubeFile[(Pre-computed Cube File
Multi-dimensional Array)] rolap --> rdbms[(Relational RDBMS
Star Snowflake Schema)] holap --> cubeFile holap --> rdbms cubeFile --> result[Result Set] rdbms --> result result --> user1 ``` ### 4.2 MOLAP Internal Flow (Pre-Computation & Query) ```mermaid flowchart LR etl1[ETL Process] --> load1[Load Data into Multi-dim Array] load1 --> preAgg[Pre-compute All Aggregates] preAgg --> cubeStore[(Proprietary Cube File
On Disk)] userQ1[User Query] --> coord[Map Query to Cell Coordinates] coord --> fetch[Fetch Pre-computed Cell] cubeStore --> fetch fetch --> resp1[Sub-second Response] ``` ### 4.3 ROLAP Internal Flow (SQL Generation & Execution) ```mermaid flowchart TD etl2[ETL Process] --> star[Load into Star Schema
Fact and Dimension Tables] star --> rdbmsStore[(RDBMS Storage)] userQ2[User OLAP Query] --> parse[MDX Parser] parse --> translate[Translate to Optimized SQL] translate --> optimize[Cost-Based Optimizer] optimize --> exec[Execute SQL on RDBMS] rdbmsStore --> exec exec --> format[Format Result as Cube View] format --> resp2[Response to User] ``` ### 4.4 HOLAP Internal Flow (Hybrid Routing) ```mermaid flowchart TD etl3[ETL Process] --> star3[Load Detail into Star Schema] star3 --> rdbms3[(RDBMS Detail Tables)] star3 --> cubeBuild[Build Aggregated Cube
for Hot Aggregates Only] cubeBuild --> cube3[(Multi-dim Cube
Pre-computed)] userQ3[User Query] --> analyze[Query Analyzer] analyze -->|Targets Aggregate| routeA[Route to MOLAP Cube] analyze -->|Targets Detail| routeB[Route to ROLAP Tables] routeA --> cube3 routeB --> rdbms3 cube3 --> merge[Merge Result] rdbms3 --> merge merge --> resp3[Unified Response] ``` ### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram of the Three Architectures ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MOLAP_BLOCK[MOLAP Architecture] m1[Multi-dim Array Storage]:::molap m2[Pre-computed Aggregates]:::molap m3[Sub-second Query]:::molap m1 --> m2 --> m3 end subgraph ROLAP_BLOCK[ROLAP Architecture] r1[Relational Tables Star]:::rolap r2[SQL Generation Engine]:::rolap r3[Slower Query via Joins]:::rolap r1 --> r2 --> r3 end subgraph HOLAP_BLOCK[HOLAP Architecture] h1[Detail in RDBMS]:::holap h2[Aggregates in Cube]:::holap h3[Hybrid Query Routing]:::holap h1 --> h3 h2 --> h3 end classDef molap fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#FF6B35,color:#000 classDef rolap fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000 classDef holap fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#009900,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5 вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **Differentiate between MOLAP and ROLAP in terms of storage, query performance, and data sparsity handling. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | MOLAP | ROLAP | |---|---|---| | **Storage** | Stores data in a proprietary multi-dimensional array (pre-computed cube file). Reserves space for *all* cells including empty ones. | Stores data in relational star/snowflake tables in an RDBMS. Only stores actual fact rows вҖ” empty cells are not represented. | | **Query Performance** | Sub-second, because the cube cell is directly accessed by coordinate mapping. | Slower (seconds to minutes) due to SQL generation, JOINs, and GROUP BY operations on the RDBMS. | | **Sparsity Handling** | Inefficient for sparse data; wastes memory on empty cells. | Highly efficient; sparse data is stored compactly as rows in fact tables. | MOLAP is preferred for **dense, small-to-medium** cubes, while ROLAP is preferred for **sparse, large-scale** enterprise data warehouses. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **What is HOLAP? Why is it considered a hybrid of MOLAP and ROLAP? (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** **HOLAP (Hybrid OLAP)** is an OLAP server architecture that combines elements of both MOLAP and ROLAP to leverage their respective strengths. It is considered hybrid because: 1. **Detail / base-level data is stored in relational tables** (ROLAP style) вҖ” preserving storage efficiency and allowing drill-down to atomic facts. 2. **Frequently queried aggregations are pre-computed and stored in a multi-dimensional cube** (MOLAP style) вҖ” preserving query speed for summary analytics. 3. The server uses an **intelligent query router** that directs aggregate queries to the MOLAP cube and detail queries to the ROLAP tables, presenting a unified view to the end user. Thus HOLAP achieves the **storage efficiency of ROLAP** and the **query performance of MOLAP** simultaneously, making it suitable for large enterprise warehouses with mixed query workloads. --- ### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Module 4, Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Explain the three OLAP server architectures вҖ” MOLAP, ROLAP, and HOLAP вҖ” with neat diagrams. Discuss their respective strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a data cube with the following three dimensions: - Time (with 4 levels: Day, Month, Quarter, Year; assume 365 days) - Product (with 3 levels: SKU, Category, Department; assume 200 SKUs) - Geography (with 3 levels: City, State, Country; assume 30 cities) Compute the following: 1. Total number of cells including the ALL-level. 2. Number of distinct non-empty aggregates. 3. Sparsity if only 25,000 cells contain non-zero values. 4. Storage required in MOLAP (assume 8 bytes per cell). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) The Three OLAP Server Architectures:** **MOLAP (Multidimensional OLAP):** MOLAP uses a **proprietary, multi-dimensional array** to store pre-computed aggregates. The ETL process loads data from the warehouse into this array, and the server pre-computes **every possible aggregation** along every dimension hierarchy. At query time, the user's request is mapped to cell coordinates and the pre-computed value is fetched in sub-second time. *Strengths:* Fastest query response; handles complex multi-dimensional calculations natively. *Weaknesses:* Wastes storage on sparse data; cube rebuilds are expensive; limited scalability (~50 GB). ``` [Diagram: ETL -> Multi-dim Array -> Pre-compute -> Cube File -> Query -> Cell Fetch] ``` **ROLAP (Relational OLAP):** ROLAP keeps the data in **relational star-schema tables** in the RDBMS. The ROLAP server sits as a middleware that translates multi-dimensional queries into **optimized SQL** (with JOINs, GROUP BY). The RDBMS executes the SQL and returns the result. *Strengths:* Excellent scalability (TBвҖ“PB); efficient for sparse data; real-time updates. *Weaknesses:* Slower query performance; heavy reliance on RDBMS optimizer. ``` [Diagram: User -> MDX Parser -> SQL Generator -> RDBMS -> Result] ``` **HOLAP (Hybrid OLAP):** HOLAP stores **detail data in RDBMS** (ROLAP style) and **aggregates in a multi-dimensional cube** (MOLAP style). A query router directs aggregate queries to the cube and detail queries to the RDBMS. *Strengths:* Combines storage efficiency of ROLAP with query speed of MOLAP; ideal for enterprise mixed workloads. *Weaknesses:* Most complex to implement; data consistency between the two stores must be managed. ``` [Diagram: Query -> Router -> Cube OR RDBMS -> Unified Result] ``` **(b) Numerical Computation:** **1. Total number of cells (including ALL-level):** $$C_{total} = (d_1 + 1) \times (d_2 + 1) \times (d_3 + 1)$$ $$C_{total} = (365 + 1) \times (200 + 1) \times (30 + 1)$$ $$C_{total} = 366 \times 201 \times 31$$ $$C_{total} = 366 \times 6231 = 2{,}280{,}546 \text{ cells}$$ **2. Number of distinct non-empty aggregates:** $$A = (L_1 + 1) \times (L_2 + 1) \times (L_3 + 1) - 1$$ $$A = (4 + 1) \times (3 + 1) \times (3 + 1) - 1$$ $$A = 5 \times 4 \times 4 - 1 = 80 - 1 = 79 \text{ aggregates}$$ **3. Sparsity calculation:** $$S = 1 - \frac{N_{actual}}{C_{total}} = 1 - \frac{25{,}000}{2{,}280{,}546}$$ $$S = 1 - 0.01096 = 0.9890 \text{ (or } 98.90\%\text{)}$$ The cube is **extremely sparse** (вүҲ 99% empty cells). **4. MOLAP storage:** $$M_{molap} = C_{total} \times 8 \text{ bytes} = 2{,}280{,}546 \times 8 = 18{,}244{,}368 \text{ bytes} \approx 17.40 \text{ MB}$$ Storage for **actual data only** in ROLAP would be: $25{,}000 \times 64 = 1.6 \text{ MB}$, demonstrating MOLAP's inefficiency in this scenario. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Module 4, June 2024 Style]** **(a)** Define a **data cube** and explain the OLAP operations of **slice, dice, drill-down, and roll-up** with an example cube of Sales(Geography Г— Product Г— Time). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare MOLAP, ROLAP, and HOLAP across the dimensions of: (i) storage structure, (ii) query response time, (iii) scalability, (iv) handling of sparse data, (v) data freshness, and (vi) implementation complexity. Which architecture would you recommend for a mid-sized retail chain with daily updates and 5 years of transaction data, and why? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Data Cube and OLAP Operations:** A **data cube** is a multi-dimensional array of values that allows data to be modeled and viewed in multiple dimensions. A 3-D sales cube with axes *Geography* (City), *Product* (Item), and *Time* (Month) вҖ” with measure *Sales* вҖ” has cells like *Sales(Mumbai, Laptop, Jan)*. **OLAP Operations:** 1. **Slice:** Fixing one dimension to a single value. *Example:* `Sales(Time = "Jan", Product, Geography)` вҖ” produces a 2-D plane of January sales across all products and regions. 2. **Dice:** Selecting a sub-cube by bounding values on multiple dimensions. *Example:* `Sales(Time вҲҲ {Jan, Feb}, Product вҲҲ {Laptop, Phone}, Geography вҲҲ {Mumbai, Delhi})` вҖ” produces a small 2Г—2Г—2 sub-cube. 3. **Roll-Up (Aggregation):** Moving up a dimension hierarchy to a coarser level. *Example:* `Sales(City вҶ’ State вҶ’ Country)` вҖ” Mumbai + Delhi rolls up to Maharashtra; further rolls to India. 4. **Drill-Down (Dis-aggregation):** Moving down a dimension hierarchy to finer granularity. *Example:* `Sales(Year вҶ’ Quarter вҶ’ Month вҶ’ Day)` вҖ” India annual sales drills down to daily sales in Q1. **(b) Comprehensive Comparison:** | Dimension | MOLAP | ROLAP | HOLAP | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Storage Structure | Proprietary multi-dim array | Relational tables (star schema) | Mixed: detail in RDBMS, aggregates in cube | | (ii) Query Response Time | Sub-second (fastest) | Seconds to minutes (slowest) | Fast for aggregates, slower for detail | | (iii) Scalability | Limited (вүӨ 50 GB) | Excellent (TBвҖ“PB) | Very good (TB+) | | (iv) Sparse Data Handling | Poor вҖ” wastes space | Excellent вҖ” only stores facts | Good вҖ” sparse detail in ROLAP, dense aggregates in MOLAP | | (v) Data Freshness | Stale until cube rebuilt | Real-time (live RDBMS) | Eventual consistency | | (vi) Implementation Complexity | Moderate | Moderate | Highest | **Recommendation вҖ” Mid-Sized Retail Chain:** For a retail chain with **5 years of daily transaction data** and **daily updates**: - **Storage size estimate:** A 5-year retail transaction history with ~10,000 SKUs, 500 stores, 1,825 days yields a cube with $C_{total} \approx 1.83 \times 10^{10}$ cells вҖ” far too large for pure MOLAP. - **Daily updates** require near-real-time data, which ROLAP supports naturally. - **Managerial dashboards** need fast aggregate queries (monthly, regional sales) вҖ” which MOLAP cubes deliver instantly. **вҶ’ Recommended Architecture: HOLAP** **Justification:** HOLAP stores the daily transactional detail in the RDBMS (supporting real-time updates and rare drill-downs), while pre-computing common rollups (monthly store sales, regional quarterly totals) in a MOLAP cube for instant dashboard rendering. This balances scalability, freshness, and speed вҖ” the ideal compromise for a mid-sized retailer. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "MOLAP is best"** вҖ” students often write that MOLAP is universally superior. You **must** discuss the sparsity trade-off to score above 10/14. > 2. **Always draw a diagram** for the architecture comparison. A question without a diagram typically loses 2вҖ“3 marks. > 3. **Numerical computation errors** вҖ” forgetting the **+1 (ALL-level)** in $C_{total}$ is the most common mistake. Marks are deducted per step, so show all intermediate work. > 4. **Do not write only advantages** of one architecture вҖ” KTU expects a **balanced comparison** with limitations of each. > 5. **Avoid generic statements** like "MOLAP is fast" вҖ” quantify with terms like "sub-second" or "pre-computed cell access." --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute revision вҖ” cover these bullets the night before the exam.** - [x] **OLAP** = Online Analytical Processing вҖ” multi-dimensional, decision-support queries against a data warehouse. - [x] **Three architectures:** MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP. - [x] **MOLAP** = Multi-dimensional OLAP вҶ’ proprietary cube file вҶ’ **pre-computed aggregates** вҶ’ **fastest query**, **worst sparsity handling**. - [x] **ROLAP** = Relational OLAP вҶ’ star schema in RDBMS вҶ’ **SQL generation on-the-fly** вҶ’ **best scalability**, **slowest query**. - [x] **HOLAP** = Hybrid OLAP вҶ’ detail in RDBMS + aggregates in cube вҶ’ **balanced speed and scalability** вҶ’ **most complex**. - [x] **Cube size formula:** $C_{total} = \prod (d_i + 1)$ вҖ” **always include the +1** for the ALL-level. - [x] **Aggregate count:** $A = \prod (L_i + 1) - 1$ вҖ” subtract 1 to exclude the empty combination. - [x] **Sparsity:** $S = 1 - N_{actual} / C_{total}$. When $S > 0.90$, **avoid pure MOLAP**. - [x] **OLAP operations:** **Slice** (fix 1 dimension), **Dice** (sub-cube), **Roll-Up** (coarser), **Drill-Down** (finer). - [x] **Decision rule of thumb:** - Dense + small cube вҶ’ **MOLAP** - Sparse + huge data вҶ’ **ROLAP** - Mixed workloads + enterprise scale вҶ’ **HOLAP** - [x] **KTU 2024 trend:** Expect a 14-mark comparison question + 7-mark numerical (cube cell/sparsity) in the End Semester Exam. --- > **End of Module 4 Notes вҖ” OLAP Architectures: MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework. Aligned with PECDT632 Data Warehousing syllabus.*

topics.title: Operations: Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, Dicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations: Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, Dicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to OLAP Operations

In the context of **Data Warehousing**, Module 4 introduces the four fundamental **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) operations** that allow decision-makers to navigate through multidimensional data cubes. These operations transform the way business analysts interact with historical data, enabling them to view information from multiple perspectives without writing complex SQL queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OLAP Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**: OLAP is a category of software tools that provides analysis of multidimensional data stored in a data warehouse, allowing users to interactively explore data through operations such as Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, and Dicing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Data GPS" Analogy

Imagine a data cube as a **3D city map** displayed on your screen:

- **Roll-Up** is like **zooming out** on Google Maps. You start by looking at a specific street (detailed data), then zoom out to see the city, then the state, and finally the entire country (summarized/aggregated data).
- **Drill-Down** is like **zooming in**. You start with a country view, then drill into a state, then a city, and finally a specific street (detailed data).
- **Slicing** is like **cutting the cube with a knife along one dimension**. Imagine cutting a 3D cake horizontally вҖ” you get a single 2D flat layer.
- **Dicing** is like **cutting a small 3D block from the cube**. You extract a smaller cube by selecting ranges on multiple dimensions simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight**: All four operations preserve the original data structure. They simply provide different "views" or "perspectives" of the same underlying data warehouse, making analysis intuitive and fast.

## 1.3 Formal Definitions of the Four Operations

### A. Roll-Up (Drill-Up / Consolidation)
**Roll-Up** is the OLAP operation that **aggregates data** by climbing up a concept hierarchy or by reducing the number of dimensions. It summarizes data from a finer granularity to a coarser granularity.

**Example**: Sales by City вҶ’ Sales by State вҶ’ Sales by Country

### B. Drill-Down (Drill-Down / Navigation)
**Drill-Down** is the reverse of Roll-Up. It **decomposes** aggregated data into more detailed levels by descending down a concept hierarchy or by adding new dimensions.

**Example**: Sales by Year вҶ’ Sales by Quarter вҶ’ Sales by Month вҶ’ Sales by Day

### C. Slicing
**Slicing** is the operation that selects **one specific value** for one dimension, resulting in a reduced sub-cube (typically a single 2D plane). It performs a "selection" on exactly one dimension.

**Example**: Viewing sales data only for the year **"2024"** while keeping all other dimensions intact.

### D. Dicing
**Dicing** defines a **sub-cube** by selecting two or more dimensions simultaneously. It performs a selection on multiple dimensions at once, producing a smaller, focused cube.

**Example**: Viewing sales data for **"2024"** AND **"Kerala"** AND **"Electronics"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: Slicing reduces dimensionality by ONE (e.g., 3D вҶ’ 2D), while Dicing reduces the SIZE of the cube without changing the number of dimensions. Students often confuse these two!

## 1.4 The OLAP Cube Foundation

All four operations operate on a **data cube**, which is a multidimensional array of values. The cube is defined by:

- **Dimensions**: The perspectives or entities for analysis (e.g., Time, Location, Product).
- **Measures**: The numerical values to be analyzed (e.g., Sales, Profit, Quantity).
- **Hierarchies**: Levels within a dimension (e.g., Day вҶ’ Month вҶ’ Quarter вҶ’ Year).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Cube Representation**: A 3D cube with dimensions $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$ each having $n_1$, $n_2$, $n_3$ levels respectively contains $\vert D_1 \vert \times \vert D_2 \vert \times \vert D_3 \vert$ cells, where $\vert D_i \vert$ represents the cardinality of dimension $i$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Granularity Concept

**Granularity** refers to the level of detail or specificity captured in a data record. It is the foundation upon which all four OLAP operations work.

- **Fine Granularity (Detailed Data)**: Day-level sales, individual transactions.
- **Coarse Granularity (Summarized Data)**: Yearly totals, regional aggregates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four OLAP operations are essentially **granularity manipulation tools**. Roll-Up increases granularity coarseness; Drill-Down increases granularity fineness; Slicing and Dicing filter granularity.

## 2.2 Structured Logic for Each Operation

### 2.2.1 Roll-Up Operation вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the dimension** along which aggregation must occur.
2. **Select the aggregation function**: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX.
3. **Apply the concept hierarchy** to move from a child level to a parent level.
4. **Group data** by the new higher-level category.
5. **Compute the aggregated measure** for each group.

**Hierarchy Example for Time Dimension**:

$$ \text{Day} \rightarrow \text{Month} \rightarrow \text{Quarter} \rightarrow \text{Year} \rightarrow \text{Decade} $$

**Hierarchy Example for Location Dimension**:

$$ \text{City} \rightarrow \text{State} \rightarrow \text{Country} \rightarrow \text{Continent} $$

### 2.2.2 Drill-Down Operation вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the current aggregation level** (parent in hierarchy).
2. **Select a specific parent value** to expand (e.g., Year = 2024).
3. **Move down one level** in the concept hierarchy (e.g., from Year to Quarter).
4. **Disaggregate the measure** into its constituent values.
5. **Repeat** the process if further granularity is required.

### 2.2.3 Slicing Operation вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Choose one dimension** to filter.
2. **Specify a single value** (or a small range) for that dimension.
3. **Fix the dimension** at the chosen value.
4. **Reduce the dimensionality** of the cube by one.

**Mathematical Representation**:

$$ \text{Slice}(C, D_i = v) = \{ m \in C \mid m.D_i = v \} $$

where $C$ is the cube, $D_i$ is the chosen dimension, and $v$ is the filter value.

### 2.2.4 Dicing Operation вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Choose two or more dimensions** to filter.
2. **Specify a range or set of values** for each chosen dimension.
3. **Apply all filters simultaneously** (logical AND).
4. **Extract a sub-cube** that matches all criteria.

**Mathematical Representation**:

$$ \text{Dice}(C, \phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_k) = \{ m \in C \mid \phi_1(m) \land \phi_2(m) \land \ldots \land \phi_k(m) \} $$

where $\phi_i$ is a predicate (filter condition) on dimension $i$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Operation** | **Direction** | **Dimensionality Change** | **Aggregation Required** | **Hierarchy Involved** | **Result Type** |
|---------------|---------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Roll-Up | Coarsening (up) | Same / Reduced | YES (SUM, AVG, COUNT) | YES | Smaller dataset, summarized |
| Drill-Down | Refining (down) | Same / Increased | NO (decomposition) | YES | Larger dataset, detailed |
| Slicing | Filtering | Reduced by 1 | NO | NO | 2D plane (one dimension fixed) |
| Dicing | Filtering | Same (n-D) | NO | NO | Sub-cube (n-D, smaller volume) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction**: Slicing always reduces the number of dimensions by exactly one. Dicing keeps the same number of dimensions but reduces the size (cardinality) of the cube.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Operation Used** |
|------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Retail Analytics | Analyzing sales trends from year вҶ’ quarter вҶ’ month вҶ’ day | Roll-Up, Drill-Down |
| Healthcare | Filtering patient data by region and disease type | Dicing |
| Banking | Viewing transactions for a specific branch (Q1 2024 only) | Slicing |
| Telecom | Aggregating call data from city вҶ’ state вҶ’ country level | Roll-Up |
| Manufacturing | Examining defect rates for specific product lines in specific months | Dicing |
| E-Commerce | Daily drill-down into hourly sales spikes during a campaign | Drill-Down |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: In Industry 4.0 environments, these OLAP operations form the analytical backbone of **Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)** and **Supply Chain Decision Support Systems (DSS)**. They enable real-time drill-down from KPI dashboards to root-cause transactional events.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Base Dataset Setup

To make the four operations crystal clear, we will use a simple sales data scenario. Consider a company selling electronic products across different cities and quarters.

**Base Sales Cube (3-Dimensional)**:
- **Dimensions**: `Time (Quarter)`, `Location (City)`, `Product (Category)`
- **Measure**: `Sales Amount (in вӮ№ Lakhs)`

### Initial 3D Sales Data Cube

| **Quarter \ City** | **Kochi** | **Chennai** | **Bangalore** | **Mumbai** |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| **Q1**           | 50        | 60          | 55            | 70         |
| **Q2**           | 65        | 70          | 60            | 80         |
| **Q3**           | 80        | 75          | 90            | 85         |
| **Q4**           | 95        | 85          | 100           | 90         |

This is a 2D slice of the cube showing **Product = Electronics**. We will perform all four operations on this base.

## 3.2 Operation 1: Roll-Up (Aggregation)

**Scenario**: The manager wants to see **annual sales** instead of quarterly sales.

**Concept Hierarchy Used**: Quarter вҶ’ Year

**Step-by-Step Aggregation**:

$$\text{Sales}_{Year} = \sum_{i=Q1}^{Q4} \text{Sales}_{Q_i}$$

For each city, we sum across all four quarters:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Kochi Annual} &= 50 + 65 + 80 + 95 = 290 \\
\text{Chennai Annual} &= 60 + 70 + 75 + 85 = 290 \\
\text{Bangalore Annual} &= 55 + 60 + 90 + 100 = 305 \\
\text{Mumbai Annual} &= 70 + 80 + 85 + 90 = 325
\end{aligned}$$

**Resulting Rolled-Up Table (Year-level granularity)**:

| **Year \ City**   | **Kochi** | **Chennai** | **Bangalore** | **Mumbai** |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| **2024 (Annual)** | 290       | 290         | 305           | 325        |

**Total Annual Sales**:
$$\text{Total} = 290 + 290 + 305 + 325 = 1210 \text{ вӮ№ Lakhs}$$

## 3.3 Operation 2: Drill-Down (Disaggregation)

**Scenario**: The manager now wants to drill from **annual sales** back to **quarterly sales** to investigate the Q3 spike in Bangalore.

**Concept Hierarchy Used**: Year вҶ’ Quarter

This is the exact **reverse** of the Roll-Up operation. The result is the original quarterly table.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Q3 Bangalore Spike} &= 90 \text{ (Original)} \\
\text{Avg of Q1, Q2, Q4} &= \frac{55 + 60 + 100}{3} = 71.67 \\
\text{Spike Magnitude} &= 90 - 71.67 = 18.33 \text{ (above average)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight from Drill-Down**: The Q3 spike in Bangalore (вӮ№90L) is **25.6% higher** than the average of the other three quarters, indicating a possible festival season effect that requires further root-cause analysis.

## 3.4 Operation 3: Slicing (Dimensionality Reduction)

**Scenario**: The analyst wants to view **only Q1 sales** across all cities and all products.

**Dimension Chosen**: Time
**Value Chosen**: Q1

**Resulting 2D Slice** (dimensionality reduced from 3D to 2D):

| **Product \ City (Q1 Only)** | **Kochi** | **Chennai** | **Bangalore** | **Mumbai** |
|------------------------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| Electronics                  | 50        | 60          | 55            | 70         |
| Furniture                    | 30        | 35          | 25            | 40         |
| Clothing                     | 20        | 25          | 30            | 35         |

**Mathematical Formulation**:

$$\text{Slice}(C, \text{Time} = Q1) = \{(p, c, s) \in C \mid p.\text{Time} = Q1\}$$

where $p$ is a product, $c$ is a city, and $s$ is the sales value.

## 3.5 Operation 4: Dicing (Sub-Cube Extraction)

**Scenario**: The regional manager of South India wants to analyze **only Q1 and Q2 sales** for **Electronics and Furniture** in **Kochi and Chennai**.

**Dimensions Involved**: Time, Product, Location
**Filter Predicates**:
- $\phi_1$: Time $\in \{Q1, Q2\}$
- $\phi_2$: Product $\in \{\text{Electronics, Furniture}\}$
- $\phi_3$: Location $\in \{\text{Kochi, Chennai}\}$

**Mathematical Formulation**:

$$\text{Dice} = \{(t, p, l, s) \in C \mid t \in \{Q1, Q2\} \land p \in \{E, F\} \land l \in \{K, C\}\}$$

**Resulting Sub-Cube (Dice Result)**:

| **Quarter** | **Product**   | **Kochi** | **Chennai** |
|-------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| Q1          | Electronics   | 50        | 60          |
| Q1          | Furniture     | 30        | 35          |
| Q2          | Electronics   | 65        | 70          |
| Q2          | Furniture     | 40        | 45          |

**Volume Reduction Calculation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Cube Volume} &= 4 \times 3 \times 4 = 48 \text{ cells} \\
\text{Dice Volume} &= 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8 \text{ cells} \\
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{48 - 8}{48} \times 100\% = 83.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix for the Four Operations

| **Parameter**          | **Roll-Up**               | **Drill-Down**              | **Slicing**            | **Dicing**                 |
|------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Purpose**            | Summarize data            | Disaggregate data            | Filter by one dim      | Filter by multiple dims    |
| **Hierarchy Used**     | YES                       | YES                          | NO                     | NO                         |
| **Aggregation**        | YES (SUM, AVG)            | NO                           | NO                     | NO                         |
| **Dimensions After**   | Same or fewer             | Same or more                 | Reduced by 1           | Same count                 |
| **Result Size**        | Smaller rows              | Larger rows                  | Smaller cube           | Smaller sub-cube           |
| **Typical SQL**        | `GROUP BY` with `SUM`     | Reverse of GROUP BY          | `WHERE` single column  | `WHERE` multiple columns   |
| **Cube Dimensionality**| 3D вҶ’ 3D (coarser)         | 3D вҶ’ 3D (finer)              | 3D вҶ’ 2D                | 3D вҶ’ 3D (smaller volume)   |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: When answering theory questions, always explicitly state the **direction** (coarsening vs. refining) and the **change in number of dimensions** (reduced vs. same) for each operation. This shows conceptual clarity.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OLAP Operations Overview Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the four OLAP operations relate to each other and the cube structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OLAP Data Cube] --> B[Roll-Up Operation]
    A --> C[Drill-Down Operation]
    A --> D[Slicing Operation]
    A --> E[Dicing Operation]

    B --> B1[Aggregate via SUM, AVG, COUNT]
    B1 --> B2[Move UP hierarchy]
    B2 --> B3[Result: Coarser Granularity]

    C --> C1[Disaggregate Data]
    C1 --> C2[Move DOWN hierarchy]
    C2 --> C3[Result: Finer Granularity]

    D --> D1[Select ONE dimension]
    D1 --> D2[Fix ONE value]
    D2 --> D3[Result: 2D Plane Slice]

    E --> E1[Select MULTIPLE dimensions]
    E1 --> E2[Apply Range Filters]
    E2 --> E3[Result: Sub-Cube Block]

    B3 --> F[Smaller, Summarized View]
    C3 --> G[Larger, Detailed View]
    D3 --> H[Flat 2D View]
    E3 --> I[Focused 3D Block]
```

## 4.2 Cube Dimensionality Change Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original_Cube
        OC[Original 3D Cube: Time x Product x Location]
    end

    subgraph After_Slicing
        AS[2D Plane: Product x Location, Time fixed at Q1]
    end

    subgraph After_Dicing
        AD[3D Sub-Cube: Time x Product x Location, all dimensions filtered]
    end

    subgraph After_Roll_Up
        AR[Coarser 3D Cube: Year x Product x Region]
    end

    subgraph After_Drill_Down
        AD2[Finer 3D Cube: Month x Product x City]
    end

    OC -->|Slice Time = Q1| AS
    OC -->|Dice with filters| AD
    OC -->|Roll-Up to Year and Region| AR
    OC -->|Drill-Down to Month and City| AD2
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy Traversal Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Time_Hierarchy
        T1[Year] --> T2[Quarter]
        T2 --> T3[Month]
        T3 --> T4[Day]
    end

```

## 4.4 Operation Decision Flowchart for KTU Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Question: Which OLAP Operation?] --> Q1{Does it involve aggregation?}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Which direction in hierarchy?}
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3{How many dimensions are fixed?}

    Q2 -->|UP| ROLL[Answer: Roll-Up]
    Q2 -->|DOWN| DRILL[Answer: Drill-Down]

    Q3 -->|ONE dimension| SLICE[Answer: Slicing]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Slicing and Dicing operations in OLAP with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| **Slicing**                                                  | **Dicing**                                                  |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
| Selects a **single value** of **one dimension**              | Selects **multiple values** across **two or more dimensions**|
| Reduces cube dimensionality by **exactly 1** (e.g., 3D вҶ’ 2D) | Keeps the same number of dimensions but **reduces volume**   |
| Result is a **2D plane**                                     | Result is a **sub-cube (n-D block)**                        |
| **Example**: Viewing sales for Q1 only (Time = Q1)           | **Example**: Sales for Q1+Q2 in Kochi+Chennai for Electronics |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for dimensionality difference, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: Explain the Roll-Up operation in OLAP. How is it different from Drill-Down?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Roll-Up** is an OLAP operation that **aggregates data** by moving **up** a concept hierarchy, summarizing data from a finer to a coarser granularity. Example: Daily sales вҶ’ Monthly sales вҶ’ Quarterly sales вҶ’ Yearly sales.

**Drill-Down** is the **reverse** operation that **decomposes** aggregated data by moving **down** a concept hierarchy, increasing the level of detail. Example: Yearly sales вҶ’ Quarterly sales вҶ’ Monthly sales.

**Key Difference**: Roll-Up **aggregates** (uses functions like SUM, AVG), whereas Drill-Down **disaggregates** (shows constituent values without further aggregation).

*(Award 1 mark each for Roll-Up definition, Drill-Down definition, and key difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Explain the four basic OLAP operations: Roll-Up, Drill-Down, Slicing, and Dicing with a suitable example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 3D data cube with dimensions **Time (Year, Quarter, Month)**, **Product (Category, Sub-Category)**, and **Location (Country, State, City)**. Identify which OLAP operation is being performed in the following scenarios: **(7 Marks)**

  1. Viewing sales for the year 2024 only, broken down by all states.
  2. Aggregating monthly sales into quarterly sales.
  3. Viewing sales for Kerala and Tamil Nadu, for Electronics and Furniture, in Q1 and Q2 of 2024.
  4. Breaking down yearly sales into quarterly sales.
  5. Viewing the entire dataset for the year 2023, all products, all locations.
  6. Converting city-level sales into state-level sales.
  7. Viewing data for Mumbai city, for the month of March 2024, for Clothing products.

#### Model Solution for (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

**1. Roll-Up (2 Marks)**:
Roll-Up aggregates data by climbing up a concept hierarchy. It reduces granularity.
- *Example*: Sales by City вҶ’ Sales by State вҶ’ Sales by Country.
- *Aggregation Function*: SUM, AVG, COUNT.

**2. Drill-Down (2 Marks)**:
Drill-Down is the inverse of Roll-Up. It navigates down a hierarchy to expose more detail.
- *Example*: Yearly sales вҶ’ Quarterly sales вҶ’ Monthly sales вҶ’ Daily sales.

**3. Slicing (1.5 Marks)**:
Slicing selects a single value of one dimension, producing a 2D plane.
- *Example*: Viewing the entire sales data only for the year "2024" (Time dimension fixed).

**4. Dicing (1.5 Marks)**:
Dicing selects ranges on two or more dimensions, producing a sub-cube.
- *Example*: Viewing sales for "2024" + "Kerala" + "Electronics" simultaneously.

#### Model Solution for (b) вҖ” 7 Marks (1 Mark Each)

| **Scenario** | **Operation** | **Justification** |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1. Sales for 2024, broken by states | **Slicing** | Time fixed to 2024 (1 dim fixed), other dims vary |
| 2. Monthly вҶ’ Quarterly aggregation | **Roll-Up** | Aggregation, moving UP in Time hierarchy |
| 3. Kerala+Tamil Nadu, Electronics+Furniture, Q1+Q2 2024 | **Dicing** | Multiple dimensions filtered with ranges |
| 4. Yearly вҶ’ Quarterly breakdown | **Drill-Down** | Disaggregation, moving DOWN in Time hierarchy |
| 5. Entire dataset for 2023 | **Slicing** | Only Time fixed to 2023 |
| 6. City-level вҶ’ State-level aggregation | **Roll-Up** | Aggregation, moving UP in Location hierarchy |
| 7. Mumbai, March 2024, Clothing | **Dicing** | Multiple dimensions (Location, Time, Product) all fixed to single values |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Define a data cube. With a neat diagram, explain how Roll-Up and Drill-Down operations traverse the concept hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A retail company maintains a data warehouse with the following sample data:

| **Quarter** | **City**   | **Product**   | **Sales (вӮ№ Lakhs)** |
|-------------|------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Q1          | Kochi      | Electronics   | 50                  |
| Q1          | Chennai    | Electronics   | 60                  |
| Q2          | Kochi      | Electronics   | 65                  |
| Q2          | Chennai    | Electronics   | 70                  |
| Q1          | Kochi      | Furniture     | 30                  |
| Q1          | Chennai    | Furniture     | 35                  |
| Q2          | Kochi      | Furniture     | 40                  |
| Q2          | Chennai    | Furniture     | 45                  |

Perform the following operations and show the resulting tables:
  1. **Slice** for Q1 only. **(2 Marks)**
  2. **Dice** for Kochi and Chennai, Q1 and Q2, Electronics only. **(2 Marks)**
  3. **Roll-Up** to compute annual sales per city. **(2 Marks)**
  4. **Drill-Down** to show quarterly sales from the annual Roll-Up result. **(1 Mark)**

#### Model Solution for (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

**Data Cube Definition (2 Marks)**:
A data cube is a multidimensional array of values organized along multiple dimensions. Each cell contains a measure (numeric fact) computed for a unique combination of dimension members.

**Concept Hierarchy Traversal (5 Marks)**:
- **Roll-Up** moves UP the hierarchy by applying aggregation (SUM, AVG).
- **Drill-Down** moves DOWN the hierarchy by reversing the aggregation.

**Diagram Description (Verbal representation for the answer sheet)**:

```
ROLL-UP Direction (UP, Aggregation):
Year вҶҗ Quarter вҶҗ Month вҶҗ Day
Country вҶҗ State вҶҗ City вҶҗ Store
Category вҶҗ Sub-Category вҶҗ Product вҶҗ SKU

DRILL-DOWN Direction (DOWN, Disaggregation):
Year вҶ’ Quarter вҶ’ Month вҶ’ Day
Country вҶ’ State вҶ’ City вҶ’ Store
Category вҶ’ Sub-Category вҶ’ Product вҶ’ SKU
```

#### Model Solution for (b) вҖ” 7 Marks

**1. Slice for Q1 only (2 Marks)**:

| **Quarter** | **City** | **Product**   | **Sales (вӮ№ Lakhs)** |
|-------------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| Q1          | Kochi    | Electronics   | 50                  |
| Q1          | Chennai  | Electronics   | 60                  |
| Q1          | Kochi    | Furniture     | 30                  |
| Q1          | Chennai  | Furniture     | 35                  |

This is a 2D slice (Time dimension fixed to Q1).

**2. Dice for Kochi+Chennai, Q1+Q2, Electronics only (2 Marks)**:

| **Quarter** | **City** | **Product**   | **Sales (вӮ№ Lakhs)** |
|-------------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| Q1          | Kochi    | Electronics   | 50                  |
| Q1          | Chennai  | Electronics   | 60                  |
| Q2          | Kochi    | Electronics   | 65                  |
| Q2          | Chennai  | Electronics   | 70                  |

This is a sub-cube with 4 cells (filtered on 3 dimensions).

**3. Roll-Up вҖ” Annual Sales per City (2 Marks)**:

For each city, sum across both quarters and both products:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Kochi Annual} &= (50 + 65) + (30 + 40) = 115 + 70 = 185 \\
\text{Chennai Annual} &= (60 + 70) + (35 + 45) = 130 + 80 = 210
\end{aligned}$$

| **Year** | **City**   | **Total Sales (вӮ№ Lakhs)** |
|----------|------------|--------------------------|
| 2024     | Kochi      | 185                      |
| 2024     | Chennai    | 210                      |

**4. Drill-Down from Annual to Quarterly (1 Mark)**:

| **Year** | **Quarter** | **City**   | **Total Sales (вӮ№ Lakhs)** |
|----------|-------------|------------|--------------------------|
| 2024     | Q1          | Kochi      | 50 + 30 = 80             |
| 2024     | Q2          | Kochi      | 65 + 40 = 105            |
| 2024     | Q1          | Chennai    | 60 + 35 = 95             |
| 2024     | Q2          | Chennai    | 70 + 45 = 115            |

Drill-down disaggregates вӮ№185 (Kochi annual) into вӮ№80 (Q1) + вӮ№105 (Q2) = вӮ№185 вң“
