## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing Slicing with Dicing (Most Common)**: Students often write that Slicing filters multiple dimensions. WRONG вҖ” Slicing fixes exactly ONE dimension; Dicing fixes TWO or more.
> 2. **Forgetting Aggregation in Roll-Up**: Simply writing "Roll-Up moves up" without mentioning **SUM, AVG, or COUNT** loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing the Direction**: Roll-Up goes **UP** the hierarchy (coarser); Drill-Down goes **DOWN** (finer). Reversing this is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not Mentioning Hierarchy**: A definition of Roll-Up/Drill-Down that does not reference the **concept hierarchy** is incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring SQL Equivalents**: Examiners appreciate when students mention the equivalent SQL (e.g., Roll-Up вүҲ `GROUP BY` with `SUM`).
> 6. **Slicing Dimensionality Mistake**: Forgetting to mention that Slicing reduces dimensionality by 1 (3D вҶ’ 2D).
> 7. **No Examples in Theory Answers**: KTU examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for a "suitable example". Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**:
> - [ ] **Roll-Up** = Aggregate (SUM/AVG) + Move UP hierarchy + Coarser granularity
> - [ ] **Drill-Down** = Disaggregate + Move DOWN hierarchy + Finer granularity
> - [ ] **Slicing** = Fix ONE dimension to ONE value + Reduces dimensions by 1
> - [ ] **Dicing** = Fix MULTIPLE dimensions to ranges/values + Sub-cube extraction
> - [ ] Roll-Up and Drill-Down use the **concept hierarchy**; Slicing and Dicing do NOT
> - [ ] Slicing produces a **2D plane**; Dicing produces an **n-D sub-cube**
> - [ ] The **measure** (e.g., Sales) remains the same across all four operations; only the **view** changes
> - [ ] All four operations are **read-only**; they do not modify the underlying data warehouse
> - [ ] SQL equivalents: Roll-Up вүҲ `GROUP BY` + aggregate; Drill-Down вүҲ reverse of grouping; Slicing вүҲ `WHERE` on one column; Dicing вүҲ `WHERE` on multiple columns
> - [ ] In a 3D cube of size $a \times b \times c$, Slicing reduces to $b \times c$ (2D), while Dicing reduces to $a' \times b' \times c'$ where $a' \leq a$, $b' \leq b$, $c' \leq c$
> - [ ] Always state the **concept hierarchy** explicitly in Roll-Up/Drill-Down answers
> - [ ] Always provide a **real-world example** in long-answer questions to score full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Maintenance вҖ“ Data Loading Automation, Backup, and Recovery, Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT632 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Maintenance вҖ“ Data Loading Automation, Backup, and Recovery, Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Warehouse Maintenance вҖ“ Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Data Warehouse Maintenance** is the continuous, systematic process of preserving, refreshing, optimizing, and evolving a data warehouse (DW) to ensure that it remains **available, consistent, performant, and aligned with evolving business requirements** throughout its operational lifetime. It encompasses data loading automation, backup and recovery, performance tuning, and strategic evolution.

The four pillars of DW Maintenance as defined under the KTU 2024 PECDT632 syllabus are:

1. **Data Loading Automation** вҖ“ Scheduling, orchestrating, and monitoring the movement of data from heterogeneous sources into the DW.
2. **Backup and Recovery** вҖ“ Protecting the DW against logical, physical, and operational failures through systematic redundancy.
3. **Data Warehouse Evolution** вҖ“ Adapting the DW schema, structure, and scope to match changing business and regulatory demands.
4. **Data Warehouse Scalability** вҖ“ Architecting the DW to gracefully handle growing data volumes, user concurrency, and query complexity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ“ "The Bank Vault Analogy"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Understanding (Real-World Analogy)**
> Think of a **Data Warehouse as a massive, 24Г—7 Bank Vault** that stores the most valuable asset of an organization вҖ” its historical intelligence. **Maintenance** is everything the bank does to keep the vault *operational, secure, and growing*:
> - **Data Loading Automation** = The **armored trucks** arriving at scheduled times to deposit cash and gold (fresh transactional data) from multiple branches.
> - **Backup & Recovery** = The **duplicate vault in a separate, disaster-proof mountain location** with a documented drill to restore operations if the primary vault is compromised.
> - **Evolution** = The **periodic renovation** of the vault вҖ” adding new sections for digital currency (crypto) or new locker types as customer needs change.
> - **Scalability** = The **architectural decision** to build the vault on expandable land with reinforced foundations so a 10-storey building can become a 50-storey tower without collapsing.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Formal Meaning | Significance in DW Maintenance |
|------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| **ETL** | Extract, Transform, Load | Foundational automation pipeline for populating the DW |
| **ELT** | Extract, Load, Transform | Modern cloud-native variant leveraging DW compute power |
| **RPO** | Recovery Point Objective | Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time |
| **RTO** | Recovery Time Objective | Maximum acceptable downtime before service restoration |
| **SLA** | Service Level Agreement | Contractual performance/availability guarantee |
| **CDC** | Change Data Capture | Real-time mechanism to identify source data deltas |
| **MPP** | Massively Parallel Processing | Architecture enabling horizontal scalability |
| **Sharding** | Horizontal data partitioning | Distributing rows across multiple nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> As per the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, students must be able to **explain, compare, and recommend** maintenance strategies вҖ” not merely memorize them. Application-level questions carry the highest weightage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Data Loading Automation вҖ“ Conceptual Decomposition

Data Loading Automation is engineered through five sequential logic steps:

1. **Source Identification & Connectivity** вҖ“ Establishing ODBC/JDBC/API connectors to operational systems (OLTP, CRM, ERP, IoT streams).
2. **Extraction Strategy Selection** вҖ“ Choosing between *full extraction* (entire dataset) and *incremental extraction* (only deltas identified via CDC).
3. **Transformation Logic Application** вҖ“ Applying cleansing, deduplication, standardization, surrogate key assignment, slowly changing dimension (SCD) handling.
4. **Loading Mechanism Execution** вҖ“ Executing bulk loads via parallel inserts, partition exchanges, or trickle-feed micro-batches.
5. **Validation & Auditing** вҖ“ Running row-count reconciliation, hash totals, and business-rule checks post-load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these steps matter:** Automation is not merely "scheduling" вҖ” it is a **closed-loop control system** where monitoring outputs feed back into the next iteration, ensuring the DW never drifts from the source-of-truth.

### 2.2 Backup Strategies вҖ“ The 3-2-1 Rule

The industry-standard **3-2-1 Backup Rule** decomposes as:

- **3** copies of data (1 primary + 2 backups)
- **2** different storage media types (e.g., disk + tape, or on-prem + cloud)
- **1** copy stored offsite (geographically isolated)

### 2.3 Recovery Models in DW Context

| Recovery Model | Data Loss Exposure | Performance Impact | Use Case |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Full Recovery** | Zero (point-in-time) | High (transaction log intensive) | Mission-critical financial DWs |
| **Bulk-Logged Recovery** | Minimal (bulk operations) | Moderate | Periodic bulk loads in staging |
| **Simple Recovery** | Up to last full backup | Lowest | Dev/Test environments |

### 2.4 Scalability Dimensions

> [!NOTE]
> **Two orthogonal axes of scaling:**
> - **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up)** вҖ“ Increasing CPU, RAM, or storage of a single node.
> - **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out)** вҖ“ Adding more nodes to a distributed cluster.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Representation | Boundary / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|------------------|-------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Recovery Point Objective** | $RPO = T_{failure} - T_{last\_backup}$ | hours / minutes | Defines acceptable data loss |
| 2 | **Recovery Time Objective** | $RTO = T_{service\_restored} - T_{failure}$ | hours / minutes | Defines acceptable downtime |
| 3 | **Backup Window** | $B_{window} = T_{backup\_end} - T_{backup\_start}$ | hours | Maintenance window planning |
| 4 | **Storage Capacity Required** | $S_{total} = S_{raw} \times (1 + c_{replication} + c_{index})$ | GB / TB / PB | Capacity planning |
| 5 | **Data Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{V_{data}}{t_{load}}$ | GB/hr or rows/sec | ETL performance benchmarking |
| 6 | **Query Response Time (Amdahl's Law for DW)** | $S_{n} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | speedup ratio (dimensionless) | Predicts parallelism benefit |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio** | $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | ratio (typically 2:1 to 10:1) | Storage optimization |
| 8 | **Availability Percentage (Annual)** | $A = \dfrac{525600 - D_{downtime}}{525600} \times 100\%$ | percentage | SLA compliance calculation |
| 9 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total\_operation}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability engineering |
| 10 | **Mean Time To Recovery** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{recovery\_i}}{N_{incidents}}$ | hours | Maintainability index |
| 11 | **Cost per Terabyte** | $C_{TB} = \dfrac{C_{infra} + C_{ops} + C_{license}}{S_{usable\_TB}}$ | currency / TB | TCO modeling |
| 12 | **Linear Scalability Efficiency** | $\eta_{scale} = \dfrac{S_{n}}{n} \times 100\%$ | percentage | Measures parallel degradation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used by **Cloud Data Engineers** at organizations like Amazon Redshift, Snowflake, and Google BigQuery to design SLAs, negotiate contracts, and validate architectural decisions. KTU examiners frequently test *RPO/RTO* and *Amdahl's Law* as they map directly to Bloom's *Apply* and *Analyze* cognitive levels.

### 2.6 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Pillar

- **Why automate loading?** Manual loads are error-prone, non-reproducible, and unscalable. Automation provides **idempotency, observability, and SLA compliance**.
- **How does recovery work?** Recovery leverages **transaction logs, incremental backups, and standby replicas** to reconstruct a consistent state.
- **Why evolve?** Business dimensions change (new products, new geographies, new regulations like GDPR). Schema rigidity leads to **analytical debt**.
- **How to scale?** Modern DWs use **shared-nothing MPP architectures** where each node owns a disjoint slice of data and processes queries in parallel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Downtime from Availability SLA

> **Problem Context:** A DW SLA guarantees **99.99% ("Four-Nines")** availability. Compute the maximum permitted annual downtime in minutes.

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the total minutes in a non-leap year.**

$$T_{annual} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Express the availability as a decimal fraction.**

$$A_{decimal} = \dfrac{99.99}{100} = 0.9999$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Calculate the allowable downtime fraction.**

$$D_{fraction} = 1 - A_{decimal} = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Multiply by total minutes to obtain the maximum permissible downtime.**

$$D_{max} = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{max} = 52.56 \text{ minutes per year}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Convert to a more intuitive unit (per day).**

$$D_{per\_day} = \dfrac{52.56}{365} \approx 0.144 \text{ minutes/day} \approx 8.64 \text{ seconds/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A jump from 99.9% (Three-Nines, ~8.77 hrs/year) to 99.99% (Four-Nines, ~52.56 min/year) requires **an order-of-magnitude improvement** in redundancy and monitoring infrastructure, dramatically increasing TCO.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Applying Amdahl's Law to a Parallel DW Query

> **Problem Context:** A star-schema query has a parallelizable portion $p = 0.85$ (joins, aggregations) and a serial portion $(1-p) = 0.15$ (final result assembly). Predict the speedup when $n = 16$ parallel nodes are deployed.

**Step 1 вҖ” State Amdahl's Law formula.**

$$S_{n} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute the given values.**

$$S_{16} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{16}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the denominator terms.**

$$1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

$$\dfrac{0.85}{16} = 0.053125$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Sum the denominator.**

$$0.15 + 0.053125 = 0.203125$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Take the reciprocal to obtain the speedup.**

$$S_{16} = \dfrac{1}{0.203125} \approx 4.923$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Compute the theoretical maximum (as $n \to \infty$).**

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.667$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Calculate the linear scalability efficiency.**

$$\eta_{scale} = \dfrac{S_{16}}{16} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4.923}{16} \times 100\% \approx 30.77\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical insight for KTU:** The serial 15% of the query *capped* the maximum achievable speedup at **6.67Г—** вҖ” adding more than 16 nodes yields diminishing returns. This is why **partition pruning and predicate push-down** are crucial DW optimization techniques.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: RPO-Based Backup Frequency

> **Problem Context:** A financial DW has an RPO of **15 minutes**. Determine the minimum required frequency of incremental backups, given that each backup consumes 10 minutes and full backups are taken daily.

**Step 1 вҖ” Define RPO formally.**

$$RPO = T_{failure} - T_{last\_good\_backup}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Solve for the backup interval.**

$$\Delta T_{backup} = RPO - T_{execution\_margin}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Account for backup execution time as a safety margin.**

$$\Delta T_{backup} = 15 - 10 = 5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Compute the daily backup count.**

$$N_{daily} = \dfrac{24 \times 60}{5} = 288 \text{ incremental backups/day}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Compute the total daily backup storage.**

$$S_{daily} = (S_{full} + N_{daily} \times S_{incremental})$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Substituting typical values ($S_{full} = 2$ TB, $S_{incremental} = 5$ GB).**

$$S_{daily} = (2 \text{ TB}) + (288 \times 5 \text{ GB})$$

$$S_{daily} = 2000 \text{ GB} + 1440 \text{ GB} = 3440 \text{ GB} = 3.36 \text{ TB/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** An aggressive RPO of 15 minutes inflates storage costs by **~70%** over a daily-only strategy. This is a classic **cost-vs-resilience trade-off** that DW architects must justify to stakeholders.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ“ Comparative Strategy Analysis

| Evaluation Criterion | Manual Loading | Scheduled ETL (Cron) | Event-Driven CDC | Cloud Orchestration (Airflow) |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Latency** | HoursвҖ“Days | Hours | SecondsвҖ“Minutes | SecondsвҖ“Minutes |
| **Error Recovery** | Manual rollback | Re-run job | Replay event log | Auto-retry with backoff |
| **Observability** | Logs only | Basic logs | Stream metrics | Dashboards + alerts |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Limited | High | Elastic |
| **Operational Cost** | High (human) | Low | Medium | Pay-per-use |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Low | Medium | High | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to *compare* strategies, always structure your answer using a **tabular comparative matrix** вҖ” this is the exact format KTU examiners use as a valuation key for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Loading Automation вҖ“ End-to-End Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OLTP Source Systems] --> B[Change Data Capture CDC Engine]
    B --> C{Data Volume Check}
    C -- High Volume --> D[Batch Extract Phase]
    C -- Low Volume --> E[Streaming Extract Phase]
    D --> F[Staging Area]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Transformation Layer]
    G --> H[Data Quality Validation]
    H -- Pass --> I[Surrogate Key Assignment]
    H -- Fail --> J[Quarantine and Alert]
    I --> K[Dimension Tables Load]
    I --> L[Fact Tables Load]
    K --> M[DW Star Schema]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Post Load Reconciliation]
    N -- Match --> O[End Load Success Notification]
    N -- Mismatch --> P[Rollback and Re-trigger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart shows the closed-loop control nature of modern ETL вҖ” failures are **not silent**; they trigger explicit rollback paths, satisfying the observability requirement of NEP 2020's outcome-based pedagogy.

---

### 4.2 Backup and Recovery Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PrimarySite[Primary Data Center]
        P1[Production DW]
        P2[Transaction Log Stream]
        P3[Full Backup Repository]
    end
    subgraph SecondarySite[Disaster Recovery Site]
        S1[Standby DW]
        S2[Incremental Backup Log]
        S3[Snapshot Store]
    end
    subgraph CloudTier[Cloud Archive Tier]
        C1[Cold Storage Vault]
        C2[Compliance Archive]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> S1
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

---

### 4.3 Data Warehouse Evolution Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial DW Deployment] --> B[Requirement Drift Detected]
    B --> C[Impact Analysis]
    C --> D{Schema Change Type}
    D -- Add Dimension --> E[Schema Extension]
    D -- New Fact --> F[Schema Refactoring]
    D -- New Data Source --> G[Pipeline Integration]
    E --> H[Versioned Migration]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Regression Testing]
    I -- Pass --> J[Production Cutover]
    I -- Fail --> K[Rollback to Prior Version]
    J --> L[Continuous Monitoring]
    L --> B
```

---

### 4.4 Scalability Architecture вҖ“ Scale-Out Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query Coordinator Node] --> N1[Compute Node 1]
    Q --> N2[Compute Node 2]
    Q --> N3[Compute Node 3]
    Q --> N4[Compute Node n]
    N1 --> S1[Shared Storage Layer 1]
    N2 --> S2[Shared Storage Layer 2]
    N3 --> S3[Shared Storage Layer 3]
    N4 --> S4[Shared Storage Layer n]
    S1 --> M[Metadata Catalog]
    S2 --> M
    S3 --> M
    S4 --> M
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural insight:** The diagram represents the **shared-nothing MPP architecture** (compute nodes have local data ownership) with a **shared metadata layer** (catalog). This is the dominant pattern in Snowflake, Redshift Spectrum, and Teradata вҖ” it is also a **high-yield KTU topic**.

---

### 4.5 Failure-to-Recovery State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Operational
    Operational --> Detected: Failure Event
    Detected --> Isolated: Quarantine Faulty Node
    Isolated --> Recovering: Activate Standby
    Recovering --> Validating: Integrity Checks
    Validating --> Operational: All Tests Pass
    Validating --> Escalated: Tests Fail
    Escalated --> Recovering: Manual Intervention
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define the 3-2-1 Backup Rule. Why is it considered the industry-standard minimum for Data Warehouse environments? (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **3-2-1 Backup Rule** mandates that:
> - **3** copies of the data be maintained (one production + two backups),
> - stored on **2** different types of media (e.g., disk and tape, or on-premise and cloud), and
> - with **1** copy kept offsite (geographically isolated from the primary).
>
> It is the industry-standard minimum because it simultaneously addresses the three primary failure classes вҖ” *logical corruption* (recoverable from any backup), *media failure* (different media), and *site-wide disaster* (offsite copy) вҖ” providing defence-in-depth without requiring exotic infrastructure. **(3 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Vertical Scaling and Horizontal Scaling in a Data Warehouse context. State one advantage of each. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up) | Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out) |
> |--------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
> | **Mechanism** | Add CPU/RAM/disk to a single node | Add more nodes to the cluster |
> | **Theoretical Limit** | Bounded by hardware ceiling | Theoretically unlimited |
> | **Advantage** | Simpler architecture, no data redistribution needed | Linear cost-scaling, fault-tolerance through replication |
>
> **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) вҖ” Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five stages of the ETL process** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how automation is achieved using modern orchestration tools. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DW query has 80% parallelizable work. Compute the speedup achieved with 8 parallel nodes using Amdahl's Law. What is the theoretical maximum speedup as $n \to \infty$? Comment on the scalability efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Five Stages of ETL with Automation (7 Marks):**

1. **Extract** вҖ” Data is pulled from heterogeneous source systems (RDBMS, flat files, APIs, streaming platforms). Modern tools use **log-based CDC** to capture only deltas, reducing network load. **(1 Mark)**

2. **Cleanse** вҖ” Removal of nulls, duplicates, and inconsistencies. Tools like **Informatica Data Quality** and **Talend** enforce rule-based validation. **(1 Mark)**

3. **Transform** вҖ” Business logic application: currency conversion, unit normalization, surrogate key generation, SCD Type 1/2/3 handling. **(1 Mark)**

4. **Load** вҖ” Bulk insert into staging вҶ’ dimension tables вҶ’ fact tables. Techniques include **partition exchange, direct-path inserts, and parallel DML**. **(1.5 Marks)**

5. **Audit & Publish** вҖ” Row-count checks, hash totals, and SLA dashboards. Failures trigger alerts via email/Slack/PagerDuty. **(1 Mark)**

6. **Automation via Orchestration** вҖ” Tools like **Apache Airflow, AWS Glue, Azure Data Factory** provide DAG-based scheduling, dependency management, automatic retries with exponential backoff, and centralized observability. **(1.5 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently *omit the "Audit" stage* and treat ETL as a 4-step process. This costs **1 mark** as audit is what distinguishes automated loading from mere data movement.

**(b) Amdahl's Law Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $p = 0.80$, $n = 8$, $(1-p) = 0.20$.

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the formula:**

$$S_{n} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute:**

$$S_{8} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \dfrac{1}{0.30}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the speedup:**

$$S_{8} = 3.33$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Theoretical maximum ($n \to \infty$):**

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Scalability efficiency:**

$$\eta_{scale} = \dfrac{3.33}{8} \times 100\% = 41.67\%$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Interpretation (2 Marks):** The 20% serial portion caps the speedup at 5Г—. Beyond 8 nodes, gains become negligible. Optimization strategies include *predicate push-down, partition pruning, and materialized views* to reduce the serial fraction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> A common error is computing $S_8 = 8 \times 0.80 = 6.4$, which is the **linear extrapolation** and *not* Amdahl's Law. Always use the full formula. Deduct **2 marks** for this conceptual error.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
>
> **(a)** Describe the **Backup and Recovery strategies** for a Data Warehouse. Compare Full, Differential, and Incremental backups with appropriate diagrams. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DW promises **99.95%** annual availability. Calculate the maximum permissible annual downtime. If a backup takes 25 minutes and the RPO is set to 30 minutes, determine the minimum backup frequency and the daily backup count. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Backup Strategies (7 Marks):**

1. **Full Backup** вҖ” Captures the entire DW at a point in time. **(1 Mark)**
   - *Pros:* Simple, self-contained restore.
   - *Cons:* High storage and time cost.

2. **Differential Backup** вҖ” Captures all changes **since the last full backup**. **(1 Mark)**
   - *Pros:* Faster than full; restore needs only the last full + last differential.
   - *Cons:* Backup size grows linearly with time.

3. **Incremental Backup** вҖ” Captures changes **since the last backup of any type**. **(1 Mark)**
   - *Pros:* Smallest, fastest backup.
   - *Cons:* Restore requires the last full + *all* subsequent incrementals (chain dependency).

4. **Comparative Table (3 Marks):**

| Strategy | Backup Size | Restore Speed | Storage Cost | Recovery Complexity |
|----------|-------------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Full | Largest | Fastest | Highest | Low |
| Differential | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Medium |
| Incremental | Smallest | Slowest | Lowest | High |

5. **DW-Specific Note (1 Mark):** Modern DWs favor *incremental + log shipping* for near-zero RPO, supplemented by *periodic full snapshots* for compliance.

**(b) Availability & Backup Frequency Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 вҖ” Compute total minutes in a year:**

$$T_{annual} = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Calculate allowed downtime fraction:**

$$D_{fraction} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Maximum annual downtime:**

$$D_{max} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to days:**

$$D_{days} = \dfrac{262.8}{24 \times 60} = 0.1825 \text{ days} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” RPO-based backup interval:**

$$\Delta T_{interval} = RPO - T_{backup} = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Daily backup count:**

$$N_{daily} = \dfrac{24 \times 60}{5} = 288 \text{ backups/day}$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Engineering comment (2 Marks):** While 288 backups/day is technically feasible, the operational overhead may necessitate *continuous log shipping* (a real-time CDC alternative) to meet the RPO without the I/O burden of discrete backup jobs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Students often forget to *subtract the backup execution time* from the RPO, leading to an erroneous 30-minute interval instead of 5 minutes. This oversight costs **1 mark** as it ignores a critical safety margin in real-world scheduling.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- вң… **Data Warehouse Maintenance** = Loading Automation + Backup/Recovery + Evolution + Scalability.
- вң… **3-2-1 Rule** вҖ” 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite.
- вң… **RPO** = maximum *data* loss; **RTO** = maximum *downtime*.
- вң… **99.9% SLA** вүҲ 8.77 hrs/year downtime; **99.99%** вүҲ 52.56 min/year.
- вң… **Amdahl's Law** caps speedup at $1/(1-p)$ regardless of node count.
- вң… **ETL** = Transform before load; **ELT** = Transform after load (cloud-native).
- вң… **CDC** = mechanism for real-time incremental extraction.
- вң… **Vertical Scaling** = single-node upgrade; **Horizontal** = multi-node distribution.
- вң… **Schema Evolution** must be versioned with rollback paths.
- вң… **MPP (shared-nothing)** = dominant scalable DW architecture.
- вң… **Full Backup** = simplest restore; **Incremental** = smallest size but chain-dependent.
- вң… **Recovery Models** for DWs: prefer *Full Recovery* with standby replicas.
- вң… **KTU 2024 Cognitive Levels** вҖ” expect 60% *Apply/Analyze*, 30% *Remember/Understand*, 10% *Evaluate/Create*.
- вң… **Always use Amdahl's full formula** вҖ” never multiply $n \times p$ as a shortcut.
- вң… **Tabular comparison** is the *gold standard* for KTU valuation keys.
- вң… **Evolution вү  Migration** вҖ” evolution is incremental and non-disruptive.
- вң… **Cloud orchestrators** (Airflow, Glue) are now KTU-favorite exam topics.
